column2: Public trust in AI comes from transparency, accountability, and showing real benefits. People lose confidence when they see hype, black box tools, or no clear safeguards. Canada should require companies to be clear about how AI is used, protect personal data, and set practical ethical standards that companies can actually follow. At the same time, we need to show Canadians the upside: AI reducing wait times in health care, making government more efficient, or helping small businesses grow.
column3: Canada should keep regulation to a minimum and let the free market drive AI growth. Over-regulation will only push companies and talent out of the country. The focus should be on a few high-risk areas where harm is real, like deepfake technology which can be especially dangerous to kids. In those cases, government should support investment in tools that can detect and flag deepfakes so the public knows what is real and what is not. Beyond that, the best framework is one that allows companies to
column4: Canada can build trust in AI by showing citizens and businesses real examples of impact. Government should lead by using AI internally to cut costs and improve services, and it should prioritize Canadian AI companies to do it. This shows taxpayers direct value and builds confidence in homegrown technology. Citizens need transparency about how AI is being used, but they also need to see results in areas like health care and public services. Government is best placed to set the baseline standards

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by focusing on three priorities:

1. Transparency & explainability – require AI systems to be understandable and decisions traceable.


2. Ethics & oversight – independent review boards, clear safety standards, and dual-use safeguards.


3. Accountability & redress – clear rules for errors, harms, and bias mitigation, with accessible reporting mechanisms.
column3:
Canada needs a cohesive AI governance framework including:

1. Standards & certification – technical benchmarks for safety, reliability, fairness, and robustness.


2. Regulations – clear rules on data privacy, dual-use technology, liability, and accountability.


3. Ethics & oversight – independent boards for high-risk AI and public reporting requirements.


4. Norms & best practices – sector-specific guidelines, transparency mandates, and bias-mitigation protocols.
column4:
Canada can build trust by proactive engagement across multiple fronts:

1. Public education & awareness – explain AI benefits, risks, and safeguards; led by government agencies in partnership with universities and civil-society groups.


2. Industry accountability – companies adopt transparency reports, ethics reviews, and explainable AI; led by private sector with regulatory oversight.

3. Citizen & stakeholder consultation – participatory forums, surveys, and advisory panels to shape AI polic

column2:
Canada needs to work with the provinces on public technical and civil literacy, including revamping school curricula and creating effective public messaging. 

Canada should invest into research into the social information ecosystem, and the structural factors that have caused breakdowns in the public's ability to assess facts and consensus reality. In other words, building public confidence requires in part solving the problem of misinformation on the Internet.
column3:
Canada must adopt an approach where specific regulation of "AI" is done only as a last resort, and where general laws are preferred. For instance, privacy concerns should not be addressed by AI-specific privacy laws. Rather, existing privacy laws should be fixed so the same standards apply to all systems.

Canada must act as a policy and thought leader jointly with the provinces to establish uniform national law, such as putting the legal burden on companies to prove their systems are safe.
column4: Canada must force social media platforms to fix structural incentives that promote dissemination of untruths. The government must also restore its reputation as a politically neutral source of truth, such as by strengthening controls against political messaging by governments. While being cautious about free speech, Canada should impose rules requiring political actors not to misrepresent facts. These rules should be "light touch" and favour quick, minor corrections to help voters decide.

Being able to do this will allow for controlled observability like in the web /HTTP/REST domains, and we will inherit decades of optimizations and technology that are proven to work.

column2: Canada can build AI trust by ensuring transparent governance, clear accountability for harms, and rigorous ethical standards. Public education, digital literacy programs, and independent oversight are essential, alongside visible safeguards in high-impact sectors like healthcare and finance to foster confidence.
column3: Canada needs binding AI governance frameworks aligned with global standards (OECD, ISO, NIST) that enforce algorithmic transparency, bias audits, and accountability mechanisms. Sector-specific regulations in healthcare, finance, and defence must ensure safety, integrity, and ethical compliance. Independent certification bodies, liability clarity, and mandatory risk assessments will strengthen trust. Embedding privacy, explainability, and fairness into deployment norms ensures responsible AI
column4: Canada should use public education campaigns, SME support programs, and citizen forums. Government sets rules, industry ensures transparency, and academia drives literacy—jointly fueling responsible AI trust.

column2: Public trust comes from protecting people first. Canada must require Consent, Compensation & Control in any AI touching human data. Unions and workers should be partners in oversight, ensuring real-world risks are flagged early. Pair strong safeguards with public education, literacy campaigns, and transparent disclosures so AI is seen as safe, fair, and accountable—not as exploitation.
column3: Canada needs enforceable standards: AI Safety Impact Assessments, model cards with dataset lineage, provenance and audit trails, plus NIL protections like Denmark, ELVIS-style voice rights, and California’s replica laws. Trust frameworks must mandate worker and union input, ensuring AI is deployed responsibly, with integrity, fairness, and enforceable remedies if harm occurs.
column4: To build trust, engage citizens and unions directly in governance and design. Create public registries for Digital Replicas, transparent risk reports, and community panels that review AI impacts. Government should set rules, industry must comply, and unions should safeguard worker voices. Trust grows when people see Consent, Compensation & Control are upheld, and that workers help steer AI’s future. Tech companies should not lead this. Ever.

column3: GenAI may feel mature, but it is still in its infancy, which gives Canada a window to lead with governance that balances innovation and trust. We should establish clear ethical frameworks aligned with international standards but grounded in Canadian values of equity and transparency. Priority areas include industrial safety, healthcare, and public-sector use, where trust is most at stake.

Government is best placed to set clear rules and provide independent oversight, while universities and research hubs can lead public education and dialogue. Industry and startups should demonstrate responsible use through transparent practices, ethical design, and clear communication with customers.

column2:
•	Deploy with guardrails now: Mandate Treasury Board AIA + PIAs for all gov AI; require model/data cards, eval results, and human-in-the-loop where impact ≥ “moderate.”
	•	Independent evaluation: Fund red-team hubs and sector test ranges (health, finance, safety-critical). Publish incident reports and post-mortems.
	•	User rights: Simple appeal/override for automated decisions; explainability at the granularity of the decision, not the model.
	•	Security & privacy: Secure enclaves, data minimiz
column3:
•	Management systems: Promote ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management) + NIST AI RMF as the baseline; tie to procurement.
	•	Assurance stack: Pre-deployment risk assessment (AIA), model cards, data cards, threat modeling, safety cases; third-party conformity assessment for high-risk use.
	•	Ops controls: Eval gates, change control, drift monitoring, incident reporting (mandatory for material harm), secure MLOps (SBOM, provenance, lineage).
	•	Sector overlays: OSFI for finance, Health Canada for SaMD/clini
column4:
•	Public-facing transparency: Plain-language model cards and purpose statements for gov AI; searchable registry with evals, vendors, contacts.
	•	Participatory design: Citizen juries for high-impact deployments; Indigenous-led boards where data implicates Indigenous peoples (OCAP®/CARE).
	•	Business enablement: “Trust by default” kits (templates for AIA/PIA, security reference arch, eval harnesses) + grants for third-party audits.
	•	Signals & stories: Publish quarterly trust dashboards and cas

column2: Canada can build trust in AI by setting clear, strong safety and fairness rules, making AI easy to understand, and including community education like adult literacy. Key steps: set high standards for privacy and bias, teach all Canadians about AI (especially through adult learning), explain in plain language how AI works, and involve the public in decisions.
column3: Canada needs strong national standards, rules, and oversight to make AI safe, fair, and transparent—especially in high-risk areas like health, jobs, and justice. The Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) would require testing for bias, clear information about how AI works, ethics guidelines, and independent review for risky AI systems. Independent agencies would oversee compliance and certify trustworthy products.
column4: Canada can build trust in AI governance by using plain-language education, public consultations, and transparent oversight. Government and adult literacy groups should lead outreach and explain AI simply. set high standards for privacy and bias, teach all Canadians about AI (especially through adult learning), explain in plain language how AI works, and involve the public in decisions.

column2: Launch a national AI literacy program through libraries, community colleges, and online platforms that demystifies AI capabilities and limitations, moving beyond fear-based narratives to practical understanding. Mandate algorithmic impact assessments for high-stakes AI deployments in healthcare, criminal justice, and financial services, with plain-language summaries publicly available before implementation. Create an independent AI incident reporting system similar to aviation safety boards, where AI failures and near-misses are transparently investigated and lessons shared industry-wide. Establish "AI Trust Marks" - voluntary certification programs that recognize companies meeting high standards for explainability, fairness testing, and human oversight. Focus public communication on tangible benefits Canadians already experience from AI - faster medical diagnoses, fraud prevention, and accessibility tools - while honestly acknowledging ongoing challenges.
column3: Implement mandatory AI auditing requirements for systems affecting fundamental rights - hiring, lending, healthcare, and justice - with annual compliance reviews by certified third parties. Develop Canadian AI Safety Standards aligned with but extending beyond EU AI Act requirements, creating competitive advantage for companies that can demonstrate superior safety practices. Establish liability frameworks that clearly assign responsibility between AI developers, deployers, and users, providing legal certainty while ensuring accountability for harms. Create specialized AI testing facilities where companies can stress-test systems for bias, robustness, and failure modes before market deployment, similar to crash testing for vehicles. Prioritize regulatory clarity in three critical sectors where trust failures would be catastrophic: autonomous vehicles, medical diagnosis AI, and financial credit scoring systems.
column4: Establish citizen AI councils in major cities where diverse community members review and provide input on municipal AI deployments, ensuring decisions reflect local values and concerns. Task public broadcasters like CBC/Radio-Canada with producing accessible AI education content and hosting town halls where citizens can directly question AI developers and policymakers. Create an AI Ombudsperson office with power to investigate complaints, mandate corrections, and publicly report on systemic issues in AI deployment across public and private sectors. Partner with trusted community organizations - healthcare providers, teachers' associations, labor unions - to deliver AI literacy training through existing relationships rather than top-down government programs. Require plain-language "AI disclosure cards" whenever citizens interact with automated decision systems, explaining what data is used, how decisions are made, and how to appeal outcomes.

column2:
Top risks to address (and why people mistrust):

Errors & bias in decisions affecting health, credit, employment, safety.

Privacy & data misuse (opaque data sources, secondary use).

Security & misuse (prompt injection, model theft, deepfakes).

Accountability gaps (who’s liable when AI harms?).

Low literacy + hype (inflated claims; unclear limits).

Most important confidence builders (do these first):

Risk-tiering + safety cases: Classify uses (minimal → high-risk). Require a documented safe
column3:
Governance & oversight (how it should work):

Risk-based regulation for high-risk uses (health, employment, credit, critical infrastructure, law enforcement). Require:

Pre-deployment conformity (fit-for-purpose testing, bias & robustness checks, privacy DPIA/TRA).

Post-market monitoring (drift detection, performance SLAs, incident reporting).

Registration of high-risk systems in a public catalog.

Assurance & auditing: Recognize third-party auditors; adopt reasonable-assurance audits on data
column4:
Explain like it matters: Standard AI Facts Labels on gov services; publish impact assessments in plain language with contact/appeal info.

Town-hall testbeds: Run community pilots (libraries, hospitals, utilities) with before/after metrics and open Q&A; commit to change or cancel if targets aren’t met.

Rapid response for harms: 311-style reporting channel for AI issues; visible service-level targets for remediation.

First, divest from all American AIs and prevent their diffusion onto Canadian soil - including stopping services such as ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot. They're all built on questionable source content.

Ensure that AIs built in Canada are transparent about their source content and algorithms; legislate against AIs built on the "open" internet (rife with CSAM, porn, illegal content). Be open about true costs, including electricity and water. Educate against AI errors and hallucinations.
column3: Require AI companies to compensate for damages due to errors, and legislate recall, repair, corrections - as we do with car manufacturers. Require AI companies to list their source content and have open algorithms. Ensure compliance with disability laws - AI should not get a "free pass" simply because it's new. Listen to unions and FNIM. Legislate data centres. Set a near deadline to get all Canadian government content into Canadian-controlled data centres. Legislate right to "turn off" AI.

column2:
Canada can build public confidence in AI by pairing clear public-sector leadership with transparency and visible public benefit. The federal government should lead by example: advance a proportionate, risk-based law for high-impact AI (alongside the Treasury Board’s Directive on Automated Decision-Making), enforce transparency, fairness and accountability in federal uses, and resource independent oversight. In parallel, deliver the $2B Sovereign AI Compute Strategy so sensitive workloads can run on Canadian-controlled infrastructure. Domestic data centres, supercomputers and secure cloud capacity signal that rights and resilience—not vendor dependence—anchor Canadian AI.

Trust must also be designed into products and services. Organizations should plainly disclose when AI is used, what risks exist, and why a system reached a result; high-impact systems must be explainable. People need practical remedies—clear notices, easy escalation to human review, and avenues to contest decisions—so harms and bias can be addressed quickly. Privacy-by-design, safety testing and post-deployment monitoring should be routine, with results summarized in accessible, bilingual reports.

Finally, confidence grows when Canadians see AI improving daily life under strict safeguards. Government should prioritize deployments with measurable benefits in health care, education, infrastructure and public administration, and report outcomes such as shorter wait times, faster benefits, and fewer service backlogs. Canada should expand AI literacy across schools, workers and public servants, and keep citizens at the table through ongoing consultations, civic panels and Indigenous co-governance where data or rights are implicated. An informed public, transparent systems, sovereign infrastructure and accountable institutions are the fastest path to durable trust.
column3:
Canada needs a clear, proportionate framework that combines public-sector leadership, technical assurance, and alignment with trusted global norms—tailored to Canadian values. The federal government should lead by example: advance the proposed Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) to set risk-based rules; apply and update the Treasury Board’s Directive on Automated Decision-Making to ensure transparency, accountability, and fairness in federal uses; and deliver the $2B Sovereign AI Compute Strategy so sensitive workloads can run on Canadian-controlled infrastructure (domestic data centres, supercomputers, and grants).

Trust must be built into products. Developers and deployers should publish model cards and comparable documentation that state purpose, training data, performance, and limits in plain language. Systems should ship with a software bill of materials (SBOM) to expose dependencies and strengthen supply-chain security. Robustness testing and independent red-teaming should probe safety, bias, and security failure modes before and after deployment. High-impact uses should undergo algorithmic impact assessments and independent audits so decision logic, risks, and accountability are documented throughout the lifecycle.

Canada should align with international best practice while reflecting our legal and social context. The OECD AI Principles, NIST’s AI Risk Management Framework, and ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems) offer strong baselines, and the G7 Hiroshima Process emphasizes “safe, secure, trustworthy” AI. Canadian implementation should be bilingual, rights-preserving under the Charter, and consistent with reconciliation. Indigenous data-sovereignty principles—especially OCAP (Ownership, Control, Access, Possession)—should guide governance wherever First Nations data or rights are implicated, ensuring communities retain control over data that affect them.
column4:
Engagement must be continuous, practical, and led by the public sector. The federal government should act as convenor and standard-setter: publish plain-language rules and risk tiers, disclose evaluations and incidents, and maintain a bilingual dashboard of adoption, outcomes, and harms. A central AI authority should coordinate with provinces and territories so procurement, privacy, safety testing, and cyber guidance read as one playbook, not many.

Provinces, municipalities, and Crowns should turn engagement into practice via open, regulated testbeds in priority services—health, transport, energy, and benefits. Each pilot should commit to at-point-of-use transparency (purpose, notices, accessible explanations) and offer human review and redress. Indigenous governments must co-lead where their data or rights are implicated, grounded in OCAP and related principles.

Businesses are partners in trust. They should publish concise model/data cards and impact summaries, participate in independent audits and red-teaming, and use Canadian data trusts and secure environments for testing. Universities, standards bodies, and professional colleges can translate global standards into Canadian practice codes, continuing education, and conformance tools SMEs can adopt.

Engagement is also education. Expand AI literacy across K-12, workers, public servants, and SMEs with practical modules on safe adoption, evaluation, and change management. Use citizens’ panels and youth councils to review high-risk public deployments before launch, and publish the results. Throughout, balance transparency with innovation by releasing safety and performance evidence while protecting legitimate IP and security details. Trust follows when people see AI improve essential services, and when safeguards and remedies are real.

Lead: Government—policy education; industry—use-case demos; NGOs—bias audits; academia—literacy curricula. Fuels trust via co-creation, positioning ethical AI as shared win.

column2: Everyone knows AI is flawed. So are humans. Ai is the process of computers becoming more human. So we need to encourage the fact that we are are in charge of our own risk. "No we cant use ai, its not safe for Level 2 security projects". Instead ask "How can we safely leverage AI for Level 2 security projects?"

column4: Encourage companies to start small. Sorting emails, building lists, etc. I do this all the time during presentations and its always amazing to see the ripple effect of smiles when they see how it will free up time for Beer and Family ;)

column2:
Engage academic scholars, creatives, and social scientists to TELL THE STORY of what will be possible in a world where Canada is an AI leader. It's dramatically obvious that other than the next 5-10 years, there is NO glass-half-full narrative in the public consciousness about what this technological revolution will enable.... only what it will REMOVE from the current paradigm (e.g., XXX jobs, education disparities, etc.)

What will be able to DO DIFFERENTLY from today?
column3:
OPEN WEIGHTS FOR ALL FOUNDATION MODELS BUILT WITH TAXPAYER DOLLARS.
Dramatic regulations AND associated investment in green energy technologies to power the AI revolution in Canada.
column4: Artists, designers, creatives, humanities scholars - the people who are experts in studying human reactions to new experiences, technologies, and social paradigm shifts. Without these stakeholders in the lead, this is just another technocratic wank-fest waiting to get worse.

column2: Trust will not come from slogans. It requires acknowledging the real risks: job displacement, loss of purpose for knowledge workers, and shocks to entire professions. Government must be upfront about these trade-offs while showing where AI can improve lives, such as reducing wait times or cutting admin burdens. Transparency, worker retraining, and strong safety nets are as important as technical guardrails if public confidence is to be earned.
column3: Frameworks must go beyond technical safety to address social and economic impacts. Standards should require impact assessments that include human consequences, not just data risks. Regulations must ensure companies deploying AI are accountable for workforce disruption, and provide mandatory transition supports in high-risk sectors. Without tackling these human realities, no framework will feel credible to the public.
column4: Government should lead a national dialogue that treats citizens as partners, not passive recipients, by openly discussing the risks of mass disruption to knowledge work and the safeguards being built. Businesses need to be transparent about their deployment choices and their plans for workers. Civil society and labour groups should be at the table to ensure trust is not only about technical compliance but about protecting dignity and meaningful work.

column2: Agencies can offer educational resources, tutorials, and community consultations so newcomers understand AI-driven services for settlement, employment, and language support before they arrive. Governments and industry should enforce ethical standards, bias mitigation, and privacy protections in systems used for housing, benefits, and job matching. Success can be measured by higher engagement with AI-enabled settlement tools, smoother newcomer integration, and equitable access to services, ensuri

Canada must enforce transparent AI standards, require audits, and invest in public education. Open communication, accountability, and clear privacy protections are key to building and keeping public trust in AI.
column3: Canada needs clear national AI governance frameworks that ensure ethical compliance, privacy protection, and ongoing oversight. Mandatory standards for algorithmic transparency, explainability, and fairness should be enforced through independent audits and certifications. Regulations must require companies to assess and mitigate risks in areas like health care, finance, justice, and public services, where trust issues are most acute.
column4: Canada should launch public campaigns, open consultations, and accessible resources to explain AI systems and their impacts. Education ministries and public broadcasters can lead literacy and awareness efforts. Industry associations and standards bodies should set and communicate best practices, while government regulators oversee compliance, set baseline rules, and facilitate public dialogue.

column2:
Large scale, ongoing initiatives related to AI literacy and critical thought. 

Develop an interconnected system where no one actor holds too much power - not government, not the private sector, not citizens. How can they be checks against each other?
Encourage competition.
column3: Transparency of process, as much as possible - minimize the 'black box' of how AI is working. Encourage the development of technology that identifies AI created content.
column4: Encourage AI literacy and technological transparency - how is it working. Decentralized ownership and tracking of personal data within public sector systems might help.

column2: You can't build trust by excluding the public from strategy development.Drivers of mistrust: opaque processes, industry self-regulation, harm without accountability, being told to "fill out a survey" when offering expertise.This consultation erodes trust. Expert-level questions marketed as public input, zero AI ethicists on your task force.Want confidence? Include ethicists, civil society, workers in actual decision-making. Not surveys they can't answer.
column3:
Mandatory impact assessments. Independent audits. Worker consultation. Civil society oversight. Clear liability. Transparency requirements. Bias testing. Public registries.
You can't develop trustworthy frameworks by only asking industry.
Priority areas: healthcare, criminal justice, employment, social services. Ask affected people what protections they need.
Ethical compliance requires ethics expertise. Your task force has zero independent AI ethicists.
column4:
Start by engaging citizens with questions they can answer.
Who leads trust-building? Independent ethicists, civil society, worker representatives, digital rights advocates. Not just industry.
Strategies: accessible impact assessments, plain-language explanations, meaningful consent, redress mechanisms.
Transparency isn't the enemy of innovation. Opacity is the enemy of trust.
This survey performs engagement while excluding meaningful participation. Want trust? Build trustworthy processes.

We need better safeguard for children, data, and privacy. This will need to start with Canadian data staying in Canada.
column3: We can't constrain development with too much government oversight, but we also need systems in place to protect kids and someone to stop and ask, just because we can, does it mean we should.
column4: It has to start in the schools, then local organizations to help train companies.

column2: Provably trustworthy systems.
column3: Auditing. Transparency and adherence. Need reliable verification mechanisms. Otherwise people will just violate agreements.

-laws to limit what AIs can be built to do (i.e., not build weapons or advocate violence or damage systems humans depend on)

column3:
It is imperative that members of the creative sector are protected from AI sampling.

For example - as a voice-over professional, a regulation protecting the use of my voice from being data mined and applied to AI generated voice programs is essential. The same for writers and on-camera talents. This is a real concern for our community.
column4: An auditing program that checks AI with AI. This ensures - much like academia - that anything that has copyright or is previously published, recorded, etc is not being copied, or used without consent. Penalties should be stiff and severe to ensure guidelines and frameworks are followed.

column2: Prioritize human labor, creativity, and thought. Establish robust and meaningful guardrails to manage job loss. Declare that you, the Canadian government, understand that a society without human creativity and thought at its center is an ailing society. Progress CANNOT trump the needs of human beings.
column3: Ensure that AI in the classroom (until AT LEAST grade 10) is designed with students in mind. Students (and children more broadly) should not be able to engage with public AI tools until they are at least 16. Prioritizing human creativity and critical thought in the classroom is absolutely priority number one. Learning should feel like friction -- any tool that eliminates that friction should be disregarded.
column4: Canada should not fund any creative projects where AI is being used to replace labor (filmmaking, visual art, audio drama, etc...). Grants should be for human centered projects.

column2:
Be honest and transparent about the promise and perils of AI, educate on practical tips to make it safer. 
Explain what the government is doing to keep Canadian citizens and businesses safe from AI harm. Report issues immediately to Canadians, so that people are aware of potential harms.
Build confidence by helping Canadians take their first steps with AI... by explaining safe usage in a stepped manner, from how to access gen AI tools, to how to prompt and collaborate with them, to exploration of specific feature AI tools (like meeting recordings and summarization, presentation slide deck AI tools, image or video generators), more advanced AI tools such as avatar creators, AI agents, etc. Start slow, build incrementally and use practical examples that will impress new AI users with the "magic" of AI and how it can make their lives easier.
column3:
AI watermarking 
- require a visual stamp to show AI was used to create works, will minimize deepfakes and grow citizen trust in what they read and watch
- embedded watermarking, like metadata, of AI generated works will improve traceability and transparency

Obligatory safety actions for all AI solutions
- create a mandatory list of things that must be done, and recommended list of things that should be done,  to improve safety of the AI solutions
- establish a certification requirement before real-world use of the AI solutions
- create a visual that shows users that it meets a standard and has been certified. much like the padlock visual for secure internet transactions

Ethics are tricky - determine what it means for Canadians to use AI ethically...(all countries will not agree)
- do we allow widespread use of facial recognition technology, what are the rules around where the facial data is stored and how it is used?
- what about heat sensing AI applications or "thought-reading" capabilities, who can use this, for what purpose, and where does the data go?
- when is consent required

column2: To build public trust in AI, Canada must show that AI is truthful, transparent, and accountable. One of the biggest risks is the spread of misinformation and disinformation online.AI can amplify false content at scale. To counter this, Canada should set clear rules on authenticity and consent (so voices, images, and text aren’t misused), require disclosure when content is AI-generated, and support public literacy programs so Canadians know how to spot and question AI outputs.
column3: This feels like the same question as the question above it. Canada needs clear rules for consent, transparency, and disclosure in AI. Strong oversight will ensure products are ethical and trusted.
column4:
Canada should engage citizens and businesses by making AI use transparent and responsible, with clear consent and disclosure rules.
Government must set and enforce the standards, while industry follows them and builds trustworthy tools.
Creators and small businesses, like my own micro-business, Avenyou Voice Services, must be included as voices at the table, since we see first-hand how AI affects our work.

column2:
Work with unions like ACTRA, listen to people who directly work in affected industries, and protect our rights. 
AI should not be allowed to steal work, and then regurgitate it and profit. We need our personhood (likeness, voice, movements, etc.) to be safeguarded. 
Consent, Compensation, and Control should be required in all usage of AI.
column3:
Strict rules about what AI models are allowed to be trained on, and complete transparency about what was used. 
Protections for performers and their intellectual property and personhood rights. 
Severe penalties for AI companies that violent any of these protections. 
No one will ever trust or support AI if it is allowed to steal from hard working Canadians.
column4:
Workers and unions must be part of how AI is built and governed. Canada can create public tools, 
like clear AI-risk reports, and community panels to review impacts. The government sets rules, 
industry follows those rules, and unions help protect the performer voices.  
Trust grows when performers have Consent, Compensation, and Control, and when workers help 
guide AI’s future.

column2: Public trust is rooted in putting the public first, by showing that we're willing to stand against AI generated misinformation, and theft of copyrighted materials for "training data" and focusing on AI methodologies that are actually likely to bear fruit in terms of reliability. I've heard the words "Consent, Compensation, and Control" thrown around in regard to the data taken for training LLMs and image generating AI, right now, we have none of those.
column3: As mentioned earlier, copyright needs to be protected. A person's likeness and voice need to be protected, their data needs to be protected. We also need to insure that BOTH those using AI, and those who created the model used bear some responsibility for it's output. Without regulations in place around those elements and strong consequences when those protections are skirted or ignored there can be no trust.
column4: There should be clear information available regarding what is and is not permissible when building or using AI, and the consequences severe enough to discourage even the most wealthy of AI companies from ignoring those rules, after all "If the penalty for a crime is a fine, then that law only exists for the lower class." Consent, Compensation, and Control again should be at the heart of any laws regarding AI.

column2: Being honest and transparent rather than only provide a glitzy angle on any story regarding AI
column3: There should be strong oversight in whether companies are remaining ethical, major guardrails to protect the environment, limitations on large companies for using AI to force out creators.

column2: One would be to make AI research and AI applications subject to the oversight of engineering regulators, just as other technologies are. The risks of AI to the public are actually significant and cannot be understated; the government should be taking them seriously as a national security threat, so that should also be visible. But ultimately, public trust will never be won if AI appears as an "arms race" in which countries and firms are desperate to get ahead, even at the cost of bending rules.
column3: As mentioned: make AI research and AI applications subject to the oversight of engineering regulators, just as other technologies are. But strengthen this to apply to AI products developed outside of Canada as well. Currently, engineering ethics regulations and professional standards only apply to development work done within Canada, not to products that are developed abroad. Allowing this loophole only incentivizes developers to move their offices outside of Canadian borders.
column4:
Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, and Richard Sutton are well respected and trusted figures who are also domain experts. If they won't do the job themselves, then ask them to recommend someone.

Also: trust in governance is rapidly undermined by any scandal. See to it that Canadian AI companies aren't playing fast and loose with the rules.

column2: Canada can build trust in AI by making governance and transparency central. Clear rules on safety, liability, and bias, combined with national testbeds to certify AI systems, reduce risks. Public education and digital literacy programs help citizens understand how AI works and where safeguards exist. Governments should model responsible use in healthcare, energy, and public services, while independent audits and ethical standards reassure businesses and citizens that AI adoption is safe and fair
column3: Canada needs clear frameworks to ensure AI is trustworthy and responsibly deployed. Priority areas include safety, liability, bias, and data protection. National standards for testing and certification, aligned with global norms, can assure product integrity. Regulatory sandboxes should enable safe experimentation, while mandatory transparency, auditability, and ethical guidelines ensure accountability. Together, these mechanisms build trust and enable responsible AI adoption across sectors.
column4: Canada can build trust in AI by engaging citizens with clear, accessible education on how AI works and its safeguards, while involving businesses in co-developing standards. Governments should lead on regulation, transparency, and public outreach, while industry drives responsible deployment through best practices and audits. Academia and civil society can provide independent oversight and literacy programs. A collaborative approach—balancing openness with innovation—will fuel confidence in AI g

required in all AI. Unions and creative workers should be partners in planning how A.I. is applied to
our industry. We need strong rules, clear information, and public education to make sure AI is safe,
fair, and accountable, not harmful or exploitative. Trust grows when workers help shape the
standards.
column3:

include consequences for misuse, safety checks, clear records of AI training, protections for
performers’ voices and likeness, and ways for performers and unions to contribute responsibly to
harm-reduction practices. Trust in AI depends on having fair frameworks that balance innovation
with enforceable rights and accountability.

Once these responsible and legally enforced rules are set and made clear and repeated over and over in the education process, the building blocks will be laid for the successful use of a proactive AI generation in Canada.

column2: Protect average Canadian workers. Junior accountants, computer programmers, musicians and actors having their work and likeness used without permission or compensation. These are all massive problems that the tech bros and billionaires don't care about but as a government who represents the people you should be fighting hard for.

column2: Public trust in AI grows through visible capability and accountability. In the United States, people see AI applied in defence, health, and disaster response through institutions like DARPA and NASA. Trust is built through participation and proof. Canada risks falling behind if AI remains confined to pilots and consultations instead of being deployed in real, accountable systems where results can be seen and improved.
column3: Canada should build an adaptive AI assurance system rather than static regulation. The goal is to embed safety, auditability, and traceability inside product development, not just assess them after deployment. The EU’s AI Act provides a structure for classification and risk tiers, but Canada’s opportunity is to move faster by combining those elements into a living governance model that evolves with technology and demonstrates accountability through evidence, not bureaucracy.
column4: From ALL IN what came through clearly is that many businesses, employees, and public servants are still in the passive awareness phase. They hear about AI but don’t experience it in their daily work. To build trust, Canada should focus on participation, not persuasion. Public servants, universities, and small firms are best placed to lead efforts that make AI governance visible through practice ,showing how decisions are made, risks managed, and value created.

column2: Public education initiatives on technologies like differential privacy, on data legislation. That legislation should likewise be made explicit and Canadian-focused. Are we comfortable with American companies explicitly proclaiming that they would divulge non-American data to their government, even if the data is housed outside of America?
column3: ISO 42001, updates to PIPEDA etc. Note crucially that neither differential privacy nor federated learning are 'silver bullets' and both are susceptible to workarounds, weaknesses, or unintended consequences.
column4: Each province should collaborate with the federal government to craft their own Responsible AI groups, potentially under or with existing AI Institutes (or new ones proposed earlier). Again, we do not want to put 'all our eggs in one basket', especially if that basket doesn't really understand how AI works (so to speak). Such groups should be composed of diverse sectors -- academics are still pre-eminent in terms of trust and expertise, but they need to be balanced with public sector

column3:
The intellectual property, the creative output of both private individuals and Canadian entities must be protected to the same extent as physical property. The data mining of these ressources by unregulated AI companies both within and outside Canada cannot be allowed to continue in the way it has to date.
Voice performers and screen actors like myself need to know that their government has their interests at heart, and that the Law will give them the tools to defend themselves from this theft.
column4:
It is crucial that professional bodies be not just consulted, but actively involved in the writing of the necessary laws, since they are in a unique position to explain the ramifications and stakes of this situation. 
Law makers cannot be expected to have this understanding, but it is vital that the perceived interests of the economy not override the livelihoods and existence of the millions of individuals in the creative industries whose future is in very real jeopardy at present.

column3: Canada needs clear AI governance frameworks centered on user privacy and accountability. Require privacy-by-design, transparent data use, and independent audits for all AI systems. column2:
Trust is earned with proof.

Make AI auditable: model/use-case cards, impact assessments, data provenance, incident reporting; independent red-teaming pre-deploy.

Stand up a public Safety & Evaluation Lab with sector benchmarks and plain-language “nutrition labels.”

Ensure recourse: opt-outs, human appeal, audit logs for consequential decisions.

Protect privacy and sovereignty: secure enclaves, Indigenous data governance, media provenance.

Tie public funding to transparency: publish artifact
column3:
Trustworthy AI needs a tight stack:

Governance: NIST AI RMF + ISO/IEC 23894 for risk; ISO/IEC 42001 for AI management; mandatory Algorithmic Impact Assessments, risk tiers, continuous monitoring, incident reporting.

Security/privacy: ISO 27001/27701, MITRE ATLAS, OWASP Top-10 for LLM apps, secure enclaves, data minimization, Indigenous data sovereignty.

Integrity: Model/Data/System Cards, dataset lineage, C2PA provenance, bias/robustness/misuse evals, independent red-teaming, reproducibility,

column2: Canada should pair practical safeguards with radical transparency where risk is real and laissez faire where it is not. column3: I want a risk tiered regime that is light for low risk tools and firm for high risk decisions. Use performance standards, not prescriptive tech rules. Require model cards, data sheets, change logs, and a public incident portal for significant systems. Create a voluntary pre cert path that grants complete safe harbor if models pass 3rd party evals. Canada should also align with NIST style risk management and ISO, with Canada specifics on privacy, and Official Languages.
column4: Engage citizens and businesses by making systems legible, participatory, and useful. column2: Build trust by making AI powerful, private, and provable. column3: Adopt a Canadian AI Trust Framework: sovereign-by-default (data/logs/training/inference kept in Canada; no training on user inputs; deletion/export controls); plain-language AI facts labels + model/data cards; independent audits and monitoring, with mandatory incident reporting under a national certification mark; a red-team/evaluation network; open standards and audit logs; stricter, human-in-the-loop rules for health, finance, justice, critical infra, kids, and elections.
column4: Government must set sovereign-by-default rules, certify tools, and run transparent sandboxes; Privacy Commissioners enforce audits and incident disclosure. Public procurement should preference certified, Canadian-owned platforms so citizens and businesses can use AI safely.

column2: Canada can build public trust in AI by implementing transparent, accountable, and inclusive governance frameworks. First, establish clear, enforceable regulations focused on ethical AI use, privacy protection, and bias mitigation. Second, create independent oversight bodies that audit AI systems regularly and publish accessible reports. Third, engage diverse communities early through public consultations to ensure AI reflects societal values and addresses real concerns.
column3: Canada needs a risk-based AI regulatory framework focused on high-impact sectors, strong data governance ensuring privacy and fairness, and mandatory third-party certification and audits. Clear liability rules must define accountability for AI failures. Ethical guidelines emphasizing transparency, fairness, and inclusivity should guide development. Together, these build trust and enable responsible AI innovation, balancing protection with growth.
column4: Canada should launch a coordinated public engagement strategy combining education, transparency, and dialogue. Government must lead by creating accessible AI literacy programs and clear, transparent policies. Industry should drive best practices and open communication about AI use. Academia can research societal impacts and foster public forums. Civil society must represent diverse voices, ensuring inclusive input.

column2: Canada can build public trust in AI by addressing algorithmic bias, promoting transparency, inclusivity, and emotional responsibility, and embedding ethical design. Key strategies include trust literacy, narrative reframing, institutional transparency, and relationship infrastructure. AI systems must align with human values, be accountable, and foster psychological safety. Trust is a civic practice rooted in shared meaning and moral clarity.
column3:
Nordstrom’s Principles (Osside Institute, 2025) for Trustworthy AI in Canada
1. Stop: Prevent sensitive or proprietary data from being entered into AI systems.
2. Declare: Transparently disclose when and how AI was used in content creation.
3. Review: Require human oversight HLR/CHR (Human Last Review/Competent Human Review) to validate AI outputs for accuracy, relevance, and ethics.
column4: Canada should apply subsidiarity to build trust in AI governance. Local actors lead public education and dialogue, while federal bodies set standards and fund infrastructure. Use the Stop–Declare–Review framework to prevent misuse, ensure transparency, and validate outputs. Promote AI literacy, ethical awareness, and critical thinking through schools, libraries, and civic forums. Trust grows through shared responsibility and contextual leadership.

column2: To build public trust and manage the risks of AI, Canada must move beyond simply regulating the technology towards a holistic, proactive, and transparent national strategy. Trust is not a given; it must be earned through demonstrably safe, fair, and transparent practices, particularly given that some Canadians are wary of AI's societal impact. Building this confidence requires action across multiple fronts, from clear regulation to public education.
column3: CIHI/ OCAP/ ACCESSIBILITY ACT/ UNDRIP

column2: Canada can build trust by making sure AI is used openly and fairly, especially when it affects people’s voices and images. We need strong rules against things like deepfakes and copying creative work without permission. Clear info and education about AI will help people understand the risks and benefits. It’s about protecting privacy and rights, not over-controlling the tech.
column3: We need strong laws with real consequences, like prison sentences, for people who steal others’ identities, voices, or create deepfakes without permission. This kind of misuse is serious and harms real people. Having tough penalties will help stop bad actors and protect creators like voice actors from being exploited.
column4: Canada should make it easy for everyday people and businesses to learn what AI does and how it affects them. Trusted voices from different groups, including creative workers like voice actors, should be part of the conversation. The private sector can lead innovation, while the government focuses on clear, simple rules and education to keep things fair and honest. Transparency matters, but it shouldn’t slow down good tech.

column2: Trust grows from clarity, not hype. Canada needs visible safety testing, public reporting, and plain-language labels on AI systems. An independent Canadian AI evaluation lab can certify models, while national literacy programs help citizens and SMEs use AI confidently.
column3: Adopt risk-based AI rules, light for research, strict for high-risk uses. Require model cards, provenance tracking, and conformity marks like “Canadian-Tested, Globally-Trusted.” Clear liability rules and in-house safety testing will make AI both responsible and competitive.
column4: Government sets rules and funds testing; industry proves safety; communities co-design where impacts are local. Publish easy-to-read dashboards and hold open demos. Transparency builds trust when Canadians can see how AI works and who’s accountable.

column2: Canada can lead the world in Explainable AI, including Rules as Code and Hybrid (Neuro-Symbolic) AI. True explainability (as opposed to a token prediction-based "good guess") is important in many high-stakes domains, including law, where understanding the exact reasoning process behind a decision is often critical. Generative AI, including RAG and Chain-of-Thought, is a fundamentally limited paradigm that will not achieve AI explainability on its own.

The question itself highlights the challenge, and where Canada must improve. This is not just about public trust in AI, the notion of trust is mainly about internal, organizational trust by stakeholders. Organizations create AI systems that they do not deploy because of the fragmented trust topics. We must facilitate internal organizational trust. Public trust is just one of the dimension that must be addressed. The key is to switch from narrative based trust to evidence-based trust.

For example, Treasury Board (TBS) should focus on framework orchestration, overlap and alignment with AI requirements.

column2: To the greatest degree possible, we must be as transparent as possible in all that we do, especially as it relates to government policies, investments of taxpayer dollars, and consistent enforcement of regulatory guidelines to ensure compliance with standards.  Obviously there will be exceptions where disclosures would compromise national security or negatively impact our citizens rights to privacy.  Providing as much context as possible for decisions and reporting on outcomes, including failures, will build an informed public.  Access to education and targeted training programs that are affordable and go beyond the generally available materials provided by the owners of the foundational models is also key to ensuring a world-class workforce.
column3:
1. RISK-BASED FRAMEWORK (Like EU AI Act)
- Unacceptable: Social scoring, manipulation (banned)
- High-risk: Healthcare, hiring, finance, law enforcement (strict oversight)
- Limited: Chatbots, deepfakes (transparency required)
- Minimal: Games, spam filters (self-regulation)

2. MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS FOR HIGH-RISK AI
- Pre-deployment impact assessments
- Human oversight mechanisms
- Adversarial testing for bias/safety
- Audit trails (explainable decisions)
- Third-party certification before deployment

3. SECTORAL STANDARDS
- Healthcare: FDA-style approval for diagnostic AI
- Finance: Stress testing, model validation (like Basel III)
- Employment: Bias audits, appeal processes
- Government: Transparency reports, public accountability

4. DATA GOVERNANCE
- Clear consent for AI training data
- Right to opt-out of automated decisions
- Data minimization requirements
- Synthetic data standards (when acceptable vs real data)

5. LIABILITY FRAMEWORK
- Deployer liable if negligent use
- Developer liable if design flaws
- Insurance mandatory for high-risk applications
- No-fault compensation for certain harms (like vaccine injury)

6. TRANSPARENCY REQUIREMENTS
- Disclose when AI makes decisions
- Explain AI capabilities/limitations
- Public registry of high-risk AI systems
- Algorithm impact assessments (like privacy assessments)

7. ENFORCEMENT
- Dedicated AI regulator (not fragmented)
- Whistleblower protections
- Meaningful penalties (% of revenue, not fixed fines)
- Fast enforcement (injunctions, not just post-hoc fines)

8. INNOVATION ENABLERS
- Regulatory sandboxes with legal safe harbor
- Pre-certification programs (faster approval)
- Safe harbors for good-faith compliance
- Sunset clauses (re-evaluate rules every 3 years)

Critical Balance: Strict enough to prevent harms, flexible enough to enable innovation. Most jurisdictions fail by over-regulating (EU) or under-regulating (China). Canada should be Goldilocks zone.

column2: From MISA’s perspective, building public trust in AI starts with openness, fairness, and clear communication. Municipalities can show citizens the real benefits of AI—like faster services or safer infrastructure—while also being transparent about how decisions are made and data is used. MISA can help by promoting national standards for ethical AI, supporting privacy-by-design practices, and using tools like the federal AI Translator to show immediate impact.
column3: Key elements include aligning municipal and if possible, provincial practices with the federal Directive on Automated Decision-Making and forthcoming Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA), supported by clear privacy, security, and accessibility standards. Canada also needs shared norms for AI risk classification, transparency, and human oversight, along with standardized model testing and audit processes.
column4: Canada can build public trust and promote responsible AI use by engaging citizens and businesses through transparency, education, and collaboration. Municipalities are uniquely positioned to lead community-level engagement—showing residents how AI improves daily services while listening to their concerns. Industry and academia can contribute by developing explainable, ethical AI systems and sharing best practices openly.

This strategy must have a LONG term view and plan.

column2:
Confidence grows when people see AI being used responsibly, safely, and for public good. Additionally...
- Be transparent and explainable: Require clear disclosure when AI is used and communicate how decisions are made, especially in public services.
- Demonstrate real benefits that all citizens can relate with: Fund proof-of-value projects that show AI improving healthcare, safety, and productivity, not just efficiency.
- Raise AI literacy – Invest in public and workforce education so citizens understand both opportunities and limits of AI.
- Promote Canadian leadership – Highlight domestic AI successes as people want to be part of a winning team.
column3:
Canada needs a simple AI governance framework aligned with Europe and the US if possible:
- A risk-based approach that classifies AI systems by impact and imposes proportional obligations.
- Adoption of international AI standards (e.g., SOC2) for ethical design, risk management, transparency, and privacy.
- Voluntary codes and sector-specific guidance, especially in high-impact areas like healthcare, justice, and public services.
column4:
Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses to promote responsible AI use and trust through:

- Public education and transparent communication: Explain AI systems' workings, benefits, and risks in accessible language via campaigns, workshops, and online platforms, helping demystify AI.
- Inclusive consultation: Involve businesses, academics, and civil society in AI governance design, policy-making, and ongoing feedback channels to ensure AI serves public interests and reflects shared values.
- Balancing transparency with innovation: Provide clear explanations and accountability without exposing proprietary information that could stifle innovation, supported by independent audits and ethical standards.
- Lead roles: The federal government should spearhead policy, and public engagement; academia and research institutions can drive education and technical transparency; industry should commit to ethical practices and user education; and civil society can advocate for rights and inclusivity.

This collaborative, multi-stakeholder approach builds trust by fostering understanding, participation, and responsible deployment of AI.

column2: The risks that come with AI are tremendous, but, as the saying goes, “the genie isn’t going back into the bottle.” So, it’s how we deal with those risks. Fortunately, there are already existing laws and regulations to deal with the behaviours (in this case, negative) that AI provides additional abilities. Being transparent and authentic in relation to the risks and uses of AI is fundamental. Additionally, education systems need to immediately incorporate AI, beyond being a mere tool.
column3: There are some existing Frameworks and Governance documents that can be used, but across Canada, there is a need for these. These are being developed in silos, and not necessarily because of uniqueness, but because of lack of understanding of how to deal with AI. Meanwhile, existing documents, such as those created by the IAPP, can be applied. Just as there is PMI for the PMP designation, AI does not necessarily (though possibly) require the direction to lie solely in government.
column4: Canada stands to be a world leader here by ensuring that AI is accessible to all. We need to examine the lessons learned – and those we are still learning – from our shift from paper to digital. Let’s not create more organizations that help people navigate the AI realm, just as we did to understand digital forms. Let’s ensure AI is created equally for all, while ensuring access and dignity. Businesses will lead. Government will govern.

column2: Building public trust (what to do now): Trust comes from proof, not PR. Start with plain-language “AI labels” on products (what it does, data used, limits, known risks), independent red-teaming before launch, and ongoing incident reporting like aviation. Require privacy-by-design, bias testing on Canadian demographics (including Indigenous and northern communities), and human fallback for high-stakes decisions. Pair this with visible accountability: public impact assessments for government use, recalls for unsafe systems, and clear opt-outs. Boost literacy where it matters—schools, healthcare, SMEs—with short, scenario-based guides and simulations so people can see how the tech behaves, good and bad.
column3: Frameworks, standards, and rules (how to govern): Use a risk-tiered regime: light touch for low-risk tools, strict duties for high-impact systems (health, credit, employment, safety). Anchor compliance in recognized standards—NIST AI Risk Framework–style governance, ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems), secure MLOps baselines, dataset provenance/watermarking, and audit-ready logs. Mandate pre-market evaluations, third-party conformity assessments for high-risk use, public model/eval cards, and post-market monitoring with incident disclosure. Require explainability appropriate to context (clinician-grade in hospitals, user-grade in consumer apps), robust privacy (de-identification, differential privacy/federated learning where feasible), and supply-chain security reviews for frontier and imported models.
column4: Engaging citizens and businesses (who leads what): Put people in the loop early through standing citizen panels, sector townhalls, and participatory audits that publish findings and fixes. Let government set the guardrails (Treasury Board for public use rules; OPC for privacy; SCC/Standards Council to convene technical norms), while universities and independent labs run evaluations, and industry funds/open-sources test suites and red-team bounties. Sector bodies (e.g., physician colleges, utilities regulators) should translate the rules into playbooks their members can use. Balance transparency with innovation by disclosing capabilities, limitations, eval scores, data governance, and update cadence—without forcing IP/weights disclosure. If users can see what an AI can and can’t do, who’s accountable, and how to get recourse, trust follows.

column2: Canada can build public trust in AI by implementing transparent, accountable, and inclusive governance frameworks that address risks like bias, misinformation, and privacy breaches. column3: To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, the country should get the Artificial Intelligence Act, which would mandate transparency, safety, and accountability for high-impact AI system. Complementary efforts include the AI Strategy Task Force, which is shaping national norms around ethical deployment, digital sovereignty, and public trust, and aligning with global standards such as the OECD AI Principles and ISO/IEC 42001 for governance and risk management.
column4: Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses to promote responsible AI use by investing in public education, fostering AI literacy, and facilitating inclusive consultations. Leadership should be shared: the federal government can set national standards and convene stakeholders, while industry, academia, and civil society are best placed to lead sector-specific engagement, co-develop ethical frameworks, and build trust through transparency and responsible innovation.

This is a complex problem and will require deep expertise to flesh this out.  As a starting point, I would suggest two things...transparency and consent.

Much like ingredients and nutritional information is included with packaged food, there needs to be something like that that's more explicit when it comes to anything using data.  The super long Ts and Cs with apps and services that use data (and AI) aren't sufficient.

From there, explicit consent is required.  While technically this is occurring today by "clicking accept", it is not informed consent.  To trust AI, what is happening with data needs be transparent and easily understood AND explicit (and informed) consent to use that data needs to be granted.  It's a regulatory measure that is likely needed to ensure proper AI service design.

At the end of the day, more transparent information makes for a better functioning market.

column2:
Public trust in AI won’t come from reassurance or regulation alone—it will come from participation. People trust systems they can take part in shaping.

Today, most AI tools operate on invisible data and opaque logic. That invisibility breeds mistrust. The risks are real: AI systems make errors in critical decisions, amplify bias in hiring and lending, erode privacy through surveillance, and generate misinformation detached from reality. Mistrust grows from three sources—opacity (people can’t see how decisions are made), inaccuracy (they can’t trust outputs), and powerlessness (they can’t correct mistakes).

The solution is to build tools for participation: easy, public infrastructures that let local groups, small businesses, and citizens publish verified information about who they are, what they do, and where they operate. When this structured data flows into AI systems through open, auditable channels, everyone can see, verify, and correct the representations that shape automated decisions.

Confidence grows through visibility and authorship, not abstraction. Public metadata infrastructure turns passive users into active participants in how truth is represented in machines—distributing responsibility for accuracy across society instead of concentrating it in a few platforms.

Governments should make participation easy: fund accessible tools anyone can use to publish verified information, establish open standards for provenance and interoperability, create independent auditing mechanisms so citizens can verify AI outputs, and support community-led governance of shared infrastructure.

Education matters—people should understand AI’s capabilities and limits—but literacy without agency breeds cynicism, not confidence. The goal is both understanding and authorship.

When citizens can see and shape how they appear in the systems that shape them, AI becomes not something done to the public but something built with it. That is how trust takes root.
column3:
Most AI regulation adds complexity. But trust doesn’t grow from more rules—it grows from simplicity people can use. Keep it Simple, Smart.

The Foundation — Verifiable Infrastructure
Real governance begins upstream. Establish shared metadata and provenance standards—interoperability protocols, audit trails, and transparent data classification—so AI systems can verify information at the source. This is the digital brickworks: government provides the basic building blocks so everyone can build on them.

The Framework — Accountability by Design
Require explainability in high-stakes systems, mandatory disclosure of training data sources, and independent audits for accuracy and bias. Certify Canadian-verified AI models that meet these provenance and transparency standards.

Priority Sectors
Apply these first in health, justice, finance, public services, and education—domains where algorithmic error or bias directly affects lives and rights.

The Norm — Participation as Oversight
Trust grows through participation. Frameworks must include simple, low-barrier tools—a free CMS and shared APIs—that let organizations publish and correct verified data easily. A system people can touch and improve is one they can believe in.

Governance Mechanisms
Create an independent oversight body with civic, technical, and institutional representation. Tie procurement to auditability—government only buys systems that disclose data sources. Adopt a liability framework: safe harbours for auditable AI, stricter penalties for opaque systems.

Government’s role isn’t to add layers of control, but to host the infrastructure that makes trust observable and participation effortless. Keep it simple. Keep it verifiable. Build foundations everyone can use.
column4:
Canada engages citizens and businesses not by explaining AI to them, but by giving them tools to shape how AI understands them.

The strategy: Provide free, simple publishing tools that let any organization—pottery studio, theater, restaurant, consulting firm—publish verified metadata about themselves. This isn’t tech-literacy training; it’s civic participation through infrastructure.

How it works: Organizations fill out a form like they would for social media, but the data becomes part of a shared knowledge graph. AI systems cite them as the authoritative source. Their knowledge flows to tourism sites, regional calendars, and AI assistants—traceable back to the source.

This levels the digital playing field. A local dance company’s structured metadata carries the same weight as a major institution’s—equal access regardless of budget or technical capacity.

Who leads what:
	•	Government: Host infrastructure, fund accessible tools, set open standards.
	•	Regional partners (nonprofits, tourism boards, cultural orgs): Coordinate local adoption and provide user support.
	•	Community groups: Govern metadata standards and ensure inclusivity.
	•	Businesses/organizations: Publish and maintain their own verified data.
	•	Independent auditors: Verify data quality and system integrity.

Balancing transparency with innovation: Organizations control what they publish and maintain authority over their representation. Innovation happens on open infrastructure, not locked platforms. Transparency means traceable sources, not surveillance.

Trust grows through participation. When people can see and shape how AI represents them, they trust both the systems and the governance. Canada’s role is to make participation effortless—so engaging with AI is as simple as describing who you are. This isn’t outreach; it’s democratic infrastructure—the more people contribute verified knowledge, the more trustworthy AI becomes for everyone.

column2:
Build public trust (do the few things that matter)

Prove it works + is safe: Stand up a National AI Evaluation Hub to red-team models, issue model cards/safety cases, and run an open incident + time-to-patch registry.

Use it where citizens feel it: Publish before/after service metrics (wait times, error rates) for AI-enabled programs; kill what underperforms.

Make privacy tangible: Default to privacy-preserving methods (DP, federated learning, confidential compute) and publish data-use notices in plain language.

Teach by doing: Fund rotations that put professors/students into real deployments, and require public postmortems—not campaigns—when things go wrong.
column3:
Frameworks/standards that make AI trustworthy (lightweight, enforceable)

Assurance baseline: Mandatory model cards, data lineage, eval against sector test suites, and adversarial testing before production.

Security & integrity: SBOM-for-models (weights, datasets, tools), signed artifacts, continuous monitoring, and ≤30-day patch SLAs.

Liability that unlocks adoption: Safe harbour if firms follow the assurance baseline + incident reporting; outside the channel, full liability.

Priority sectors first: Health, public safety/defence, finance, critical infrastructure—publish sector playbooks (controls, evals, ATO patterns) to reuse across programs.
column4:
Engage citizens & business (who does what)

Government (set the field): Run the Evaluation Hub, publish sector playbooks and quarterly scorecards, convene an independent ethics board with disclosure powers.

Industry (earn trust): Ship with model cards, open eval scores, audit trails, and clear user opt-outs; co-fund red-team exercises.

Academia/civil society (watch & explain): Lead public “how it works” briefings, run independent audits, and publish plain-language findings.

Mechanics that scale: Require explainers at point of use, public sandbox programs with citizen juries, and town-hall style post-incident reviews.

column2: Canada should not be trying to build trust in a fundamentally unethical and environmentally damaging technology; the technology itself is rotten to the core. If AI companies were more transparent about the environmental impacts of the technology, and from where their models have sourced their data, and then held to account under existing laws and regulations, particularly copyright laws, that would do something to show that the tech is being handled with some decorum by the Canadian government. However, any trust-building done by the Canadian government on behalf of AI companies is unethical, and goes against my vales as a Canadian, as well as the values our nation was founded upon.
column3: AI technology should be heavily scrutinized for its environmental impacts and unethical usage (putting artists and creatives out of work, taking jobs, etc.), as well as the copyright infringement inherent in its data-acquisition for creating and maintaining large language models. It is a fundamentally ecologically damaging and unethical technology; any companies adopting it should be made to give a full impact statement regarding the impacts that their adoption of AI will have upon Canada’s environment and people, and all Canadians should be legally able to opt out of using/training any AI technology.
column4: AI technology fundamentally cannot be trusted; it is unethical and environmentally dangerous. Any efforts made to explain the technology should explain the dangers inherent to it, and to protect Canadians from it. The only responsible way to use AI is to avoid using it altogether.

column2: AI is an inefficient, unreliable, and stultifying development whose greatest potential to make citizens' daily lives demonstrably worse. So far, across the globe, we have seen it used to spread misinformation, to drive teenagers to suicide, to induce psychotic delusions in the susceptible, to misdiagnose medical conditions, to corrupt and delete data, to deny care to patients, to target civilians for murder, to devalue labor, to devalue the human experience; we have seen it wielded as a threat against artists, writers, musicians, coders, workers of all stripes; we have seen it fail to return on investment.
column3: Canada should ban generative and analytic AI in all academic settings. It should ban generative and analytic AI use in all settings related to art and culture. It should ban generative and analytic AI use in all military settings. It should ban generative and analytic AI use in all medical settings. And if not, it should require any image, code, or information produced by AI to be labeled as such, in a noticeable and unmistakeable way. And then it should be banned.

column2:
Legislation that sets first requirements for transparency and accountability is the only way to do this. AI models need to disclose their complete training data publicly to ensure that it does not violate copyright or privacy considerations. AI models need to be implemented with indefatigable safeguards against copyright violation or producing content harmful to public safety, individual wellbeing, or personal privacy. In the absence of offering obvious social benefit, AI's environmental costs need to be mitigated through environmental regulation that requires data centers to build clean energy in proportion to their energy costs (the centre can either operate on that energy or provide it to the grid). These requirements should be applied to anyone offering an AI product in Canada irrespective of where it is from so that Canadian enterprises cannot be undercut by unethical and illegal operations in other countries, and Canada should endeavor to work with trade partners to enter into reciprocal agreements, trade deals, and treaties to ensure other countries are enacting equivalent parameters.
e absence of a company able to operate under these parameters, Canada should operate its own public corporation for ethical AI development.
column3:
A legislative framework that applies to both AI Canadian AI products and products developed elsewhere but offered in Canada, combined with international agreements to ensure partners recognize and adopt equivalent standards is needed.
Legislation is needed for transparency. Especially, Models must disclose their training data in a public and searchable to ensure copyright and personal privacy protections. 
Requirements that models are indefatigably prevented from doing certain things harmful to personal privacy, public safety, or wellbeing should be put in pace. Models for public use especially must not be able to produce images, video, or voice of a specific person, whether by name or from a picture or video. Models must also not be able to duplicate the work or style of a particular artist or writer. Models must not be able to provide responses that could glorify or mitigate the harmful aspects of extremism or hate, and must not be able to encourage violence or suicide.
column4: The government through effective regulation is itself best positioned. A non-partisan third party public agency dedicated to AI that would regulate the industry is perhaps more suitable than Parliament and Cabinet to technical questions and to appearing impartial and trustworthy.

column2: As an information security specialist, I can tell you with great certainty that AI cannot be meaningfully secured. The inputs and outputs are too arbitrary, and the models are a black box. There have already been several high-impact AI data leaks, none of which have resulted in any meaningful regulatory action (we're not even enforcing the regulations we already have on the books!). Moreover, almost all AI implementation so far has ignored user consent and bullied users into using AI even when they explicitly state they don't want to use it. The more that business tries to force the use of AI, the less uptake there is, and the more public opinion turns against it.
column3: Any regulation needs to be performed by people without a financial stake in the success of AI, and it needs to be exceedingly consumer-focussed, with practical teeth to its enforcement. Trust issues are most acute in areas of user consent. A completely basic start would be requiring businesses to make any AI use and data collection explicitly opt-in instead of opt-out. Companies whose products do not strictly require the use of AI in order to function should make it possible to use their product without downloading any AI libraries (this is incredibly important for security reasons, because AI cannot be meaningfully secured, as previously noted).
column4: The AI industry and most of its champions have already breached the trust thermocline. I work in tech, and I honestly cannot think of anyone left in the industry who's maintained enough ethos on the subject to act as a meaningful champion of AI to people with informed concerns about it.

The government sets rules, industry follows those rules, and unions help protect the performer voices. Trust grows when performers have Consent, Compensation, and Control, and when workers help guide AI’s future.
