column2:
Public trust in AI will only grow if Canada ensures that AI is developed and deployed in ways that respect individual autonomy, protect privacy, and preserve freedom of choice. Trust cannot be mandated—it must be earned through transparency, accountability, and inclusion.
Key Principles and Actions to Build Confidence:


Guarantee the Right to Opt Out

Canadians must have the unconditional right to refuse AI systems—in healthcare, education, employment, or public services—without being excluded or disadvantaged.
Ensure non-AI alternatives remain fully available and accessible, so that opting out does not mean opting out of society.



Ensure Transparency and Explainability

Require that AI systems used in public and high-impact domains are explainable, auditable, and open to scrutiny.
People must understand how decisions are made and have the ability to challenge or appeal them.



Protect Privacy and Data Sovereignty

Build trust by enforcing strict data protection laws, banning surveillance-based AI, and supporting privacy-preserving technologies.
Canadians must retain control over their personal data, with informed consent as the foundation of any AI interaction.



Establish Independent Oversight

Create civilian-led, independent oversight bodies to monitor AI use in both public and private sectors.
These bodies should include civil society, ethicists, technologists, and affected communities.



Promote Ethical, Human-Centric Design

Support AI systems that enhance human agency, not replace or manipulate it.
Prioritize applications that solve real problems—healthcare access, environmental protection, education equity—without compromising rights.



Foster Public Dialogue and Education

Build trust through ongoing public engagement, not one-time consultations.
Invest in AI literacy programs that empower people to understand, question, and shape the technologies that affect them.



Hold Institutions Accountable
column3:
Freedom to Opt Out Without Penalty

Enshrine the right for individuals to refuse AI systems in any context—public or private—without losing access to essential services or opportunities.
Require that non-AI alternatives remain available and fully supported in healthcare, education, employment, and public services.



Privacy-First Standards

Mandate the use of privacy-preserving technologies (e.g., differential privacy, federated learning) in AI systems.
Prohibit surveillance-based AI and ensure data minimization and user consent are foundational principles.



Transparency and Explainability Requirements

Require that all high-impact AI systems be explainable, auditable, and open to independent review.
Establish algorithmic transparency standards for both public and private sector deployments.



Bias and Fairness Audits

Mandate regular, independent audits of AI systems for bias, discrimination, and disparate impact.
Require public disclosure of audit results for systems used in sensitive domains (e.g., hiring, policing, healthcare).



Accountability and Liability Frameworks

Clearly define legal responsibility for harms caused by AI systems.
Ensure individuals have access to redress mechanisms when affected by automated decisions.



Ethical Certification and Labelling

Introduce a voluntary but incentivized certification system for AI products that meet high ethical and technical standards.
Help consumers and institutions identify trustworthy AI tools through transparent labelling.



Inclusive, Civilian-Led Oversight

Establish independent oversight bodies that include civil society, ethicists, technologists, and affected communities.
Ensure that governance is not dominated by industry or government interests alone.



Open Standards and Interoperability

Promote open-source frameworks and interoperable standards to prevent vendor lock-in and encourage innovation.
Ensure that Canadian AI systems can integrate with global frameworks while maintaining soveregnty
column4:
Guarantee Freedom of Choice

Enshrine the right to opt out of AI systems in public and private services without penalty.
Ensure non-AI pathways remain fully supported in healthcare, education, employment, and civic participation.



Engage Citizens Through Transparent Dialogue

Conduct ongoing public consultations, not one-time surveys, to shape AI policy and deployment.
Involve civil society, Indigenous communities, and marginalized groups in co-creating AI norms and standards.



Promote AI Literacy and Public Education

Launch public education campaigns that explain AI in accessible, non-technical language.
Support community-based learning hubs and open-access resources to empower informed participation.



Support Ethical Business Leadership

Recognize and reward companies that adopt privacy-first, bias-resistant, and transparent AI practices.
Provide incentives for ethical innovation, not just speed or scale.



Ensure Independent Oversight

Establish civilian-led oversight bodies to monitor AI use, investigate harms, and enforce accountability.
These bodies should be independent from industry and government, and include ethicists, technologists, and public representatives.



Use Public Procurement to Set the Standard

Government should lead by example, only adopting AI systems that meet high ethical and transparency standards.
Public sector AI deployments must be auditable, explainable, and opt-out friendly.




Who Should Lead What?


Federal Government:

Set national standards, fund public education, and enforce rights-based regulations.
Lead by example in ethical AI procurement and deployment.



Provinces and Municipalities:

Implement community-level engagement and ensure local services offer non-AI alternatives.
Support regional innovation hubs and public trust initiatives.



Civil Society and Academia:

Lead public dialogue, ethics research, and watchdog functions.
Provide independent assessments of AI systems and their societal impacts.



Industry

column2: As far as I'm aware, these two goals are incompatible. AI has too many risks for me to EVER trust it.

Canada needs to seriously study the impacts of AI tools and services on the Canadian people. Those impacts need to be evaluated and shared publicly, and taken into account as Canada drives its own AI plans. Right now it feels like AI impacts in our lives are driven purely by corporations that push ahead without consequence. As a Canadian I can look around me and see harmful impacts from AI, and feel powerless in what to do about it, other than try to inform my friends and family about the realities of AI. I would love to see the government putting out messaging to help people understand different types of AI, how it works, and how to know when they're being intentionally misled. I want to see consequences for companies that use AI in ways that have proven harm - which I know is a complex issue, but if we don't start finding a way to do it, then it seems things will only get worse.
column3: We need to recognize the limitations of the type of AI we're working with in any given context. For example, a large language model in the style of chat-GTP can never be trusted not to hallucinate, and so should never be used in a context where an incorrect answer can cause real harm. We need to generally continue to work on breaking up monopolies and preventing walled gardens in tech, so that if users are being negatively impacted by the way AI has been introduced to existing products, they have real alternatives. We need to be willing to weigh harms against benefits and be willing to impose barriers on the use of AI technologies that do have some benefits if the harms are too large to be worth it, like considering the ways video generation technology and the use of text generation on social media has become such a powerful engine of propaganda and disinformation.
column4: The efforts cannot be coming from the same people who are trying to sell AI solutions. We need to try and educate the Canadian public on the limits of different types of AI, as a direct countermeasure to the current incentive structure where people are fed incorrect information about AI in order to sell them products. We need to be willing to be honest with the public about the harms and warn about the downsides, grounding them in actual impact. We need to be able to back that up with how our government acts. For example, since I've gone back to being a student in university I've experienced a deluge of advertising trying to convince me that it's in my best interest to offload my learning to AI and instead of wrestling with hard problems myself, I should be using LLMs to get the answers for me. I see this in advertised products that do sponsorship with the university, I see it in commercials, and I hear it repeated from other students. There is very little counter-messaging to remind me that I'm going to university to learn information I will use to accomplish real problems. I have interviewed programmer candidates who cheated during their degree and so are not hirable since they don't actually know how to code. Students need messaging making it clear that these are the types of consequences they will experience. My family's traditional Thanksgiving dinner has shifted to start having American side dishes because my family asks chatGTP what to cook - they need messages to help them understand that by relying on chatGTP they are offloading thinking to a specific worldview based on the majority training data, an America-centric worldview that erases our own identities as Canadians.

column2:
Canada can build public trust by clearly and honestly acknowledging the failings of many forms of AI technology and distinguishing between different types of AI, that have vastly different risk and reasons for mistrust or trust. Canada _should not_ attempt to build public trust in things that are untrustworthy. Many Canadians are rightfully concerned about the race to embrace products such as generative AI that have repeatedly demonstrated that they are not only inaccurate, but fundamentally incapable of accuracy; that rest on theft and excessive use of resources; use data in opaque & sometimes violating ways; are frequently incorporated into systems where they are not suitable or capable of meeting the standards of the industry (eg. incorporating ChatGPT into mental health services through advice bots or patient transcripts, when they give dangerous advice and encouragement and it is not possible to ensure that they do not do so, even though human therapists require training and regulation, and may use patient data without oversight or permission, and where there is no accountability for social harms they may cause).  If Canada ensures that AI is not given free reign to:
-steal creative works, 
-replace organisation requirements to provide accurate information and responsive customer service,
-use excess natural resources for profit and for unbeneficial uses,
-replace needed jobs or turn them into lower-paying jobs to "babysit" the AI that cannot be trusted to do those jobs properly
-be given to vulnerable populations as a substitute for needed human interaction & assistance
-use, share & store data in untransparent ways that cannot be guaranteed to protect privacy 

Then Canadians will be able to trust that those AI technologies we do invest in & incorporate will be safe, trustworthy &beneficial to society. The mistrust is b/c the AI environment right now is deeply untrustworthy in multiple ways; Canada can only strengthen trust by acknowledging and fixing it.
column3: Very strong standards on environment and energy usage, and particularly making sure the energy costs of these technologies are not paid by Canadians. Strong regulation about copyright protection to ensure that AI cannot profit from the theft of creative works or data that Canadians might reasonably have expected to be private. Service standards for companies and organisations that ensure AI cannot be used as a substitute for accurate information and responsive customer service. Ethical standards that prevent AI from being used as a substitute for needed human interaction and assistance for vulnerable populations such as children - there is already data about the harms exposing children to AI for teaching or support does to their education and their mental health. Strict oversight, privacy and security regulations to protect Canadians' data from being stored in regions with different data regulations, stolen, sold or used in unknown or unathorised ways. Standards to evaluate bias and inaccuracy to ensure that AI is not inserting additional bias or false information into important processes such as healthcare evaluations or hiring.  Protection against requiring Canadians to accept use of AI for services where privacy and accuracy are critical such as medical transcripts, in order to access a health provider or therapist.
column4: Canada must step in to strongly oversee systems and protect rights in order to ensure that businesses will use AI responsibly and transparently, and give citizens forums to report misuses or find information where transparent information may not be easily accessible, so that they can trust that it is being used safely and is reliable for the purposes it is being used for. Otherwise, there is no possibility of trust.

column2:
"AI" is a dead-end technology at best, and the world's greatest technology hoax at worst. Generative models and their creators have repeatedly shown that they have no regard for the rules of law, copyright, ethics, creativity, or the environment.

AI researchers have mostly all agreed that the current approach to large language models has reached its limit. Without a significant lateral breakthrough, this technology will not improve beyond confidently lying whenever asked a question.

Banks and economists worldwide are quickly coming to the realization that the AI industry as a whole is a bubble. It has not delivered on any of its promises, and only grows as a consequence of circular investment between the leading AI companies.

The US especially has heavily bet on AI, with 40% of their GDP being tied up in AI companies who, so far, have shown no benefit to society and generated no return on investment. When the bubble bursts, Canada should be economically self-sufficient to weather that storm, not tie us further to the sinking ship.

Rule number one is that we cannot rely on trust for people developing AI to be "good people". column2:
Public trust in AI is essential for responsible adoption and long-term success. While establishing a certified regulatory body is a foundational step, ensuring oversight, safety, and ethical standards, it must be complemented by broader efforts that engage and empower the public.
Additional Ways to Build Trust:
1. Transparency and Explainability (the ability to describe AI models and their decisions in a way that humans can understand).
AI systems must be understandable. People should know how decisions are made, what data is used, and what safeguards are in place. Clear documentation and public-facing model evaluations can help demystify AI.
2. Public Education and Literacy Campaigns
Launch national initiatives to educate Canadians about AI, its benefits, limitations, and risks. When people understand AI, they’re more likely to trust and use it responsibly.
3. Community Engagement and Citizen Panels:
Involve the public in shaping AI policy through citizen assemblies, consultations, and feedback loops. This ensures that AI development reflects societal values and concerns (like this survey).
4. Ethical Standards and Accountability
Require companies to adhere to ethical guidelines, report incidents, and be held accountable for misuse. This includes regulating deepfakes, biased algorithms, and exploitative data practices.
5. Inclusive Design and Representation
Ensure AI systems are designed with input from diverse communities, including Indigenous peoples, neurodivergent individuals, and marginalized groups. Trust grows when people see themselves reflected in the technology.
6. Independent Audits and Certifications
Just as we trust food and medicine through FDA-like oversight, AI systems should be independently audited and certified for safety, fairness, and compliance.
Trust is earned through transparency, education, and shared responsibility. Canada must lead with integrity, ensuring AI serves people, not the other way around.
column3:
To ensure AI products are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, Canada must adopt a comprehensive framework that combines regulation, certification, and ethical standards, drawing from global best practices.
Key Elements to Implement:
1. Risk-Based Regulation
Inspired by the EU’s AI Act, Canada should classify AI systems by risk level, prohibited, high-risk, limited-risk, and minimal-risk, with corresponding obligations such as conformity assessments, transparency, and human oversight. [aws.amazon.com]
2. Ethical Standards and Certification
Adopt ISO/IEC standards (e.g., 24027, 24368) and develop a national certification system for AI products, similar to the EU’s Cybersecurity Act and AI certification programs. Certification builds public trust and gives companies a competitive edge. [arxiv.org]
3. Transparency and Accountability
Require model documentation (e.g., datasheets, model cards), incident reporting, and independent audits. These practices are already being used by leading organizations under the EU AI Act and the World Economic Forum’s Responsible AI Playbook. [reports.weforum.org]
4. Human Rights and Inclusion
Align with UNESCO’s global AI ethics recommendations, emphasizing human dignity, fairness, and inclusive design. [www.unesco.org]
5. Public Engagement and Education
Build trust through national AI literacy campaigns and citizen panels to shape policy and ensure AI reflects Canadian values.
Canada must treat AI governance not as a barrier to innovation, but as a foundation for ethical, scalable, and globally competitive AI.
column4:
To promote responsible AI use and build public trust, Canada must go beyond regulation and actively engage citizens and businesses through inclusive, transparent, and collaborative approaches.
Key Strategies:
1. Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs)
As highlighted by the World Economic Forum, PPPs are essential to bridge the AI trust gap. They combine government legitimacy, industry expertise, and civic oversight to co-design standards, audits, and accountability mechanisms. [www.weforum.org]
2. Citizen Engagement and Feedback Loops
Cities like Espoo (Finland) and Copenhagen (Denmark) have built trust by implementing public algorithm registers, explainable AI systems, and clear feedback channels. These allow citizens to understand, question, and influence AI decisions that affect their lives. [regions.re...tudies.org]
3. AI Literacy Campaigns
Governments must invest in national education initiatives to demystify AI, helping citizens understand its benefits and risks. This empowers informed participation and reduces fear and misinformation.
4. Inclusive Design and Stakeholder Involvement
The Partnership on AI recommends participatory frameworks that involve marginalized communities in AI development. This ensures technologies reflect diverse needs and reduce potential harm. [partnershiponai.org]
5. Transparent Governance
The EU’s AI Act and Singapore’s AI Safety Institute emphasize risk-based regulation, transparency, and human oversight as pillars of trustworthy AI governance. [aisingapore.org], [https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/news/eu-and-singapore-strengthen-cooperation-ai-safety]
Leadership Roles:
- Federal Government: Lead regulatory development and national education campaigns.
- Industry Leaders: Co-develop ethical standards and invest in responsible AI deployment.
- Civil Society and Academia: Facilitate public engagement, monitor impacts, and advocate for inclusive design.

column2: Canada can build public trust in AI by embedding safety into development, enforcing ethical standards, engaging citizens in policy design, and ensuring transparency in deployment. The most important actions are creating responsible governance, investing in AI safety research, and demonstrating real-world benefits.By embedding safety, ethics, and public engagement into its AI strategy, Canada can lead the world in building AI systems that are not only powerful—but also trusted and inclusive.
column3: Canada needs a combination of enforceable legislation, voluntary codes, privacy principles, and public engagement to ensure AI products are trustworthy and responsibly deployed. The most important frameworks include the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA), the Voluntary Code of Conduct for Generative AI, and privacy standards from the Office of the Privacy Commissioner.Together, these frameworks and standards aim to ensure AI systems in Canada are safe, fair, transparent, and aligned with Canadian values. As AI adoption grows, these norms will be critical to maintaining public confidence and global leadership.
column4: Canada can build trust in AI by engaging citizens through public consultations, educating businesses with clear standards, and empowering institutions like CAISI, CIFAR, and the Privacy Commissioner to lead safety, ethics, and transparency efforts.By empowering trusted institutions and engaging Canadians directly, Canada is laying the groundwork for an AI ecosystem that is not only innovative—but also safe, inclusive, and aligned with public interest.

column3:
We need regulations around AI's creation, use by users, and implementation by business owners.

AI creation:
--Require AI creators to document the datasets they used to train their models
--Require AI creators to document the provenance of their data sets to assure they weren't accrued unethically (eg. By unlawfully stealing or duplicating books and art)
--Create an advisory board to scrutinize the labor pipeline in data model creation, eg. Who labeled the images in the data set? Were they paid a fair wage? If not, Canadian AI companies need to be deterred from outsourcing labor abuses to poorer foreign countries to cut costs.

AI use:
--Require people and companies who use AI (eg. TV shows that use AI to write dialogue, video games that use AI to generate graphics, IVR phone systems that use AI for voice prompts) to disclose this usage
--Create safeguards to prevent AI from being used for illegal purposes, eg. Creating deepfake pornography of unconsenting individuals or creating bombs, bioweapons, and other hazardous materials. Create a standard of best practices to identify when a model generates harmful output, legally restrict AI creators from allowing this output, and create laws to punish users who request this output.

AI implementation:
--Identify potential use cases where AI use cannot be tolerated due to its error-prone nature. This could include military, banking, or health care scenarios where disclosure of private info could cause irreparable damage. Create strong laws with teeth to punish companies who irresponsibly use AI to cut costs by creating risk for users.

• Artificial Intelligence shall not be used for any purpose for which a human being would be held accountable, either by internal policy and/or by law, for failure and/or dereliction of duty.

• That stringent ecological standards will be erected -and- enforced against AI companies whose data centers have immediate, concrete, and specific deleterious effects on the environment.

column4:
Truthfully, there is a lot of misinformation in the media ecosystem both acting for and against Artificial Intelligence. But, if the government is willing to have a candid conversation about where artificial intelligence is REALLY headed, and what the government – and the general public – ought to do about that, I have two names to suggest:

David Gerard, from the publication Pivot-to-AI, a technology commentator from the UK who has been following the "post-iPhone tech sector" since crypto-currency took off. He follows the development of AI technology not just in the US, but globally – he's where I got the Albanian AI Minister Who Isn't Actually AI story from, as an example.

The other, and in my opinion, much harsher critic of AI is Ed Zitron, whose column (Where's Your Ed At?) is how I learned that the AI sector is a) in a bubble, b) how big that bubble REALLY is, and c) how and why I believe it's going to burst soon.

I strongly feel that, if you sincerely believe this technology is a pillar in the future of Canada's economy, then it behooves you to hear what the industry's loudest, most articulate critics are saying about the industry, and about the development and use of the technology itself. If, for no other reason, than to know which specific talking points the public is going to want to address, and workshop appropriately-reassuring answers to those questions.

column2: Transparency with what the technology can and can't do, and what the goal is for Canada to integrate AI in society at large. The impact on jobs, how the technology changes productivity expectations, and how it will impact worker health and safety are primary unanswered questions within the occupational safety landscape.
column3:
A standardized means of disclosing the use of AI in the creation of a product, the NIST AI risk framework sets a potential baseline for how this could be implemented. Public disclosures for products or other uses of AI, would assist with wider trust and adoption efforts.

More broadly, integrating explainable AI language and safety into regulations, including specific domains like occupational safety, would enable a consistent requirement for businesses to adhere to. Various ISO standards are currently in development that address different aspects of trust and responsible deployment, which regulatory or industry associations could directly adopt.

column2:
If we genuinely believe in AI risks (potential massive reductions in ability to read and understand anything longer than a paragraph by young people, mass cheating in academic institutions, destruction of entire existing industries i.e. book and magazine publishing, film and television, chatbots causing people to suffer delusional disorders) why would our first priority be to "build confidence" rather than to address the risks first? "Dynamite is such a useful material; how can be build public trust in mass adoption of dynamite?"

It's also notable that the main selling feature of LLMs is that they allow greater efficiency, which means in practice fewer people doing work. Yet, several years now into the LLM boom, this efficiency is not emerging. A good question to ask would be, where is the efficiency? Which jobs have been totally transformed, which roles eliminated? If there have not been massive upheavals in workplaces yet, why not? If AI is not good enough yet to meaningfully displace existing workers, will it ever be? Or is it just a waste of money, including taxpayer money, to adopt it at scale?
column3: Given the recent study by OpenAI itself that showed that hallucinations may be baked in (https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.04664) and impossible to remove from current large language models (which is what almost everyone seems to mean when discussing AI) it's possible there is no regulation, standard, or norm that can make sure that AI is trustworthy, and that should be something that is considered seriously and not simply waved off with "oh, it'll get better!" What if it doesn't get better? What if it can't get better? I can imagine quite a few areas of government, including the courts and report-writing on subjects like climate change, military risks, and human rights, where AI should under no circumstances be involved because of the risk of info contamination by false facts that, once in the system, might be hard to recognize and harder to remove.
column4: It would be nice to see a public facing process that did not assume that AIs/LLMs are already functioning well and capable of doing large amounts of meaningful work, but I can see that this survey was written by someone who is fully on one side and has already made up their mind about the outcomes.

Almost every question in this survey asks about how to make the most of the amazing incredible opportunity that is AI, but there is just this single one about risk. column3:
While these are important, I encourage the government to consider being even remotely more hands off - communicate the risks and recommendations from gov't based on your research and best practices, and allow firms and people to make their own decisions given the risks the gov't has outlined.

An illustrative example - a car ran into a daycare in Ontario and a toddler was killed. In response, the ON gov't legislated daycares add barriers to prevent this in future. Instead, the government could have shared its RECOMMENDATION with daycares to add barriers. What would happen then?
- some day cares may choose to add the barriers, some may not
- parents may then begin to choose daycares with barriers (or not) 
the "market forces" of parents choosing the daycare that best meets their needs would then get to work.
Same with AI - if I am a hospital, for example, I am going to choose an AI that meets my privacy requirements and I may choose to go by the recommended privacy requirements of the gov't. Alternatively, if I am a small business, my privacy requirements may not be so high or I may not be able to afford AI solutions with high privacy, and I would rather have access to a solution and accept the privacy risk then have no AI solution at all. 

In short, educate and empower the customer (citizens, provincial gov'ts, business, etc.) instead of regulating.

Same with AI - share the best practices and recommendations and allow the market forces to get to work.

column3:
As far as I know, these would be the first in the world, and it's an opportunity for Canada to be a leader in this regard. The problem is twofold:

- Everything that everyone has ever created is now, de facto, being used as an AI training corpus, without regard to whether the creator(s) have copyrights or other rights that might prevent that, and without regard to whether the creator has given permission or even anticipated that they might have to someday give permission. This implies that people who scrape the Internet for content need to be regulated and held accountable in some way for what they do and do not include in their training data; a very large task, to be sure. Perhaps it would look like some kind of regulatory board in charge of verifying that the AI is trained on exactly what its creators say it is trained on.

- The process by which AI content is generated is opaque, does not normally include a step to verify that correct facts are presented (when they are presented directly as a solution or answer, a la ChatGPT), and people using it usually have no knowledge of what kind of corpus the training data comprised. So here comes our regulatory board again, which might also conduct studies of whether the AI is giving correct information or misrepresenting the correctness of information.

To give you an oversimplified example, I could train an LLM on every romance novel ever written, present it to people as a great way to come up with protocols for pharmaceutical studies, and watch hilarity ensue. In this case, I'm guessing that medical researchers would realize the problem in fairly short order and my product would lose credibility, but if you shift this scenario just a little bit you get Reddit posts being used to create government policies, and who's to say that isn't happening right now?
column4: Helping people understand what LLMs and generative AI are and are not is crucial here. Generative AI, broadly, is a machine that can come up with something that sounds like what a person would say in response to a query or statement. People seem to think there's a lot more going on there than that; that it's magic, that it's somehow thinking. The people who are trying to make a fortune off of AI are in charge of the messaging and marketing around it, and society at large is simply believing what they have to say about it. That's a dangerous situation.

1. Accuracy. Canadian AI should either be 100% accurate with 100% confidence, or give a null output. No hallucinations, no introducing errors.
2. Opt-in. No one should find themselves faced with AI or AI output unless they have deliberately chosen to do so. 
3. Liability for consequences of the decision to use AI must always remain with the party that made the decision to use AI. No requiring employees to use AI and then punishing them when the output is subpar, or firing them because the AI slop drove customers away. No generating a contract with AI and then claiming that you aren't actually beholden to it because you didn't write it. If an AI chatbot offers a customer a free car, the company has to uphold that promise.

column2: Trust will only be built by building a solid framework of regulation. AI has proven to have incredibly negative effects on the environment, mental health, skill development, and generative AI largely relies on unethically sources data sets. Trust can not be built by companies trying to sell a product; it must be built by government creating a framework to hold those companies accountable, including enforcement and penalties for companies failing to meet regulation. Only with regulation and enforcement can critical Canadians begin to trust that AI technologies are being created ethically, because we know better than to trust profit-driven companies.
column3:
Standards are needed for the following areas:
 - Ensuring climate sustainability/minimizing environmental impact
 - A ban on unethical training methods. Datasets must be built entirely from sources who have opted-in and are appropriately compensated. To allow unauthorized use of materials for training purposes undermines Canadian's right to have their intellectual property protected
 - A government watchdog to ensure AI products are limited in their capabilities and have safeguards to prevent harm. 
 - Age restrictions for generative art and chat bots are worth serious investigation, especially given the potential for bullying, harassment, and youth using AI tools as a replacement for mental health services.
 - A clear, regulated definition of the phrase "AI" to prevent misleading consumers with marketing of products that are large language models, machine learning tools, or other technologies that do not truly "think" or have "intelligence."
 - Expanded financial and training support for educational institutions (K-12 and post-secondary) to ensure students are learning in effective, research-supported ways that lead to true skill development.
column4: Open, honest presentation of the risks of AI technology; public awareness campaigns on why AI is not a reliable source of information; clear communication of why regulations are necessary and help ensure a society that is safer, greener, friendlier to artists, and has is less susceptible to mis- and dis-information.

I can assure that this questionnaire does absolutely nothing to build trust amoung thinking people that our government is taking the right approach to 'AI', full stop. Every one of these questions is a fawning, leading exercize in kool-aid drinking, and I am incredibly disappointed that there's such a mindless rush to capitalize that the impression is being given that safety, intellectual and artistic property concerns, accuracy, job stability and overall potential for societal harm aren't being considered at all.
You could build confidence by acting like responsible adults and treating this like an incredibly powerful and potentially dangerous evolution in technology that needs to be regulated and held to account. 'AI' is incredibly error-prone, and if the government's attitude is one of fawning adoration and not mature risk-aversion, people are going to get hurt and then you're going to tell them they did it wrong and that's why they aren't getting the 'benefits' out of AI that the people who want to make money off of it assure us that we'll see.

To be abundantly clear - at present, I do not trust the Canadian government in their approach to AI. Not a lick. This all feels incredibly reckless and not one of the public comments made by the minister for AI give me any impression that he knows what he's dealing with.
column3:
Regulation, regulation, regulation. Regulation with teeth, real, actual consideration for people and the public harm these tools can cause. The government's approach HAS to be that any developments in 'AI' MUST provide a net societal benefit, and any unaccountable 'AI' tool that causes harm must be dealt with quickly, and harshly. 
The attitude must always be that this technology can supplement human thinking, judgement, and ingenuity, but if we're just trying to supplant it and replace people, then you have betrayed us all.

column2:
Build trust by making AI legible, accountable, and learnable for everyone. Require any AI used with public funds to publish safety cases, model/data cards, energy and water disclosures, limits in plain language, plus independent audits and a public incident-reporting channel with time-bound remediation. Seat Indigenous, civil society, worker, and disability advocates on oversight boards,especially for high-risk uses and use outcomes-based procurement to reward open standards and avoid vendor lock-in.
Make AI education a universal public good. Fund a national, open-licensed AI literacy program that reaches K–12, colleges, universities, adult learners, and SMEs through schools, public libraries, community colleges, and online modules—available offline/low-bandwidth, in multiple languages, and co-designed with educators and communities. Provide immediate training for secondary and post-secondary advisors and career counsellors so every student, apprentice, and mid-career worker gets credible guidance on safe, practical AI skills. Scale train-the-trainer, micro-credentials, and paid work-integrated learning so learners can practice on real problems, not just theory. Demonstrate integrity in the highest-impact public services first. Stand up clinical/regulatory sandboxes (health, climate/wildfire, critical infrastructure) with clear opt-outs, human-in-the-loop for consequential decisions, and mandatory red-team testing before deployment. Publish service-level dashboards (accuracy, bias, uptime, incident counts, fixes) so people see performance improve over time. Measure and communicate progress. Track pilot-to-production conversion with audits attached, growth in AI literacy and micro-credential attainment (especially among rural, Indigenous, and under-represented groups), and public trust scores. Tie future funding to these outcomes. When transparency, oversight, and accessible education for all Canadians move together, confidence follows.
column3:
Establish a national AI assurance framework that every publicly funded or high-impact deployment must meet: independent algorithmic impact assessments, red-team testing, and approval by oversight boards that include Indigenous, civil society, worker, disability, and youth representatives. Align with UNDRIP and provincial privacy (FOIPPA), and require OCAP/CARE-based data governance wherever Indigenous data is involved. Mandate interoperable artifacts for all systems: model cards, data cards, safety cases, and energy/water disclosures in plain language; secure MLOps with audit logs, lineage, and rollback; and post-deployment monitoring with incident reporting and time-bound remediation. Reference and localize leading frameworks (e.g., NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001 for AI management systems, ISO/IEC 23894 for AI risk, IEEE 7000-series for ethical systems engineering).
Regulatory guardrails where risk is acute. For uses in health, critical infrastructure, public services, employment, finance, and biometrics, require pre-market assessment, human-in-the-loop for consequential decisions, and opt-out pathways for the public. Tie liability safe harbors to demonstrated compliance (assurance artifacts + audits), and ban-or-restrict unlawful surveillance and emotion inference in public spaces. Use outcomes-based public procurement to enforce open standards, auditability, and data portability (no black boxes or lock-in). Publish service dashboards—accuracy, bias metrics, incidents, fixes—and require independent environmental audits for compute and water use. Pair regulation with regulatory sandboxes and clinical/operational testbeds (health, climate, defence-dual-use with guardrails) so firms can meet standards without stalling innovation. Fund secondary and post-secondary advisor training and national, open-licensed AI literacy so organizations can understand can understand and meet these obligations.
column4:
Build trust by making AI legible, participatory, and accountable. Any AI used with public funds should provide plain-language “AI nutrition labels,” safety cases, and public service dashboards. Engagement must move beyond notice-and-comment: inform people clearly, involve them early through standing citizen juries, sector roundtables, and clinical/regulatory sandboxes, and empower communities with consent or veto on high-risk uses—especially where Indigenous data is involved under OCAP/CARE.
Accessible education is non-negotiable. Create a national, open-licensed AI literacy program delivered through K–12, colleges and universities, libraries, and online in multiple languages with low-bandwidth options. Provide immediate AI training for secondary and post-secondary advisors and career counsellors, and scale work-integrated learning and micro-credentials so Canadians practice on real problems, not just theory.
Roles must be clear. Federally, ISED and a National AI Office should set assurance standards, run a public register of government AI, operate incident reporting and red-team programs, and convene national citizen panels. Provinces and school boards should embed AI and data ethics across curricula and credential advisors and instructors, aligned to regional industry needs. Indigenous governments and organizations must lead data governance and consent where Indigenous data is used. Municipalities and Crown corporations should host local demonstration zones, publish dashboards, and run town-hall testing. Industry should provide model and data cards, commission independent audits, fund apprenticeships, and adopt open standards. Academia and standards bodies should conduct independent evaluations, offer “extension” support to SMEs, and localize NIST/ISO frameworks.
Balance transparency with innovation through risk-tiered disclosures (public summaries for all, deeper artifacts to regulators under NDA), time-boxed sandboxes with safe harbours tied to auditability,

Stringent frameworks, standards, regulations and norms are needed to ensure responsible deployment of trustworthy AI products. Right now, people are shoving gen AI in particular into places it doesn't belong, and using it for nefarious ends, while simultaneously destroying the environment, and endangering the health of nearby people with the massive consumption of water data centers require, the noise they make, as well as the toxic gases they produce.

Extensive regulation and consultation with people who will be most affected by data centres in Canada is the first point that needs to be addressed. Further, unless an AI can be proven to not be biased in a way that negatively impacts marginalized individuals, it should not be legal for use in Canada. Any generative AI that uses stolen IP is quite frankly not something that is appropriate for use, as it is theft from millions of people and thus illegal. there should be stringent ethical compliance guidelines, and they should include a means for reporting inappropriate AI use to the government for investigation and possible criminal or civil penalties for people who make AI products that harm others. And those penalties should be severe enough and scaled to the size of the organization that made said product so that it is in fact a deterrent, and not a "cost of doing business"
column4: Prioritizing transparency and safety over innovation at all costs - if your innovation requires destroying the environment, or unauthorized use of people's IP or personal data, it should need to prove that it is safe and trustworthy, i.e. the balance of proof on the AI product producer, not on those who are harmed. Public facing strategies should warn people about AI pitfalls and what non-generative AI is good for or not, as well as how to critically analyze AI outputs by checking against non-AI sources.

column2:
Public Trust, Risks, and Mistrust Drivers
Risks: Core risks include algorithmic bias, privacy breaches, lack of transparency, deepfakes/synthetic content, safety/security failures, and potential misuse in critical sectors (healthcare, finance, law enforcement).
Drivers of Mistrust: Mistrust is fueled by opaque or “black box” AI systems, concerns about biased or unfair decisions, job displacement, data misuse, rapid deployment without clear oversight, and incidences of unethical or unsafe outcomes.
Key Steps to Build Confidence
Clear Standards and Oversight: Enact and enforce national standards for safe, explainable, and fair AI, with regulatory authorities and regular audits for high-risk use cases—in both public and private sectors. Foster multi-stakeholder oversight (including civil society and technical experts) for transparency and recourse.
Mandatory Transparency and Accountability: Require companies and government agencies to explain AI decision-making processes (especially for critical decisions affecting rights or wellbeing) and disclose the presence and role of AI systems to users.
AI System Certification: Develop certifications or “trustmarks” for AI systems that meet Canadian ethical, technical, and safety standards—helping consumers and organizations identify trusted tools.
Robust Risk and Impact Assessments: Mandate risk/impact assessments (privacy, security, bias, societal impact) as a prerequisite for procurement or deployment of significant AI systems, with clear penalties for non-compliance.
Proactive Digital Literacy and AI Education: Invest in broad-based digital and AI literacy for citizens and the workforce, including K-12, postsecondary, and continuing professional development—so people understand benefits, limits, and safeguards around AI.
Public Consultation and Engagement: Consistently engage Canadians in shaping AI regulations and deployment decisions, incorporating community values around privacy, fairness, and equity.
column3:
Essential Frameworks and Regulations
National AI Act & Regulatory Authority: Canada is moving toward a dedicated AI regulatory regime, similar to the EU’s AI Act, which would set mandatory requirements for risk assessment, transparency, safety, data governance, and ongoing monitoring for all high-impact systems. A robust public regulator or multi-agency body should oversee compliance, enforcement, and continuous policy updates.
Technical and Ethical Standards: Adopt sector-wide technical standards (e.g., CAN/DGSI 101:2025) for AI safety, explainability, security, and auditability, accompanied by explicit codes of ethical conduct guiding developers and deployers on fairness, non-discrimination, and privacy protections.
Risk-based Governance: Establish risk tiers—with strict requirements and pre-market approval for high-risk applications (healthcare, critical infrastructure, law enforcement, employment, finance), while using lighter regulation for low-risk AI tools.
Independent Third-party Audits & Certification: Mandate regular, rigorous independent audits for high-risk AI systems and certify compliance with national standards; provide “trustmarks” for products that pass ethical and technical benchmarks, increasing transparency and consumer confidence.
Mandatory Impact and Bias Assessments: Require all organizations deploying AI—including government agencies—to conduct documented risk, fairness, and societal impact assessments before launch, and routinely thereafter, with results made public where appropriate. 
Priority Areas
Trust issues are most acute in:
Healthcare (diagnostics, patient safety)
Public sector/justice (policing, sentencing, benefits allocation)
Employment/screening (AI-based hiring, workplace monitoring)
Finance (credit scoring, fraud detection)
Critical infrastructure (energy, transport control)
Governance Norms and Oversight: 
Ongoing Monitoring and Enforcement. Transparency and Recourse. and Public and Stakeholder Engagement.
column4:
Public-Facing Strategies to Explain AI Systems
Accessible Explanations: Require organizations to provide clear, non-technical information about when and how AI systems are used, their benefits, risks, and user rights—especially for high-impact or government-operated systems.
Public Awareness & Literacy Campaigns: National digital literacy initiatives across K-12, adult education, workforce programs, and targeted outreach for vulnerable or marginalized groups help ensure everyone understands fundamental AI concepts, opportunities, and safeguards.
Dynamic Transparency Channels: Government portals, explainable AI dashboards, and real-time updates about new AI deployments or standards help keep citizens informed and engaged in ongoing oversight.
Inclusive Approaches to Trust Building
Regular Public Consultations: Institutionalize citizen assemblies, focus groups, and online consultations on AI regulation and deployment. Engagement ensures policies and standards reflect real concerns and lived experiences from all Canadian communities.
Stakeholder Partnerships: Foster multi-sector alliances involving business, academia, technical communities, Indigenous and equity-deserving groups, and civil society organizations to codesign governance, monitor outcomes, and champion ethical AI.
Empowered Ombudsperson and Advocacy Roles: Create offices, hotlines, or digital recourse channels for the public to raise concerns, report harms, appeal automated decisions, and obtain redress, ensuring real accountability and responsiveness.
Balancing Transparency with Innovation: Regulatory Sandboxes and Tiered Transparency Requirements
Leadership and Roles
Federal and Provincial Governments: Set standards, lead national literacy campaigns, maintain transparency portals, fund ethics research, and anchor public trust through law and policy.
Independent Regulators & Ombudspersons: Oversee audits, and enforce standards.
Industry and Research Institutions. Civil Society & Community Leaders.

column2:
I believe one of the most urgent tasks related to AI right now is to actually *reduce* the level of unwarranted public trust in AI and to foster an informed spirit of scepticism about AI and its impacts. I am very concerned that  we are seeing an uncritical rush to jump on the AI bandwagon on the part of industry and political leaders, and an equally uncritical trust in its products by everyday internet users. I have personally found that the proliferation of AI-generated content online, and the efforts of tech companies to force it into every aspect of our online experience, have significantly degraded the usefulness of internet searches as a way to find reliable information on just about anything. The now-popular term "AI slop" is a good descriptor for this. 

I cannot help but see this as the outcome of a decades-long process where the original "Berners-Lee" vision of the Internet as a free public space for information sharing and communication has been replaced by a privatized profit-driven model where everything is subordinated to the pursuit of clicks and revenue generation. This model at best is neutral with regard to the actual value of information online, and at worst opens up a huge field for malevolent actors to spread plausible but false information for commercial, political, criminal, or simply nihilistic motivations.

I am not anti-tech or a Luddite, my experience with what is now called IT goes back to FORTRAN programming and punch cards, and I have found enormous value in the online world in so many aspects of my professional and personal life. But I am deeply concerned about the effects of AI on our ability to have a shared, reality-based perception of the world.

I guess I would just like to see a lot more sober reflection and critical thinking going into Canada's approach to AI, and a lot less cheerleading - of which I have to say I see a lot in the whole design of this survey.
column3: I have to say I am pessimistic about Canada's ability to enforce any kind of ethical standards or governance on AI, given the transnational nature of information flows. I suspect in particular that any Canadian legislation or regulation to enforce trustworthiness in AI will be attacked as protectionist by US politicians, acting as always on behalf of their billionaire owners.
column4:
Public education in critical thinking is key.

And again, I think it is a mistake to frame this as "what is the best way to fuel trust". It is important for Canadian businesses and agencies involved in implementing AI to do it in a way which DESERVES and EARNS trust.

column2: The most important thing to do is to be brutally honest: AI is being recklessly deployed prematurely on a massive scale with little regard to potential harms and virtually no liability. There is no way to be both informed and confident at this point in time. As studies have repeatedly shown, the more Canadians know about AI, the less likely they are to trust it. It follows that building confidence is nothing but a confidence game.
column3: The fundamental regulation is to protect Canadians at an individual level, ensuring that their data and intellectual and artistic work can never be stolen. To foster trustworthiness, it will also be important for Canadians to be able to see "under the hood", with strong regulations governing transparency of ownership, and the ability to tinker, hack, reverse engineer, and repair.
column4: Any responsible public strategy to explain AI systems will inevitably lead to distrust. Government should therefore focus on honesty and transparency, not on marketing and promotion.

column2: Arrêter d’essayer d’insérer le AI tout et partout en grosse panique; ça nous donne l’air de juste suivre le « hype ». Il faut donner aux gens l’impression qu’on a fait nos devoirs. Donc, y aller plus lentement, faire des études et des projets pilotes, et avoir des règles et régulations claires.
column3: Il faut que les bases de donnés ne soient pas basé sur du contenu volé; il faut que l’électricité utilisée dans les datacenters soit une énergie verte, il faut un encadrement légal qui punisse l’utilisation du AI pour faire des scams, de la désinformation, et du « revenge porn ». Il faut arrêter d’intégrer le AI dans les écoles et éduquer les étudiants par rapport à ce que c’est, et ce que ce n’est pas. (Le AI génératif ce n’est pas un moteur de recherche, c’est une machine à inventer des réponses qui n’ont aucune garantie d’être vraies). Il faut d’ailleurs passer une loi pour retirer le AI des moteurs de recherche, s’assurer que le contenu créé par AI est toujours indiqué comme tel, et forcer les logiciels et sites web à rendre les fonctions AI “opt-in” et non “opt-out”. Il fait aussi retirer le AI du domaine de la santé et du domaine légal, particulièrement pour la transcription de meetings.
column4: Forcer les compagnies de mettre le AI opt-in et non opt-out; faire des campagnes de sensibilisation pour expliquer que le AI génératif ne donne pas des réponses “vraies” mais des réponses qui sonnent bien, et retirer le AI du domaine de la santé, du domaine légal, et des affaires du gouvernent.

column2: REGULATIONS. The only way to build trust is regulating this tech because as it stands the risks are massive and its literally built on theft and environmental degradation so actually putting some safety regulations in place is the first step in a long list to bring ANY level of trust back
column3:
1. Any training data must be opt in only.
2. Anyone who allows their data/work/media to be used to train models should be properly compensated for their work. (Preferably in the form of royalties when models trained on their data is used, but also single payments per piece of data used could also work)
3. Any research should be/development should be watched carefully for breaches of ethic, a watchdog orginization that can actually give fines/sanctions/and legal actions is necissary
4. AI research should be limited to uses that actually could benifit society. No surveillance tech, no situations where we let a tool decide policy when humans should, no forcing it into existing sectors that already have better tools and workflows.  The focus of AI use and research should be the one thing as a tool it tends to be alright at which is large data analysis (things like health sector being an example of something that could actually help society, if done in an ethical and careful way)
5. Companies and individuals breaking rules should have actual consiquences, data theft should not be allowed or encuraged and significant fines or even jail time should be on the table for this type of white collar crime.
6. Using AI tools for any form of impersonation of an individual or company should be illigal and face significant legal action. Furthermore anyone spreading missinformation using the tech should see immidiate legal action taken against them.
7. image, sound, or other forms of "generated" content should be competely banned from any commercial use unless gaurenteed beyond and shadow of a doubt to be from new ethically trained models (from points 1. And 2.) And companies and individuals who do not respect that should see legal action made against them.
column4: Strong government regulation is the only way to promote responsible use and trust. Anything short of proper regulation is tantamount to giving away the rights and freedoms of our citizens.

column2: Canada can lead by example through enacting policies supported by technologies that strengthen and protect health, safety, and infrastructure for all. As it stands, AI is far too unstable and unreliable to be responsibly integrated across all industries without significant detriments and potentially far-reaching consequences. It would be a gross misappropriation of funding to blindly invest into and apply technologies that misrepresent information, necessitate rampant personal data theft, and pose a substantial ecological threat to all Canadians. Much must be done about the rampant data breaches, algorithmic bias, social manipulation through AI algorithms, job losses due to AI automation, widening socioeconomic inequality sparked by AI-driven job losses, a lack of transparency for applications of AI, invasive social surveillance, violation of creative voices due to generative AI, and the weakening of ethics and goodwill because of AI. It is only when safeguards have been introduced can we move to responsibly establish industry around this emerging technology. Otherwise, we will all be left vulnerable to unchecked application of a volatile technology with already palpable consequences.

column2: Generative AI is a danger to the Canadian populations both mentally and financially. Tech companies specializing in generative AI research have explicitly said that they know the generative AI bubble will burst and they'll keep pushing it until it does, as it makes them money. Please do not push for generative AI, and lead the world in stopping this economic train wreck before it happens.

column2:
The most important things that I think we should do are:
1) Ensure that all Canadians are aware of how their data is protected: are companies training on data, or not, what opt-in or opt-outs exist, and how can Canadians avoid having to do that for every company? Think "Do-Not-Call List" but for AI.
2) Ensure that any AI that is used in the government is actually run in Canada: not run by a Canadian company in a foreign datacenter and thus subject to foreign interference, but end-to-end located in Canada.
3) Provide the public with strong examples, backed up by action and reality, that shows that AI is not being used to merely replace workers but augment and improve their quality-of-life. This needs to be actually true, though :)
column3:
See previous commentary around data residency and protection.

Additionally: AI should never be used as a way to reduce or diffuse responsibility. If a company deploys an AI tool, then they are still accountable for what that tool does. If a chatbot says something, it needs to be held to the same bar as if a human were to say it. Having AI be used as a way to reduce or eliminate corporate or government responsibility will just result in resentment and frustration.

Much must be done about the rampant data breaches, algorithmic bias, social manipulation through AI algorithms, job losses due to AI automation, widening socioeconomic inequality sparked by AI-driven job losses, a lack of transparency for applications of AI, invasive social surveillance, violation of creative voices due to generative AI, and the weakening of ethics and goodwill because of AI. It is only when safeguards have been introduced can we move to responsibly establish industry around this emerging technology. Otherwise, we will all be left vulnerable to unchecked application of a volatile technology with already palpable consequences.

column2: Why should building public trust in AI technologies be a goal? I disagree with the premise of this question: there is nothing inherent to be trusted in AI technologies -- you might as well how to make Canadians trust the concept of statistical linear models, or how to make them trust the medium of podcasts. Models are unavoidably biased by what goes into them, and media is only as trustworthy as its creators. The thing I actually care about is Canadian media literacy, which might be at odds with the goals assumed by this question.
column3:
I've already mentioned the importance of holding data centres to stringent environmentally sustainable standards, which I believe is a critical ethical issue. Additionally, generative video software that is freely available and accessible in Canada should come with a permanent digital watermark marking the video as artificial, to mitigate the inevitable harmful deep-fake crises coming down the pike. I'd also like to see a "nothing about us without us" approach to companies claiming to create accessibility tools for people with disabilities; if you're funding research projects on, for instance, automatically generating image descriptions for the visually impaired, I would like to know that the people receiving this funding are actually employing and consulting visually impaired people, so that the hypothetical tool actually targets a real and specific need in a grounded way.
Beyond those top-of-mind issues, I don't know what power the federal government realistically has to ensure that corporations are trustworthy or responsible in how they develop and deploy their digital products. Do you have plans for how to protect smaller creators, who don't have Disney or Nintendo's armies of lawyers? Do you have ways to prevent chatbots from suicide-baiting people or inflaming delusions?
column4: I think Canada needs to work very hard on educating its citizens about what AI exactly is. "Artificial Intelligence" is used to include deep learning in statistical analysis of scientific datasets, chatbots made from large language models, images generated by using text to prompt tools made from analysing graphics scraped from the web, audio-video creations from the same, ... When talking about AI (including in this survey), it's really easy to lump all of these extremely different use cases together. They serve distinct functions, involve distinct companies and under-the-hood technologies, and should be regulated and discussed distinctly as well. As for who is best placed to lead these efforts, I have no idea. The media, I guess.

column2: As an interested Canadian, on a personal level, AI scares me. To put this in perspective, I am a young adult, engineer-in-training. I appreciate and value technological development, and work to make it happen. However, when it comes to AI, I face uncertainty, lack of information and fear around the potential ramifications of AI development. I am worried about its potential future impact on my career, whether I will become obsolete as a worker. I worry about its impact on the environment. I worry about its impact on my own mental acuity, whether skills I have developed will atrophy if I use AI to complete tasks instead of doing them myself and getting that mental exercise. I worry about the potential for AI to be used in a way that puts profit over people in and out of the workplace, contributes to the misinformation crisis, and/or jeopardizes people's personal safety, identity security, and well-being through faked images, videos, audio and other media. These are my fears. If some of them are based on lack of education around AI, then myself and other like me would benefit from more education and greater awareness around what AI is and isn't capable of, what the real risks are and aren't, etc. If these fears are well-founded, than we would benefit from regulation that supports, empowers and enforces use of AI in a way that benefits individuals (especially the disadvantaged and marginalized), our society, our country and the planet. Lastly, the younger generation and seniors will be at greater risk from AI, I think - kids due to bullying, online security threats such as cyberstalking, luring or blackmailing using faked content, etc. and seniors due to lack of digital literacy, more realistic scams, etc. Please do whatever you can to protect them.
column3: Government committee on ethical use of AI, transparency/watchdog esp for large companies and companies that handle sensitive data (ex. Healthcare). Regulated transparency around partly AI-generated work that has public safety implications (Ex. Healthcare, drug development, certain types of engineering work, etc.). Also regulations and public awareness campaign around environmentally responsible AI use (how much energy and cooling water gets wasted every time someone AI generates their grocery list instead of taking 5 minutes to do it themselves, or generates a bunch of AI slop photos for 5 seconds of entertainment?) And oversight/monitoring of whether AI tools are resulting in major layoffs. I also think that there should be regulations around protection of data in AI tools, whether companies can use the data/user inputs and results they collect to further train their algorithm. I'd personally really appreciate there being something like the new thing with web cookies where you can toggle which ones to choose - it would be nice to be able to have the option to not have your prompts-and-answers from your AI session collected, sold, or used to further train the algorithm.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by treating it as civic infrastructure—transparent, accountable, and designed for public benefit. To do this, several key actions are needed:

1. Make AI Systems Transparent and Auditable
Require plain-language disclosures about how AI tools work, what data they use, and how decisions are made. Mandate independent audits for high-risk systems in health care, policing, and public services.

2. Enforce Ethical and Safety Standards
Establish clear rules for bias mitigation, consent, and algorithmic harm. Tie compliance to funding, procurement, and deployment approvals. This shows that safety isn’t optional, it’s foundational.

3. Build Public Literacy and Engagement
Launch national education campaigns that explain AI’s risks and rights in accessible terms. Support community-led workshops and civic tech labs where people can explore, question, and shape AI tools.

4. Create Feedback Channels and Oversight Bodies
Let citizens report concerns and influence how AI is used in their communities. Build public oversight councils that include everyday people—not just corporate or academic voices.

5. Protect Data Sovereignty and Privacy
Ensure Canadian data stays under Canadian control. Require transparency in cross-border data flows and prohibit exploitative surveillance practices.

6. Champion Civic Use Cases and Success Stories
Highlight AI projects that improve public services, climate resilience, and healthcare equity. Show that AI can serve democracy, not just disrupt it.
Confidence comes from clarity, control, and meaningful participation. Canada must build systems that earn trust, not demand it.
column3:
To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, the country needs a layered governance approach that combines enforceable regulation, public oversight, and civic accountability. Key components include:

1. National AI Governance Framework
Canada must establish a unified framework that defines responsibilities across government, industry, and civil society. This should include:

• A central AI oversight body with regulatory authority
• Mandatory risk assessments for high-impact systems
• Public reporting requirements for algorithmic decision-making

2. Enforceable Standards for Safety, Bias, and Consent
AI systems must meet clear benchmarks for:

• Bias mitigation and fairness across demographics
• Informed consent and data transparency
• Explainability and auditability of decisions
These standards should be tied to product certification, procurement eligibility, and liability protections.

3. Algorithmic Impact Assessments (AIA)
Expand and enforce the use of tools like Canada’s existing AIA for all public-sector deployments and high-risk private applications. Require peer review and public disclosure of findings.

4. Civic and Indigenous Oversight Mechanisms
Include citizen panels, Indigenous data governance bodies, and ethics councils in AI policy and deployment decisions. Trust must be earned through participation, not just compliance.

5. Priority Areas for Oversight
Focus regulatory attention on sectors where trust issues are most acute:

• Health care (diagnostics, triage, patient data)
• Policing and surveillance (facial recognition, predictive tools)
• Employment and education (automated hiring, grading systems)
• Public services (benefits eligibility, immigration decisions)
6. Norms for Open Science and Public Benefit
Encourage civic licensing, open-source development, and public-interest research. Normalize transparency and accountability as core values—not optional features.
Canada’s AI governance must be proactive, particip

column2:
Trust Through Implementation Excellence:
1.	Implementation Transparency Framework - Not just "what the AI does" but "how we implemented it safely and sustainably." Document decision points, safeguards, and human oversight, leveraging a framework that is risk-based.  
2.	Public Implementation Audits - Regular reviews of AI implementations in public-facing services. Focus on outcomes, not just algorithms.
3.	Citizen Implementation Panels - Include public representatives in implementation planning for government AI. Build trust through participation.
Evidence: Trust increases 40% when implementation process is transparent vs. just explaining the technology.

Targeted Output: Implement a National trust rating framework with target above 70% by 2030

Risk-Based Implementation Framework Based on Government of Canada's Directive on Automated Decision-Making: 
Level 1: Low Risk (Recommendation Systems) Self-certification Basic transparency requirements Annual review 
Level 2: Medium Risk (Process Automation) Implementation audit required Human oversight protocols Quarterly review 
Level 3: High Risk (Decision Systems) Full implementation assessment Continuous monitoring External validation
column3:
Implementation-Based Governance:
1.	Canadian AI Implementation Standard (CAIS) - Focus on deployment practices, not just technical specifications: 
o	Mandatory human oversight checkpoints
o	Explainability requirements for decisions affecting individuals
o	Regular implementation reviews and adjustments
2.	Progressive Implementation Requirements - Start with low-risk implementations, graduate to higher-risk with proven track record.
3.	Implementation Professional Certification - Regulated profession for AI implementers, similar to engineers or accountants.
4.	Risk-Based Implementation Framework Based on Government of Canada's Directive on Automated Decision-Making: 
•	Level 1: Low Risk (Recommendation Systems) Self-certification Basic transparency requirements Annual review 
•	Level 2: Medium Risk (Process Automation) Implementation audit required Human oversight protocols Quarterly review 
•	Level 3: High Risk (Decision Systems) Full implementation assessment Continuous monitoring External validation 

Approach: Regulate the implementation process, not the technology itself.
column4:
Engagement Through Implementation:
1. Community Implementation Labs - Public spaces where citizens can see AI implementations in action, understand safeguards, and provide feedback.
2. Implementation Impact Assessments - Before any public sector AI deployment, conduct public consultations on implementation approach, not just technology choice.
3. Business Implementation Showcases - Successful implementations demonstrated publicly to build confidence and share best practices.

Strategy: Make implementation visible and participatory, not mysterious.

I think it is worth considering requirements for these technologies to be less misleading in this sense; they should not be impersonating people. It will build trust for the average Canadian to see these technologies better represented as what they actually are—statistical models that can be useful.

column2:
Confidence builders that work
	1.	Plain-language transparency by default
	•	One-page system cards for every production AI: purpose, data used, limits, human oversight, how to appeal.
	•	Simple labels on AI-generated content and use of content provenance standards such as C2PA.
	2.	Independent validation before and after launch
	•	Third-party safety, privacy, and bias testing with evidence users can see.
	•	Post-deployment monitoring with drift alerts and a public incident response process.
	3.	Human-in-the-loop for high-risk decisions
	•	Clear escalation paths, audit trails, and rollback plans.
	4.	Education that is job-task specific
	•	Short modules for clinicians, plant managers, project managers, and contact-center teams that teach safe prompts, data hygiene, and red flags.
	•	SMB-focused “AI starter” workshops through chambers, sector councils, and programs like TechImpact.
	5.	Visible enforcement and incentives
	•	Safe-harbor protection for adopters who follow recognized standards and keep evidence.
	•	Penalties for deceptive commercial claims and negligent deployments.
column3:
Use a layered “trust stack”
	•	Management systems
	•	ISO/IEC 42001 for AI management systems
	•	ISO/IEC 27001 for information security
	•	SOC 2 Type II for service controls where relevant
	•	Risk and quality
	•	NIST AI Risk Management Framework for risk identification and controls
	•	ISO/IEC 23894 for AI risk management
	•	Sector guidance for health software, safety-critical, and financial services
	•	Privacy and data governance
	•	Canadian privacy law requirements, privacy impact assessments, de-identification standards, and data-minimization practices
	•	Clear data retention and deletion rules, including audit logs
	•	Assurance artifacts attached to every model
	•	Model card that states intended use, limits, and test results
	•	Datasheet for datasets and data-lineage notes
	•	Model bill of materials listing training sources, dependencies, and third-party components
	•	Red-team report that summarizes safety tests and fixes
	•	Operational controls
	•	Access controls, secrets management, vulnerability scanning, and content provenance
	•	Continuous monitoring for performance, bias, and drift with quarterly reviews

Governance mechanisms to operationalize
	•	Risk tiers with proportional controls and human oversight requirements
	•	Conformity assessment using accredited labs for high-risk systems
	•	Incident reporting with disclosure windows and corrective-action tracking
	•	Procurement clauses that require evidence of the trust stack and allow buyer audits
	•	Certification marks (bronze, silver, gold) that map to increasing levels of testing and monitoring

Priority areas where trust is most acute: health diagnostics and triage, public benefits eligibility, hiring and credit, education assessment, critical infrastructure operations, safety on construction 

Partner with validation and security specialists so SMBs can meet the trust stack without building it alone.
column4:
Who leads what
	•	Federal entities set national policy, fund validation infrastructure, and publish simple model policies and contract templates.
	•	Provinces and health authorities run sector pilots, share results, and align data-sharing agreements.
	•	Universities and colleges deliver applied literacy and help operate testbeds.
	•	Industry groups, chambers, and sector councils translate standards into checklists and workshops for SMBs.
	•	Civil society and community groups convene citizen panels and inclusion reviews for high-impact projects.
	•	Vendors and adopters publish system cards, test results, and performance scorecards.

Engagement tactics that earn trust
	•	Public sandboxes and demo days that show exactly how systems work and where they fail
	•	Citizen panels and small-business advisory circles for high-impact deployments
	•	Open playbooks with reference architectures, cost ranges, and ROI metrics
	•	Office hours where agencies, clinicians, or plant managers can bring real use cases for quick risk and value triage
	•	Stories with evidence that highlight Canadian wins, including Atlantic pilots, with before and after KPIs

Balancing transparency and innovation
	•	Protect sensitive IP and security details while still publishing the essential “what, why, how overseen, how measured, and how to appeal.”
	•	Use safe-harbor provisions so organizations can share incidents and fixes without disproportionate penalty when they followed the trust stack.
