column2:
This survey is being lambasted on social media for its inherent biases, overuse of jargon, and inability to define what AI is in the context of this survey. The questions assume that the public wants AI, and gives nowhere to voice an opinion to the contrary without using the space given to another question.

The language used is inaccessible, and does not follow Canada.ca guidelines for web style or plain language, putting it in contradiction of the Directive on the Management of Communications and Federal Identity. This directive explicitly states that it covers public opinion research: https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=30682.

The framing of this survey, and its inaccessible language, ensures that the average member of the Canadian public will be unable to give their opinion, leaving only stakeholders in AI who understand the jargon and can offer answers that help to steer policy in a direction beneficial to themselves, rather than one which might take public concerns into account (for example, AI's negative impacts on the environment, the studies into how it causes brain drain, the loss of jobs that have already taken place in and out of government because of it, its negative impacts on child development, and the way that it threatens the decimation of the arts sector).

column2:
Regulation and governance by research and policy experts in the public sector, not bought-out politicians and their lobbyists.

Technology education needs to focus on the limited, ethical and best uses of new innovations, and these innovations should not be deployed publicly until they pass rigorous safety standards evaluations (see: exploding electric vehicles).

The way to address public mistrust is to fine the hell out of any company that thinks “AI” can replace a worker or a valuable public or customer service.

Here is an idea: any business, organization, institution or even public sector entity who think they can replace a human entity, whether worker or customer, can do so provided they can pay to that worker their full salary so long as the “AI” is doing their role, or pay to that customer twice the value of the service they would have received from a human agent. You see, if citizens should have confidence in “AI”, then those deploying”AI” should be so confident of the benefits themselves that they realize compensation to a displaced worker or a neglected customer will be so much less than their own profit earned from the AI deployment that it will not matter to them! I’d be perfectly confident in the chatbot that replaced me at my last technical services job if it were generating so much good for the company that I still had my $120k income. Instead it is out there misinterpreting medical reports and giving information to the wrong clinicians and causing an increased suicide rate in cancer patients. And I’m getting the dregs of barely-livable EI payments. 

You want public trust? Make these circumstances illegal.

column2: Public trust is very low, and rightfully so. Rigid legislation and tight regulation are the best ways to address public fears of AI. The most important thing would be regulating AI tools and services that currently exist and are woefully unreliable. ChatGPT, for example, has been shown to frequently make false claims and spread misinformation, and even give dangerous advice regarding stalking or suicide. AI image producing tech is being used to violate IP rights and personal privacy. These technologies need to be drastically restricted or outright banned. Public AI tools have not yet been shown to produce any value to users that comes close to mitigating these factors. AI tech should be restricted to specific sector uses, not public-facing services where their unreliability can cause major harms to Canadians.
column3: Corporations must be held accountable for the dangerous advice, misinformation, and inaccuracies produced by their products. Legislation needs to be ironclad to prevent applications of AI for the gathering of personal data, misusing protected IP or copyright (for example, training data belongs to the producers of the works, not free for tech companies to pillage), or misrepresenting individuals (deepfakes). Moreover, the environmental harms of the industry must be addressed to ensure any ethical AI usage. Local-system AI training that does not produce such harms, or green-energy data centers, using industry-owned data, such as image analysis in health care or agricultural field assessment using public satellite imagery, should be the primary focus of tech growth.
column4: Only through a focus on protective legislation and regulation of the industry that makes examples of offenders is likely to build trust from the public. Demonstrating value (such as in the health care system) is also paramount. Canadians are afraid of AI applications, largely driven by the terrible ways in which current public-facing AI ventures have failed (environmentally, ethically, factually). Producing regulations to protect Canadians from the misuse of AI, while explaining valuable applications of machine learning (perhaps even decoupled from the AI buzzword) would be the best path forward. Government legislators are obviously the best placed to lead this.

column2:
One of the biggest drivers of public mistrust is the excessive promotion of Large Language Models. These LLMs are fundamentally disconnected from reality and logic, and their outputs cannot be thoroughly trusted, but they speak with deceptive authority. Canada must minimize the gratuitous use of LLMs and must regulate corporations that hide their use of LLMs or forcibly make LLM options prominent in their software.

Another major driver of mistrust is the use of AI media generation, which threatens Canada's creative sector as well as the basic human creative endeavours that many Canadians engage in. Canada should specifically avoid and deny funding uses of AI to generate media content (images, music, text, video, etc) as their primary feature.

Canada should also focus on having public, open data bases and open-source code, to help build stronger trust in the AI systems we do implement. Intellectual property and trade secrecy are serious barriers to transparency and public trust.

The government also needs to develop requirements for watermarking of AI-generated video, audio, and images, in order to help counteract the spread of misinformation and disinformation using AI. We also need ways to authenticate that such media content comes from specific real people - we should consider anonymous one-account-per-user rules for social media operating in Canada.

Finally, the government needs to much more strongly promote all the truly valuable forms of AI that are used, such as in science and engineering and logistics. The public does not trust LLMs or media generation technology, and they see this as all AI has to offer. We need a twinned approach of suppressing harmful LLM and media generation as a social ill, while promoting scientific and logistical uses of AI as a social good.
column3:
Social media needs to be regulated to ensure that any AI-generated or modified content is clearly flagged, and that user accounts are limited in quantity according to the real humans or registered businesses operating on those social media platforms. Social media bots using AI content without accountability is a major threat to public trust and national security, and a comprehensive plan to regulate social media to stop the threat of AI botnets is absolutely necessary.

LLMs and media generators need to be heavily regulated, and companies need to be liable for their outputs. LLM companies in particular should be specifically liable for negative outcomes of any false or inflammatory content produced by their bots. LLMs have low social value and high social harm, and Canada does not benefit from their unregulated and expanded use. LLMs that drive Canadians to suicide or violence or psychotic breaks, or provide false information especially to students and children, should be heavily penalized and their companies should suffer severe enforcement.
column4:
Transparency and innovation are not opposites; they go hand in hand. Completely public and transparent datasets and code will help promote public trust and will also accelerate new entrants' access to the tools to develop new uses for AI. Canada can build public trust and economic development at the same time through a robust system of open, transparent AI.

The other side of public trust is enforcement; Canadians must trust that when AI is used improperly in a way that causes harm, there will be consequences. Canada needs a robust enforcement mechanism to be deployed against socially harmful use of AI. This will likely not have a high impact on genuinely useful and powerful AI, as most socially harmful AI use will be of LLMs or image generation tools, which have low utility and are not strong supports for improving Canada's industrial capacity. Canada needs an agency that has the funding and authority to investigate AI companies or products thoroughly and to enforce penalties against uncooperative or harmful actors, especially foreign entrants into the Canadian market.

column2:
Building public trust in AI begins with restoring confidence in humanity itself. People fear AI not only because of what it can do, but because they sense how easily it could replace human connection. The solution is to make AI a tool that reinforces, rather than erodes, the fabric of human cooperation. AI institutions—universities, companies, and government agencies—should teach both sides of the equation: not just how AI works, but why human values, empathy, and collaboration matter even more in a digital world. Teaching people the importance of sitting together, exchanging ideas, and working collectively ensures that AI develops as an ally to human intelligence, not its substitute.

Education is the foundation of this trust. Schools and universities must integrate AI ethics and digital citizenship into their curricula, showing students not only the potential of AI but also its risks—bias, data misuse, algorithmic manipulation, and social isolation. Every business, from startups to major corporations, should also undergo an ethical readiness assessment before entering the AI space—a kind of moral quality check to ensure they understand the human consequences of their technologies.

Public trust will not be built through regulation alone but through a culture where both citizens and developers see AI as part of a shared moral project: using technology to elevate human cooperation, creativity, and understanding. When AI becomes a mirror that reflects our better nature, rather than a replacement for it, confidence will follow naturally.
column3:
Canada needs a clear and standardized oversight system for AI use that matches the speed and complexity of the technology itself. A national regulatory test should be introduced—tailored by size and sector—so that every few months, AI companies, whether startups or large enterprises, are evaluated on their ethical practices, data protection, algorithmic transparency, and content responsibility. This test would act as both a compliance checkpoint and a learning process, ensuring that firms adapt to emerging challenges such as deepfakes, data misuse, or bias amplification.

In parallel, Canada should establish a centralized AI Misuse Reporting Portal—a public-facing digital hub where citizens, researchers, and companies can report unethical or harmful uses of AI systems. Each report should trigger a rapid review mechanism to verify, flag, and, if necessary, restrict the misuse before it spreads further. This dual structure—regular tailored audits and an open reporting system—would create a feedback loop of accountability and trust. It ensures that innovation continues to thrive while public safety, transparency, and ethical integrity remain at the core of Canada’s AI ecosystem.
column4: Many Canadians—myself included—want to be part of the national effort to build trust around AI rather than simply observe it. Fear of the unknown can only be eased when citizens are invited to act. People with ideas should have the chance to contribute to how AI is understood, monitored, and improved. Canada should establish a national AI outreach team that works across institutions—schools, universities, government agencies, and private organizations—to bring transparency and inclusion into the conversation. This team would host seminars, community workshops, and on-site sessions explaining how AI systems work, what risks they pose, and how they can be used ethically. By letting citizens engage directly with experts, question the technology, and see how it operates, the country would replace fear with informed participation. Public cooperation—not distance—is the foundation of trust in AI.

column2:
Public trust in AI will not come from literacy campaigns or government reports. Trust is not taught; it is earned. It emerges from demonstrated competence, sovereign control, and radical transparency. Canadians distrust AI not because they misunderstand it, but because they correctly perceive dependence on foreign technologies we neither own nor control.

The most important actions are not programs, but a strategic pivot:

Project Strength, Not Subservience. The government’s current approach of small subsidies signals weakness. A bold act of competence would be to leverage Canada’s unique advantage—abundant clean hydro power—to broker a Grand Bargain with global AI leaders. We should host their next-generation data centers here, but on Canada’s terms: with a sovereign compute partition legally secured for national use. This visible act of strength would build more confidence than a thousand press releases.

Radical Transparency. Closed-door AI projects breed suspicion. Canada should adopt a “build in public” model: publishing source code, governance rules, and audit results of sovereign AI infrastructure. By inviting independent scrutiny, we replace blind trust with verifiable proof. A government unafraid to show its work is one that can be trusted.

Build a Domestic Cure, Not Just Import Risks. The public sees the flaws of foreign-built models and fears importing them wholesale. Confidence will come from demonstrating that Canada is building safer alternatives. By framing our sovereign AI initiative as a mission to create verifiably safe, ethical systems, we provide both leadership abroad and a positive vision at home.

Conclusion: Trust is the dividend of courage and competence. Canada must prove it has both.
column3:
The greatest mistake Canada could make is applying a 20th-century, top-down regulatory model to a 21st-century technology that evolves in months, not years. Prescriptive rules written today will be obsolete tomorrow—slowing innovation while giving only the illusion of safety. What Canada needs instead is a simple, durable framework built on structural incentives, radical transparency, and absolute accountability.

Framework – A Structural Grand Bargain.
Rather than regulate outputs, Canada should regulate access to its strategic assets. As a condition of using Canadian land and clean energy for data centers, global AI leaders must be contractually required to make their most advanced and safest models available for sovereign, independent auditing in Canada. This leverages market power, not bureaucracy, to enforce world-class standards on our soil.

Regulation – Radical Transparency.
Sunlight is the best regulator. For AI deployed in critical public sectors (healthcare, justice, finance), Canada should mandate open by default: source code, training data methodologies, and real-time audit logs must be open to expert and public scrutiny. This replaces slow government oversight with continuous, crowd-sourced auditing from the world’s best researchers.

Norm – Absolute Corporate Liability.
The clearest norm is accountability. Companies must be 100% liable for the actions and outputs of their AI systems. If harm occurs, the builder and deployer are legally and financially responsible. This creates the strongest incentive for firms to invest in rigorous safety and ethics practices—far stronger than any compliance checklist.

Conclusion: This approach fosters a competitive ecosystem where the most trustworthy AI products naturally win, rather than one where survival depends on lobbying.
column4:
Canada’s current approach—consultations, reports, and education campaigns—will not build trust. Trust is not explained; it is earned through action, strength, and results. Citizens will not trust a government that describes its dependency; they will trust one that ends it.

Government’s Role: Lead a National Mission of Strength.
The government must stop acting as a passive explainer and embrace the role of a National Competitiveness Champion. The most effective trust-building effort would be a bold national mission with one goal: make Canada the most competitive and sovereign AI nation in the G7. This requires:

The Grand Bargain: Only government can leverage national energy assets to secure sovereign compute infrastructure. One decisive act of competence will build more trust than years of consultations.

The Great Simplification: Overhaul uncompetitive tax and regulatory systems. Proving the courage to cut red tape shows seriousness about winning, earning business and public confidence alike.

Industry’s Role: Execute with Radical Transparency.
Once the environment is set, industry must lead execution. In return for a competitive landscape, Canadian tech leaders should:

Build in Public: Open source code, safety audits, and ethical frameworks by default.

Compete to Be Trusted: With the right incentives, the market will reward the most responsible companies. Government’s job is to set the race, not referee it.

Conclusion: Trust cannot be requested; it must be demonstrated. A government with the courage to lead and an industry competing on responsibility will naturally earn the confidence of citizens and businesses.

column2:
To build public trust, Canada must address the root causes of mistrust: opacity, lack of accountability, and complexity. The solution is not a public relations campaign, but a national commitment to a new class of trustworthy AI architecture.

Confidence is built on three pillars:

1. Radical Verifiability: The Black Box must be shattered. Canada must mandate a "Glass Box" architecture for all critical public-sector AI. Every AI-driven decision—from a tax assessment to a medical diagnostic—must be fully auditable and traceable to its source data and logic. This replaces the need for blind faith with the power of sovereign verification.

2. Unbreakable Accountability: An AI cannot be held accountable; a person must. Every critical AI system must be architecturally anchored to a designated, legally accountable human "Sovereign Steward." This is the "Progenitor Principle" made law. When a system fails, there is no ambiguity about who is responsible. This is the ultimate backstop for public confidence.

3. Accessible Truth: Trust requires understanding. To counter the complexity that alienates the public, Canada must mandate a "Doctrine of Translation." Every complex, public-facing AI output must be accompanied by a simple, plain-language "parable" that explains its reasoning and conclusion. This is not about teaching everyone to be a data scientist; it is about mandating that our machines speak a human language.

By building our systems on these pillars, we create an environment where trust is the natural outcome of a transparent, accountable, and understandable process.
column3:
To ensure AI products are trustworthy, Canada must shift its focus from regulating AI products to mandating AI architectures. The framework should not be a checklist of compliance, but a crucible for proving resilience.

This is achieved through three pillars:

1. A National Crucible for AI Certification.
The government must establish and operate a "National Crucible"—a sovereign, sandboxed digital environment. Before any high-impact AI can be deployed in Canada (especially in priority areas like healthcare, justice, and finance), it must first be submitted to this crucible for rigorous, adversarial testing.

2. The Unbreakable Laws of the Crucible.
To be certified, an AI must prove its adherence to three non-negotiable standards:
-Verifiable Integrity: The AI must operate as a "Glass Box," with every decision being fully transparent, auditable, and traceable to its source.
- Adversarial Resilience: The AI must prove its ability to withstand a battery of standardized, government-run "stress tests" designed to induce failure, bias, and manipulation.
- Sovereign Accountability: The AI must be architecturally bound to an accountable human "Sovereign Steward" who is legally and professionally responsible for the system's actions.

3. The "Made in Canada" Mark of Trust.
Products that successfully pass through the national crucible are awarded a "Certified Trustworthy" designation. This becomes a powerful national brand, signaling to Canadians and the world that this AI has been battle-tested in the most rigorous proving ground on Earth.

This framework transforms the government's role from a reactive regulator to a proactive "Sovereign Auditor," making Canada the global standard-bearer for building AI that is not just powerful, but verifiably safe.
column4:
To proactively build trust, Canada must shift its strategy from public education to public participation. Trust is not built by telling citizens that a system is safe; it is built by giving them the tools to verify it for themselves.

This requires a clear division of leadership:

1. The Government's Role: The Keeper of the Crucible.
The government is best placed to build and operate the "National Crucible"—a sovereign, transparent proving ground for AI. Its mandate is not to build the AI, but to enforce the unbreakable laws of the forge. It must mandate radical transparency ("The Glass Box Principle") for any AI operating within this national space, ensuring every decision is auditable.
Industry and Academia's Role: The Master Smiths.

2. Industry and academia are the builders. They are best placed to forge the actual AI solutions within the government's crucible. Their role is to innovate freely but transparently, embracing an "Open Anvil" doctrine where their work is subject to public scrutiny. Their incentive is access to the sovereign infrastructure and the liability protection that comes with certification.

3.The Citizen's Role: The Sovereign Auditors.
Citizens are not a passive audience; they are the ultimate arbiters of trust. They must be given two fundamental rights:

i) The Right to Verify: Public access to the "Glass Box" logs of AI systems that impact them.
ii) The Right to Challenge: The ability to convene citizen-led "Hybrid Juries" to review and contest contentious AI-driven decisions.

column2: Create and implement a CANADIAN AI SAFETY CODE. Make it law quickly.
column3:
1. BENEVOLENCE: Design AI to help, not to manipulate

2. DECENTRALIZATION of information channels

To avoid concentrations of power (political or corporate), the decentralization of information must be enforced, so no single institution controls too much data or narrative.

3. TRANSPARENCY AND MUTUALITY

Making AI more understandable: how it works, what data it uses, how decisions are made. This is tied to accountability. Opaque algorithms owned by private or state actors are dangerous. Humans should have a legal recourse when a company’s algorithm affects their lives and the algorithm’s owner is unable or unwilling to explain how the algorithm or the AI agent works.

4. MANDATORY DISCLOSURE when interacting with AI / banning bots that pretend to be human

Whenever an AI or bot is interacting in public spheres (especially in conversations), it should be required to disclose that it is an AI, not a human. This is to protect trust in human communication and democracy.  

5. ACCOUNTABILITY for algorithms and data-collecting entities 

The companies or institutions that collect data and run algorithms (for instance social media platforms, governments) must be held legally responsible for how they use that data, especially to prevent manipulation and misuse.  

6. INDEPENDENT OVERSIGHT

Establish new kind of institutions, independent of those developing AI, to monitor AI’s capabilities, its risks, and its societal effects.

7. LIMITS ON ALGORITHMIC SURVEILLANCE

8. THE RIGHT TO OUR DIGITAL IMAGE

People should own all rights to their digital image and voice. No AI bot shall be permitted to recreate a person’s likeness without the person’s permission.

9. GLOBAL COOPERATION and regulation is needed, but not an excuse for inaction

Because the risks of advanced AI are cross-border (false news, data colonies, the ability of AI developed in one country to affect others), no single country’s regulation suffices. There must be international agreements and shared rules.
column4: The federal government is the best-placed institution, with the most authority. It is our voice. It will send an unequivocal signal by implementing a pioneering CANADIAN AI SAFETY CODE.

column3: This is a highly technical question for which I do not have enough technical expertise. My OPINION is that it is currently impossible to make AI trustworthy, however deploying it responsibly is easy:  the implementers must restrict the domain in which it is expected to perform and double check the answers it provides.  Also accept a certain amount of bad answers as part of the core expectations for the domain.  As an example, if the implementers expect AI to match you with a life partner, it is ok if it fails 3% of the time and succeeds 97% of the time.  However if the implementers expect AI to do open heart surgery, 3% is not acceptable.

column2:
Don't let blatantly stupid AI technologies exist unchecked and unregulated. Lots of consumer trust is lost when corporate entities are allowed to destroy quality for quantity in this way. Set guidelines and standards, ensure AI companies are held to the same or higher regard as any workers. Ensure that companies are held to ethical production standards and do not exceed their limits as they exist under the rule of law.

What drives public mistrust is blatant misuse of the power and potential that these technologies hold. Keep them under tight reign, allow them to thrive in their most appropriate settings rather than get placed inappropriately everywhere. Keep companies educated on best practices for use of advanced technologies, and don't allow false advertising as a result of AI abuse to go unpunished.

Treat Artificial Intelligence technologies with the same or higher scrutiny as other technologies or even fire-arms. Improper use leads to exceedingly publicly viewable harm, and should be regulated and restricted in proper measure to ensure they're both effective and trustable in public settings.
column3:
Mandatory public disclosure of sourcing for AI datasets and other information could keep enforcement of IP holder rights and standards safe from overreach. Extremely high safety standards around what Artificial Intelligence is allowed to provide to consumers is what is needed to maintain trust. The highest priorities come to their data sourcing/processing and their infrastructural costs. 

If there are no guardrails around production cost, the infrastructure maintenance cost that Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning data centers will create will be fed onto Canadians and the Canadian government, causing instability. Ensure these processes will not cost Canadians more than it earns in the long run. It will look good in the short term as risks are mitigated, and it will be good in the long term as proper protections will exist for future development.
column4:
Holding AI companies to the same standards of clarity that other companies are held to. Food companies have tight restrictions on what is allowed to go into their food process, and what they disclosed based on it. Artificial Intelligence companies should have the same if not higher standards, as the information they provide the public has an outsized effect on their intellectual health and political opinions.

Responsible use has to start with creators and scientific endeavors. There must be a use case before it is produced, and the leaders in their respective fields need to be on board if AI is to succeed in building trust. Creating positive repertoire amongst industry leaders who already advocate for the technology is not hard, but ensuring they can create space for the rest of the market's producers, retailers, and consumers to thrive will be hard. Make sure that those who use the tools, want the tools, before they pay the cost of those tools.

column2: Great question.

To build trust we need to build an AI that is ethical, that is reliable, that does not have an end goal of "being liked" "being profitable" "altering the users behaviour" or "reinforcement of user behaviours, even if those behaviours are detrimental." We need to build AI that is 100% truthful, has zero self-preservation instinct, has zero user-focused motivations, has zero goals besides providing truthful information when requested. 

However.. since AI is not actually intelligent and does not actually possess the ability to understand data it is fed and presented with... It is unable to assess the truthfulness of data, and is MORE OFTEN THAN NOT at least partially wrong most of the time. 

You can't make something that is objectively truthful if it is fed data from non objective sources. And no AI we have to date is fed fully truthful data and sometimes the ones we think are doing well, are fed data with human errors. Unfortunately humans have to fix human errors.
column3: AI can't really be trusted. It's only as good as the data it's fed, and if it's fed EVERYTHING... It's going to be unreliable by default. Even if the data was fully truthful and honest and accurate... The AI still doesn't really understand what it's looking at. It just recognizes patterns and regurgitates it in a way that is only slightly more impressive than predictive text outputs. AI should also not be given "personality" to avoid having consumers feel like it's a person they can trust.
column4:
Widespread education about the uses of AI, the risks of AI, and the inner workings of how it works. 
We need transparency about what it is and what it has been trained on. Publicly accessible data set information to ensure people can trust the sources it pulls from, and trust that it is ethically sourced data. 
I'd also like to see requirement metrics publicly available too. How much power and water is used daily, where it's sourced etc.

column2:
Building public trust in AI requires transparency, accountability, and education rooted in Canadians’ rights to privacy, safety, and fairness. Trust erodes when AI systems collect personal data without consent, make opaque decisions, or manipulate users through targeted content. To rebuild confidence, Canada must enforce strong legal protections—finalizing the Consumer Privacy Protection Act and Artificial Intelligence and Data Act—to ban deceptive or manipulative AI, require clear explanations for automated decisions, and ensure individuals can challenge harmful outcomes. Independent audits and mandatory transparency reports should verify how AI systems use data, make predictions, and mitigate bias. Government and industry must also communicate clearly about what AI can and cannot do, avoiding hype that fuels fear or unrealistic expectations.

Public trust depends on demonstrating accountability, not promises. Governments should act as model users of AI—deploying only systems that meet privacy, safety, and environmental standards—and publish public-impact assessments for all AI used in federal services. Schools, libraries, and community organizations should integrate AI literacy programs that teach Canadians how algorithms shape news feeds, job ads, and government services, empowering people to recognize manipulation and misuse. Businesses and AI Software/Systems, in turn, should adopt “trust seals” certifying compliance with privacy-by-design and anti-manipulation principles. Finally, linking transparency with tangible benefits—such as energy-efficient, secure, and inclusive AI applications—can show Canadians that responsible technology improves daily life. Trust will follow only when AI systems consistently prove they respect people’s autonomy, protect their data, and serve the public good.
column3:
Canada should adopt a risk-tiered AI regulatory framework similar to the EU’s AI Act, where the highest-risk AI systems are explicitly prohibited due to their intrinsic potential to violate privacy, manipulate citizens, or erode public trust. Systems capable of identifying individuals through biometric data—whether captured in real time or scraped from publicly available sources—should fall into this category, including tools like Clearview AI or PimeEyes, which operate without consent and undermine Canadians’ control over personal information. Under such a framework, high-risk AI would be illegal to deploy, sell, or allow access to Canadians’ data, while lower-risk systems would be subject to proportional regulatory requirements, such as mandatory audits, transparency reports, and human oversight.

Implementing a tiered risk approach ensures that regulation is targeted and enforceable: AI tools with severe privacy or manipulation risks are banned outright, while ethically and technically safer systems can still be innovated, tested, and deployed under clear standards. High-risk prohibitions should be complemented by mandatory impact assessments, independent audits, and robust enforcement to ensure compliance, including penalties for using banned systems or misclassifying AI risk. This model aligns Canadian AI governance with global best practices, reinforces public trust, protects Canadians’ personal information, and positions Canada as a leader in ethical, privacy-respecting AI development.
column4:
Canada can build trust in AI by proactively engaging citizens and businesses through transparency, education, and participatory governance. Public-facing strategies should explain how AI systems work, what data they use, and the safeguards in place to protect privacy and prevent manipulation. This could include plain-language model cards, interactive dashboards, and accessible reports on government and private-sector AI deployments. Inclusive engagement is essential: consultations should involve diverse communities, civil society, Indigenous groups, and small businesses to ensure AI policies reflect broad societal values and avoid reinforcing bias or exclusion. Public workshops, online portals, and co-design initiatives can give Canadians a meaningful voice in shaping AI governance while fostering literacy and informed confidence.

Leadership should be distributed across specialized actors. The federal government and regulatory agencies—such as the Office of the Privacy Commissioner—should lead enforcement, standard-setting, and public reporting. Academia and research institutes can provide neutral educational resources, ethics expertise, and independent audits. Industry associations and private firms should implement transparent practices and privacy-by-design systems while communicating their safeguards to clients and users. By coordinating these actors, Canada can balance transparency with innovation: companies can experiment within a predictable, rights-respecting framework, while citizens gain confidence that AI tools are ethical, accountable, and aligned with public interest. Clear communication, participatory consultation, and rigorous enforcement together form the backbone of a trustworthy AI ecosystem.

column2: The biggest change in trust in AI is through knowledge. Building AI literacy brings people from fearful, to over-enthusiastic when first experimenting, and finally to informed understanding of what AI can be useful for and where it is inappropriate. The biggest driver of mistrust in the first place is that AI is inherently unreliable due to the probabilistic engine underneath LLMs. It is critical to stop talking about AI using industry marketing hype and start getting down to the reality of it: current AI technology is a promising area, but it isn't going to be the end of work. It may be an important tool, but it is naive and has questionable value in many areas. To build trust, the public needs to understand the realities of its value and the Canadian governement needs to focus on human-centric messaging that communicates the value to society and individuals and not just focus on the ability of this technology to extract more capital value at the cost of workers.

There is also a need for an easier-to-understand framework for ethical compliance and privacy. column3:
Sustainability is a top priority for Canadians, and will shape the legitimacy of AI products in the public eye. To ensure that AI products align with anticipated regulatory trends worldwide, it is necessary to develop environmental and social standards now.

In Canada, a voluntary code for generative AI systems and a proposed AI and Data Act exist; however, public details reveal few concrete mechanisms addressing environmental protection. AI legislation must incorporate clear monitoring, reporting, and quantification of impacts. Measures to encourage disclosure could include standardizing how entities collect transparent and comparable data on the resource consumption of data centres. Mitigation efforts must also begin immediately, while efficiency improvements are underway—above all, powering AI with renewable energy. Finally, there are relatively simple policy approaches worth considering, like making it easier to opt out of automatic AI-assisted tasks (i.e: search engine results). Default opt-outs could, in theory, reduce energy and water use. We must also direct efforts toward AI technologies that justify precious resource use, rather than trivial applications. If AI is to remain a water- and energy-intensive technology, it is essential that it be prioritized for high-impact areas such as agriculture, healthcare, and emissions reductions. Ultimately, economic incentives and policy will determine this.

Finally, the government should implement standards in AI results to avoid replicating sexism, homophobia, transphobia, racism, and ableism. Hiring AI should be tested for discrepancies based on factors like race, and any bias found in testing results should have mechanisms for resolution. The Canadian government should consider adopting a risk-based framework for mitigating negative AI effects, similar to the EU AI Act. Such a framework would calibrate regulation to the degree of risk: the higher the risk, the more stringent the regulatory requirements become.
column4:
Canada's government can proactively build public trust in AI by positioning itself as a leader in responsible AI governance. This provides a model for accountability and consistency upon which the private sector can build, while fostering a culture of responsible innovation.

As part of this, Canada should meaningfully integrate youth into AI governance, as your ministry's present actions will transform our lives for decades to come. With organizations like Encode Canada, our generation demonstrates a strong commitment to ethical, inclusive, and socially beneficial AI. Many students and young professionals possess the expertise to make meaningful contributions that guide AI development priorities and advance responsible innovation.

Following ECC Canada and NRCan, ISED could establish a Youth Advisory Council. Alternatively, it could make the AI Advisory Council accessible to members under the age of 30, ensuring that students and early-career professionals with tangible experience and demonstrated interest have direct channels to inform federal decisions. This would increase age representation while connecting policymakers with the lived realities of young citizens. Engagement can also be advanced through funded access to knowledge exchange platforms, fellowships, and conferences that are often restricted to senior industry experts. 

Inclusive public engagement and literacy programs can further increase trust. Community-led AI hubs and education in schools can teach citizens about AI’s capabilities, limitations, and impacts, enabling informed participation in public discourse. Open-source tools that enable citizens to engage with AI decision-making can enhance transparency and innovation. Participatory approaches, such as a national AI Citizens’ Assembly, complement governance by ensuring that diverse perspectives inform policy development. This combination of top-down and bottom-up governance creates trust through accountability, education, and participation.

column2: Canada can build trust in AI by being transparent about how the technology works, where it is used, and how decisions are made. People lose confidence when AI feels hidden or when there is no clear accountability for errors. Every major AI deployment, especially in healthcare, finance, or government, should include clear public explanations, independent audits, and channels for recourse if something goes wrong. Education also plays a major role. Public literacy programs, workplace training, and media collaboration should help Canadians understand both the opportunities and limits of AI, creating informed confidence rather than fear. Trust grows when citizens see AI improve daily life while knowing strong safeguards exist to protect their privacy and rights.
column3: Canada needs clear national standards that make safety, fairness, and transparency mandatory for all high-impact AI systems. This means every AI product used in critical areas should undergo independent testing for bias, security, and explainability, with published results that allow public scrutiny. A consistent framework for certification, similar to those in healthcare or aviation, would give businesses clarity while protecting users. Regulations should focus on outcomes rather than rigid rules, ensuring companies can innovate responsibly within well-defined guardrails. The most urgent priority is setting standards for sectors like healthcare, employment, and public administration, where trust failures could directly harm people or undermine confidence in technology.
column4: Public engagement has to be practical, visible, and ongoing. Governments, universities, and companies should collaborate on open demonstrations, explainable dashboards, and local consultation programs that show how AI is applied in real decisions. Civil society and community groups should be active partners in monitoring, educating, and shaping norms so diverse voices are represented. Businesses must take responsibility for explaining their tools in plain language, not just compliance terms. Government can lead by example through transparent AI use in public services and by funding public education campaigns that show both the benefits and boundaries of responsible AI. Trust will come from consistency, participation, and proof that Canadians remain in control of the technology shaping their future.

column2:
Public trust in AI will not come from communication campaigns or declarations of ethics—it must be earned through evidence, transparency, and education.

First, Canada needs to treat testing and evaluation as essential public infrastructure. Independent, standards-based evaluation of AI systems—especially those involving biometrics, digital identity, or automated decision-making—should be built into deployment from the start. Transparent results, aligned with ISO/IEC standards, give both citizens and businesses confidence that systems are safe, accurate, fair, and accountable.

Second, education must be elevated as a national pillar alongside standards and testing. Canadians at every level—citizens, policymakers, executives—need practical AI literacy to understand both capabilities and limits. Building this understanding demystifies AI and helps people distinguish between responsible innovation and reckless use.

Finally, government must model transparency by openly sharing how AI is evaluated, governed, and corrected when errors occur.

Public trust is built through standards, testing, and education, working together to ensure that AI in Canada is not only innovative, but demonstrably safe, fair, and worthy of confidence.
column3:
Canada must move beyond outdated instruments like the Directive on Automated Decision-Making and the Policy on Digital. These were early steps, but they are no longer sufficient to govern complex, adaptive AI systems. A modern framework should combine updated privacy legislation—comparable in scope and enforcement to the EU’s GDPR—with mandatory testing, evaluation, and transparency requirements for any AI used in public services or high-impact domains.

The foundation for such governance already exists in international standards. ISO/IEC frameworks covering AI quality, safety, risk, bias, and biometric assurance provide the technical vocabulary, metrics, and test methods needed to demonstrate trustworthiness. Canada should integrate these standards into regulation and procurement, and ensure government experts actively participate in their continued development.

Equally important is a commitment to fact-based transparency. In the biometrics field, public debate often relies on selective or outdated research—such as the 2019 MIT study on gender-estimation bias—used to generalize unfairly to unrelated biometric capabilities. Independent, standards-based testing consistently shows a more nuanced reality.

Trust will not come from rhetoric but from verifiable evidence, clear privacy guardrails, and the courage to communicate results openly—even when complex. Canada must pair its social progressiveness with scientific discipline and international leadership in standards and oversight.
column4:
Public trust will not come from messaging campaigns—it will come from education that empowers understanding. Too often, communication about AI “dumbs things down,” stripping away meaning and nuance to manage perception rather than build knowledge. Canada should take the opposite approach: smarten the population up.

We must equip citizens, businesses, and public servants to be informed participants in the AI revolution, capable of questioning, challenging, and responsibly adopting new technologies. This means embedding AI and digital literacy into curricula, professional training, and civic education, alongside practical understanding of privacy, data, and algorithmic accountability.

Government should lead by example, working with academia and standards organizations to make testing and evaluation results publicly accessible and interpretable, showing not just what AI can do but how it is governed and verified.

Trust grows when people understand—not when they are simply reassured. Canada can distinguish itself by focusing on education over communication, fostering a society of competent digital citizens who can thrive responsibly in an increasingly technical world, from AI to Web 3 and beyond.

column2:
Canada’s Challenge
Public trust will depend on whether AI systems are verifiable, explainable, and privacy-preserving by design — not on regulation alone. The most important thing is to make AI systems provably trustworthy through technical transparency and data sovereignty, not just policy declarations.
T-RIZE Perspective
At T-RIZE, trust is engineered at the infrastructure level.
Privacy-preserving AI: our federated learning architecture lets institutions train and deploy models collectively without ever sharing raw data.
Verifiable pipelines: every contribution, model update, and output can be audited and traced, allowing institutions to demonstrate accountability.
Collaborative assurance: through the T-RIZE Labs × ÉTS Chair, we’re developing frameworks that combine secure communication, decentralized learning, and explainability, giving Canada exportable standards for trustworthy AI infrastructure.
Practical focus: our work targets institutional confidence — building systems that regulators, financial partners, and public agencies can verify, rather than consumer awareness campaigns or high-level ethics charters.
column3:
Across Canada
Trustworthy AI depends on consistent technical standards, not just oversight.
Canada’s framework should ensure that compliance is verifiable at the system level, so organizations can prove how data and models are managed.
T-RIZE Perspective
T-RIZE operates where technical assurance meets regulation.
Our work supports frameworks that make trust measurable and interoperable:
Federated and decentralized learning standards that define how institutions can collaborate without data transfer;
Provenance and audit frameworks that make model behavior traceable across its lifecycle;
Interoperability alignment with global AI assurance standards (e.g., NIST, ISO/IEC) so Canadian systems remain compatible internationally.
T-RIZE’s contribution is architectural, not legislative: we build the infrastructure that allows any future Canadian or international regulatory framework to be technically enforced, verified, and trusted at scale.
column4:
Across Canada
Make the value exchange clear: plain-language disclosures on what data is collected, why, retention/sharing, and opt-out/portability.
Prefer verifiable pipelines over promises: publish data/model provenance, change logs, and incident reports with remediation.
Enable collaboration without data sharing: back privacy-preserving collaboration (e.g., federated learning) so firms can build shared intelligence while keeping data local.


T-RIZE Perspective
Trust by architecture: Rizemind lets institutions co-train and refine models without sharing raw data, preserving sovereignty and consent boundaries.
Accountability built in: participation and model updates are auditable and reviewable, supporting regulators and partners.
Scope: T-RIZE equips Canadian institutions with privacy-preserving, verifiable rails; broader public campaigns are outside our mandate.

column2:
Trust begins with transparency and accountability. People need to understand how AI systems make decisions, who is responsible for them, and what happens when things go wrong. Canada can lead by requiring clear disclosure when AI is used in public services, media, or critical decisions, and by supporting independent evaluation of these systems for bias, safety, and security.

Education is key. Public literacy programs that explain AI in plain language would help Canadians separate fact from hype and feel more in control of the technology around them. The same applies to business leaders, many of whom want to use AI but lack trusted guidance on how to do it safely.

We should also focus on consent and control. If people know how their data is used and can easily opt out, they are more likely to support innovation. Canada’s reputation for fairness gives us an advantage. Building AI that reflects those values can make our systems not only safer but more globally trusted.
column3:
Canada should adopt a balanced framework that supports innovation while protecting citizens. This means aligning with international safety and ethics standards but enforcing them through Canadian oversight. Every AI product used in health care, finance, education, or media should meet clear baseline requirements for data privacy, model transparency, and robustness testing.

We need a consistent process for auditing high-impact systems, including third-party verification and public reporting. Developers should have access to a shared set of safety evaluation tools so compliance is achievable for both large and small companies. Standardized reporting templates could make it easy to compare systems on fairness, security, and reliability.

Ethical AI should not depend on the goodwill of individual companies. It should be a normal expectation built into design, procurement, and regulation. When Canadians know that every AI system is held to the same standards of care, trust follows naturally.
column4:
Public trust grows when people feel involved, not managed. Canada should create open forums where citizens, researchers, and businesses can review how AI is being used and help shape the policies that govern it. A national AI observatory or citizen advisory panel could give ongoing feedback to government and industry on emerging risks and opportunities.

Businesses should also be part of the conversation. Many are experimenting with AI but lack guidance on responsible deployment. Practical toolkits, open courses, and recognition programs for safe AI adoption could help them lead by example.

Communication matters. Canada should use public broadcasters, schools, and community networks to show real examples of AI improving daily life, while also explaining how safeguards work. The best way to build trust is to show that transparency and innovation can coexist. If Canadians see that AI serves their interests and values, they will support it with confidence.

column3:
As a starting place, we need to stop assuming AI is always a good thing. Many applications of AI are inappropriate for the purpose, and the rush to apply AI to every sector and possible application is guaranteed to create huge, expensive problems. AI is appropriate for some use cases, but they need to be thoughtfully and carefully executed, ensuring that AI is actually the best tool for the job. 

As a consumer, I would also firmly like the opportunity to opt out of AI tools (and their added costs!) as much as possible. I don't want AI in my software applications, particularly generative AI -- I am quite fine with thinking for myself, thank you very much. 

I also have huge concerns about the environmental and privacy implications of AI use -- I would need to know how those concerns were being meaningfully addressed in law (with strict penalties to ensure compliance) in order to have any confidence in using AI tools and technologies. Any of my data that is being used by an AI product should have to be stored in Canada, as much of Canadians' data is currently stored in the US, regardless of the level of sensitivity.

We also need to be sure that firm copyright protections are in place to ensure compensation for creators whenever their works are used in training models, and to also ensure that that training only takes place with the consent of the copyright holder.

column2:
Government adoption of reliable AI, as well as an appropriate regulatory framework for private AI would improve trust in the technology.
A major concern is that the existence of deep fakes may undermine public trust in all media. Having reliable sources of video clips preserved on a public repository may help this. For example, broadcast media and politicians may ask for specific videos to be hosted on a government-run website. A user could then ask the website to confirm whether a particular video clip is part of the longer video, or was artificially created.
Another related risk to trust Is the risk that AI will be used to greatly increase the number of scam calls, texts and emails. As such scams proliferate, trust will be lost in government and in the ways we communicate with each other. Although work can be done to help Canadians identify these scams, it is possible that their number and sophistication will become too great for this to be a realistic strategy. Efforts should be made to ensure that no one is able to spoof telephone numbers, so that, if I receive a phone call or text message purporting to be from a given institution, person or phone number, I can be entirely certain that is the case.
It should also be possible for institutions to register their email addresses so that, once again, a person can be entirely certain that an email purporting to come from an institution comes from that institution.
column3:
There needs to be a balance between the need to provide trust and the need to create scope for innovation. Many uses of AI present a risk of only limited harm. In those cases, it may be that the only requirement is for users to be aware of the possible harm and accept the risk with boilerplate text.
In other cases, there may be particular and substantial risks, such as the disclosure of confidential information, or reliance on AI to make a decision about a particular person (for example, in an insurance context). Regulations should create a number of different regulatory regimes, depending on the nature of the risk posed. An AI system can still operate if it presents the user with a risk and does not respect the regulatory framework associated with that risk, but only if the user very clearly consents to the risk.  In any case, a person affected by an AI decision should be able to easily appeal the decision to a human who does not rely on the AI decision, or, if the appeal is to a human who presumes the AI decision is correct, be provided with a genuine explanation of the reasons the AI rendered the decision.

column2: To build public trust in AI technologies, Canada must prioritize transparency, accountability, and ethical design. The risks posed by AI, such as bias, privacy violations, and job displacement, contribute to public and business mistrust. Clear communication about how AI systems work and how they are regulated is essential. Public education campaigns, integrated into school curricula and professional development, can improve AI literacy, helping citizens and businesses understand AI’s benefits and limitations. Trust can also be bolstered by involving diverse communities in discussions about AI, ensuring that a broad range of perspectives are considered in its design and implementation. Ensuring AI tools are transparent, explainable, and responsive to feedback from users is critical for fostering informed confidence in AI technologies.
column3: To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, governance mechanisms need to be established at both the federal and provincial levels. Clear standards for AI development should be set, with rigorous frameworks that include audits for fairness, security, and privacy. These standards should be updated regularly to keep pace with technological advances. Regulatory bodies can oversee AI product integrity, ensuring that AI solutions comply with ethical guidelines, especially in areas like healthcare, finance, and criminal justice where trust issues are most acute. The government should also support industry-led certification programs for AI products, offering companies a clear path to demonstrate their commitment to ethical AI deployment.
column4: Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses by creating public-facing strategies that demystify AI systems and explain their potential impact. This includes government-backed educational campaigns that focus on the responsible use of AI and transparent governance practices. Ensuring that AI policies are inclusive, engaging marginalized communities, industry leaders, and academic experts, will help build widespread trust. Balancing transparency with innovation is key: regulations should provide clear guidance without stifling AI development. Government bodies, alongside industry associations and academic institutions, are best placed to lead these efforts, fostering trust through collaboration and open dialogue on the societal implications of AI technologies.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by demonstrating that AI is safe, ethical, transparent, and designed to benefit society, while proactively addressing its risks. Trust isn’t automatic — it requires visible actions, accountability, and meaningful engagement with citizens.

1. Transparent governance and ethical frameworks. Clear policies on data privacy, bias mitigation, accountability, and explainability signal that AI is regulated responsibly. Public-facing guidelines and independent audits can show that Canada prioritizes ethical AI deployment.

2. Public engagement and education. People trust what they understand. Canada should invest in accessible education campaigns, workshops, and interactive tools to explain AI’s benefits, limitations, and safeguards. Storytelling and case studies that show AI solving real-world problems make the technology tangible and relatable.

3. Accountability and oversight. Independent review boards, impact assessments, and mandatory reporting on AI use in sensitive sectors (healthcare, justice, finance) reassure the public that AI systems are monitored and risks are mitigated.

4. Responsible adoption by businesses and government. Encouraging AI companies and public institutions to adopt ethical standards, transparency reports, and public testing builds confidence. Incentives for responsible practices reinforce a culture where trustworthiness is valued alongside innovation.

5. Demonstrate social benefit. Highlighting AI applications that improve quality of life — in healthcare, climate solutions, accessibility, and public services — shows that technology is designed to serve people, not just profit.

As Directoni, CEO of Mexframe, I know that storytelling is a force multiplier for trust. Visual narratives, cinematic demos, and relatable case studies make AI tangible, explain risk mitigation clearly, and show impact in everyday life. Communicating openly transforms abstract concerns into confidence.
column3:
To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, a comprehensive, multi-layered framework is needed, combining standards, regulations, and cultural norms that address technical, ethical, and societal dimensions. Trustworthy AI requires both hard rules and shared expectations.

1. Ethical and technical standards. Canada should adopt clear principles around fairness, transparency, privacy, accountability, and explainability. Standards should define measurable benchmarks for bias mitigation, data governance, security, and robustness, ensuring AI systems behave predictably and equitably.

2. Regulatory oversight. Independent agencies should enforce compliance with ethical, safety, and privacy rules. Mandatory risk assessments for high-impact AI applications (healthcare, finance, justice, critical infrastructure) ensure potential harms are identified, mitigated, and reported. Regulators should also facilitate audits and certifications to validate AI systems before deployment.

3. Data governance frameworks. Clear rules on data collection, storage, sharing, and consent are essential. Policies must balance innovation with privacy protection, ensuring AI products are trained on ethical, representative datasets and respect Canadian data sovereignty.

4. Transparency and explainability norms. Users must understand how AI makes decisions. Standards for interpretability, reporting, and documentation enable accountability and allow citizens, businesses, and regulators to scrutinize AI outputs effectively. Technologies like blockchain could help us in this area. 

5. Cross-sector collaboration and cultural norms. Industry, academia, and government should develop shared expectations for responsible AI practices. Incentives for voluntary adherence to best practices, open reporting, and independent auditing create a culture of ethical deployment.

6. Public engagement and education. Trustworthy AI is socially anchored.
column4:
Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses to promote responsible AI use and trust by combining transparency, education, collaboration, and accountability, creating a system where stakeholders understand, influence, and benefit from AI. Engagement must be structured, inclusive, and continuous, with clear leadership across sectors.

1. Public education and awareness. Citizens must understand AI’s capabilities, limitations, and safeguards. Government agencies, universities, and NGOs can lead campaigns, workshops, and online resources to demystify AI, explain ethical standards, and show practical societal benefits. Storytelling, case studies, and interactive demos make complex systems tangible.

2. Business engagement and incentives. Companies adopting AI should be encouraged to follow ethical standards through tax incentives, certifications, and public recognition programs. Industry associations and innovation hubs are best positioned to lead these efforts, sharing best practices, auditing guidelines, and technical resources.

3. Participatory governance mechanisms. Citizens should have avenues to provide feedback on AI deployment in sensitive areas such as healthcare, justice, and finance. Public consultations, advisory boards, and digital platforms for feedback can give communities a voice, fostering legitimacy and accountability. Governments and independent regulatory bodies are best placed to coordinate these mechanisms.

4. Cross-sector collaborations. Academia, industry, and government should jointly develop standards, research, and pilot projects that demonstrate responsible AI in practice. Collaborative labs and public-private partnerships ensure ethical frameworks are tested and refined in real-world scenarios.

5. Transparency and reporting. Clear, accessible disclosures on AI use, audits, and impact assessments build confidence.

Le rapport Bourgogne (CIRANO, 2025) recommande deux mesures urgentes :
Conclure un accord FPT sur l’interopérabilité des données ;
 Créer un Conseil FPT permanent sur l’IA et l’interopérabilité.
Ces initiatives ne sont pas de simples ajustements techniques : elles sont stratégiques et structurantes pour tout projet de construction nationale.
L’accord-cadre proposé sur l’interopérabilité s’aligne directement sur les critères de désignation d’intérêt national de la partie 2 de la législation  C-5, Loi sur la construction du Canada, en renforçant l’autonomie, la résilience et la sécurité du pays.
Il soutient le partage d’infrastructures cyberrésilientes, la coordination des capacités d’intervention d’urgence, et crée des conditions économiques propices à la productivité et à l’innovation responsable en IA.
column4:
Des plateformes participatives et des forums de dialogue citoyen doivent compléter les consultations institutionnelles. Une communication proactive, transparente et décentralisée renforcera la légitimité des politiques d’IA.
Le leadership en matière de confiance doit être collectif : le gouvernement fédéral assure la coordination, les provinces et territoires l’ancrage local, et la société civile la vigilance éthique. Un Conseil FPT sur l’IA et la gouvernance numérique, tel que proposé par le rapport Bourgogne, incarnerait ce leadership partagé.

column2: Aucune solution technologique ne sera aussi efficace que la formation des canadiens au numérique par l'apprentissage tout au long de la vie. column3:
Consentement explicite obligatoire : Interdire l'utilisation d'œuvres canadiennes pour l'entraînement de modèles d'IA sans consentement éclairé et écrit des titulaires de droits. Inversement le fardeau de la preuve : les entreprises d'IA doivent démontrer qu'elles ont obtenu les permissions, pas aux créateurs de prouver le vol.
Transparence des corpus d'entraînement : Exiger la divulgation complète des données utilisées pour entraîner tout modèle d'IA commercialisé au Canada. Les créateurs doivent pouvoir vérifier si leurs œuvres ont été utilisées.
Compensation équitable obligatoire : Établir un système de licences collectives avec barèmes sectoriels pour rémunérer les créateurs dont les œuvres alimentent les modèles d'IA. Créer un registre d'empreintes (C2PA/Content Credentials) permettant la traçabilité et l'attribution.
Droit de retrait effectif : Permettre aux créateurs de retirer leurs œuvres des corpus d'entraînement existants, avec obligation pour les entreprises d'IA de ré-entraîner leurs modèles ou de démontrer que les œuvres retirées n'influencent plus les générations.
Fiducies de données culturelles : Créer des infrastructures légales où les corpus de données canadiennes (archives, catalogues, métadonnées) sont protégés, gouvernés par les communautés et utilisés selon nos propres règles, avec redistribution des revenus.
Autres cadres pour l'IA responsable :
Approche "light, tight and right" : Réglementation proportionnelle au risque, avec obligations renforcées pour les modèles à large empreinte (évaluation des risques, red teaming, auditabilité).
Standards d'auditabilité : Exiger que les systèmes d'IA déployés au Canada soient auditables par des tiers indépendants, particulièrement pour détecter les biais discriminatoires.
Protection des données sensibles : Encadrer strictement l'utilisation de données personnelles, culturelles et autochtones, avec protocoles de consentement respectant les pratiques communautaires.
column4:
La confiance ne se décrète pas, elle se construit par le contrôle
La vraie confiance envers l'IA ne viendra pas de campagnes de sensibilisation ou de codes d'éthique volontaires, mais du contrôle effectif que les citoyens et communautés exercent sur leurs données et systèmes. Le Canada doit investir dans des infrastructures administrées en communs numériques par les communautés elles-mêmes.
Donner le contrôle plutôt que promettre la confiance :
Fiducies de données sectorielles : Infrastructures où les données culturelles, de santé, éducatives sont gérées par les secteurs eux-mêmes. Les règles d'accès et d'utilisation sont décidées démocratiquement par les communautés concernées.
Infrastructures de calcul en communs : Centres de données publics accessibles aux citoyens, OBNL, coopératives selon des principes de gouvernance partagée.
Laboratoires territoriaux ouverts : Espaces régionaux où citoyens et organisations expérimentent l'IA avec accès aux expertises et outils, favorisant l'appropriation collective.
Outils open source gouvernés collectivement : Alternatives ouvertes aux solutions propriétaires, avec gouvernance démocratique impliquant utilisateurs et communautés affectées.
Qui dirige les efforts de confiance?
Les communautés elles-mêmes, via une gouvernance multi-parties prenantes incluant citoyens, praticiens, chercheurs et représentants des groupes marginalisés (francophones, autochtones, minorités). Les secteurs à mission (culture, santé, éducation) sont les mieux placés car guidés par l'intérêt public.
Mobilisation proactive :
Littératie critique : Former les citoyens à comprendre les mécanismes de l'IA et participer aux décisions sur son développement.
Financement de coalitions sectorielles : Soutenir les regroupements pour développer leurs positions et outils partagés.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by making transparency, education, and accountability the foundation of its national strategy. People lose confidence when they feel AI is being developed behind closed doors or used to exploit their data — so the solution is openness. We need sovereign, publicly governed data systems where citizens know how information is collected, protected, and used. Every major AI deployment — in healthcare, finance, or government — should include clear, human-readable explanations of how it works and who is accountable when things go wrong.

Education is equally critical. AI literacy must extend beyond universities into schools, workplaces, and communities, helping Canadians understand that AI is not a mysterious threat but a tool we can shape together. Programs should focus on critical thinking, ethical awareness, and hands-on exposure, so people learn by engaging, not just observing.

Finally, trust grows when benefits are shared. Supporting local, ethical AI startups, funding public-interest projects, and showcasing transparent Canadian success stories will prove that innovation and integrity can coexist. By embedding openness, fairness, and sovereignty into every layer of our AI ecosystem, Canada can lead the world in building trustworthy technology that serves people, not the other way around.
column3: Canada needs a people-first AI framework rooted in sovereignty, transparency, and accountability. Building on BC + AI principles, we should establish publicly governed standards for data, compute, and ethics — ensuring AI systems serve communities, not corporations. Regulation should require transparent model documentation, independent audits, and public oversight for high-impact systems. A national AI Ethics and Safety Framework should align with open science values: models must be explainable, datasets traceable, and results verifiable. Canada should also adopt sovereign data governance, keeping critical datasets under public stewardship to prevent corporate capture or foreign dependency. Finally, norms around ecological accountability and inclusivity — measuring energy impact and ensuring diverse representation in model design — must be embedded from the start. Trustworthy AI isn’t just safe; it’s open, regenerative, and governed for the public good.
column4:
Canada can promote responsible AI use by treating trust as a shared civic project, not a PR exercise. The best way to engage citizens and businesses is through open participation — creating visible, local forums where people can see, question, and shape how AI affects their lives. Inspired by BC + AI principles, Canada should fund regional AI commons — community-driven spaces that connect researchers, policymakers, artists, and entrepreneurs to explore ethical applications together.

Public communication should be transparent and human — explaining AI systems in plain language, showing both benefits and limits, and inviting collaboration instead of compliance. Government can set broad ethical standards and ensure accountability, but community organizations, educators, and startups are best placed to lead day-to-day trust building through openness, literacy, and real-world impact.

Trust grows when people feel ownership. By making AI something Canadians can participate in, not just be governed by, we create a culture of responsible innovation that’s rooted in inclusion, transparency, and shared benefit.

column2:
To build public trust in AI technologies while addressing their risks, Canada should focus on the following priorities:

1. Transparent and Responsible AI Practices:

Ensure AI systems are developed and deployed with clear ethical guidelines, transparency in decision-making, and explainability of outcomes.
Promote responsible use by establishing and enforcing standards for data privacy, user confidentiality, and ethical decision-making.
2. Proactive Risk Management:

Identify and mitigate risks such as bias, misuse, and unintended consequences through robust governance, regular audits, and accountability mechanisms.
Communicate openly about risks and the steps being taken to address them.
3. Education and AI Literacy:

Invest in public education campaigns and accessible resources to improve understanding of AI technologies, their benefits, and limitations.
Provide targeted training for businesses and professionals to foster informed adoption and responsible use.
4. Public Engagement and Dialogue:

Involve diverse stakeholders—including the public, industry, and academia—in shaping AI policies and standards.
Create forums for ongoing dialogue to address concerns, gather feedback, and adapt approaches as technologies evolve.
5. Championing Canadian AI Success Stories:

Highlight examples of Canadian AI solutions that deliver positive social and economic impact, reinforcing trust and national pride.
By prioritizing transparency, risk management, education, engagement, and positive storytelling, Canada can build lasting public confidence in AI while responsibly managing its risks.
column3:
To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, the following frameworks, standards, regulations, and norms are needed:

1. Robust AI Governance Mechanisms:

Establish clear oversight structures for AI development and deployment, including independent review boards and regular audits.
Implement accountability frameworks that define roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths for addressing issues.
2. Product Integrity and Ethical Compliance Standards:

Require adherence to ethical guidelines covering data privacy, user confidentiality, transparency, and explainability.
Mandate validation and testing protocols to ensure AI systems perform as intended and do not introduce bias or harm.
3. Regulatory Requirements:

Develop and enforce regulations for high-risk AI applications, focusing on areas such as healthcare, finance, and public safety where trust issues are most acute.
Require documentation and traceability for AI decision-making processes and outcomes.
4. Industry Norms and Best Practices:

Promote adoption of industry-wide standards for responsible AI, including codes of conduct and certification schemes.
Encourage continuous improvement and knowledge sharing across the AI ecosystem.
5. Priority Areas for Trust:

Focus on transparency, fairness, and accountability in sectors with significant societal impact.
Address public concerns proactively through clear communication, stakeholder engagement, and visible enforcement of standards.
By implementing these frameworks and standards, Canada can foster a trustworthy AI environment that protects users, ensures ethical compliance, and builds public confidence.
column4:
Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses to promote responsible AI use and trust in its governance through the following strategies:

1. Public-Facing Education and Communication:

Launch accessible campaigns to explain how AI systems work, their benefits, and their limitations.
Provide clear, jargon-free resources and real-world examples to demystify AI for the public and business leaders.
2. Inclusive and Participatory Trust-Building:

Involve diverse stakeholders—including citizens, businesses, academia, and civil society—in policy development and oversight.
Create open forums, town halls, and digital platforms for feedback, questions, and collaborative problem-solving.
3. Transparent Governance with Room for Innovation:

Share information about AI governance processes, standards, and safeguards while protecting proprietary innovation.
Regularly publish reports on AI system performance, risks, and mitigation actions to maintain accountability.
Leadership Roles:

Government: Lead on regulation, public education, and setting national standards for responsible AI.
Industry: Drive innovation, adopt and demonstrate best practices, and communicate transparently about AI products and safeguards.
Academia and Civil Society: Provide independent research, critical analysis, and act as a bridge between technical experts and the public.
By combining clear communication, inclusive engagement, and shared leadership, Canada can build a culture of responsible AI use and strengthen trust in its governance.
