column2:
Building public trust in AI while managing risks requires transparency, clear rules, education, and demonstrating benefits.

Understanding Risks & Mistrust: Key risks driving mistrust include:

Bias & Discrimination: AI amplifying unfairness.

Privacy Violations: Concerns over data use and security.

Job Displacement: Fear of automation replacing human jobs.

Lack of Transparency: Difficulty understanding AI decision-making ("black box").

Misinformation: AI generating convincing fake content (deepfakes).

Safety & Security: Concerns about errors in autonomous systems or malicious use.

Most Important Actions to Build Confidence:

Establish Clear Governance: Finalize and implement strong AI laws (like AIDA/Bill C-27) defining responsibilities, setting safety standards (especially for high-impact systems), mandating risk assessments, and establishing accountability. Clear rules signal seriousness about risks.

Promote Transparency & Explainability: Require organizations (especially government) to be open about when and how AI is used. Mandate understandable explanations for AI-driven decisions affecting individuals. Support explainable AI (XAI) research.

Invest in Public AI Literacy: Fund national AI education programs for all Canadians, teaching critical concepts, capabilities, limitations, risks, data rights, and how to identify AI-generated fakes. Informed confidence reduces fear.

Ensure Human Oversight & Risk Mitigation: Mandate human-in-the-loop for critical decisions. Require comprehensive risk management frameworks to proactively identify and mitigate harms before AI deployment.

Foster Public Dialogue & Inclusion: Create ongoing public consultations and ensure diverse voices (including marginalized groups) help shape AI policies and applications, aligning development with Canadian values.

Combining strong governance, transparency, education, human accountability, and inclusive dialogue is crucial for building the public confidence needed to adopt AI
column3:
To ensure AI products in Canada are safe and reliable, we need a mix of clear rules, good practices, oversight, and public understanding.

1. Clear Laws & Rules:

Finish the AI Law (AIDA/Bill C-27): This main law needs to be clear about who is responsible for AI, especially for high-risk uses (like in health or hiring).

Sector-Specific Rules: Add specific rules for critical areas like healthcare or self-driving cars.

Liability: Make it clear who pays if an AI causes harm.

Protect Data: Strong, enforced privacy laws are essential.

2. Standards & Check-ups:

Technical Standards: Create agreed-upon rules for how AI should be built and tested (covering data quality, bias, security, and how decisions are explained).

Independent Checks: Set up ways for experts (auditors) to check if important AI systems meet the standards and laws, like safety checks for other products.

Risk Assessments: Require companies to check for potential problems (like bias or safety issues) before launching high-impact AI.

3. Oversight & Governance:

AI Watchdog: Have a dedicated body (like the proposed AI and Data Commissioner) with real power to monitor and enforce the rules.

Company Rules: Require businesses using risky AI to have their own internal rules, training, and plans for when things go wrong.

Report Problems: Make it mandatory to report serious AI failures or harms.

Safety Research: Support the new AI Safety Institute to figure out best practices.

4. Culture & Awareness:

Promote Ethics: Encourage AI creators to build ethically, not just follow the minimum rules.

AI Literacy: Help everyone understand AI basics – what it can and can't do, and its risks.

Keep Talking: Maintain open conversations between the public, government, and industry as AI evolves.

Priority Areas: Focus these efforts first on high-stakes areas like healthcare, finance, construction, teachers, law enforcement, employment, and critical infrastructure.
column4:
Here's a concise guide (under 2000 characters) on building AI trust in Canada through engagement:

How to Engage Citizens & Businesses:

AI Literacy: Run national campaigns explaining AI basics, risks (bias, fakes), and rights in plain language. Use diverse channels (schools, online, communities).

Transparency: Government and businesses must clearly state when and how they use AI, especially for impactful decisions. Explain data use and safeguards simply.

Public Dialogue: Hold regular, open consultations on AI policies and deployments. Actively seek diverse views to ensure rules reflect Canadian values.

Showcase Benefits: Highlight real examples of AI used responsibly and effectively for public good (e.g., health, services) while detailing safety measures.

Clear Standards: Develop accessible standards for responsible AI. Consider voluntary certifications for trustworthy AI products.

Who Leads What:

Federal Gov: Sets laws (AIDA), national standards, funds literacy, ensures responsible government use, leads national dialogue & oversight (AI Commissioner, Safety Institute).

Provinces: Integrate AI literacy into education, regulate specific sectors (health), support regional SMEs.

Industry: Implements ethical practices, maintains transparency with users, invests in workforce training, helps create standards.

Academia: Conducts research (ethical/safe AI), creates educational content, trains talent, provides expert advice.

Civil Society: Advocates for public interest/rights, offers independent critiques, runs community programs, ensures accountability.

column2: UBI

The first step the federal government can take to build public trust in AI technologies is by ensuring that the rights of workers and citizens are addressed, listened to, and that all legislation and regulations regarding AI will prioritize the rights and needs of workers above those of big businesses. By ensuring that AI is developed and deployed in a manner that does not harm the livelihoods, legal rights, and intellectual property of Canadian citizens, faith in the technology's potential to benefit the workforce will increase tenfold.
column3:
AI companies should only be allowed to legally train on data that is either not protected by copyright (i.e. in the Public Domain), is licensed for training purposes by the intellectual property owners, or when given explicit permission by a customer to do so. Due to how difficult, if not practically impossible, it is to remove material that was unethically used for AI training to be removed from generative AI models after the training process, opt-in is the only reasonable solution to ensure that all AI training data is obtained in an ethical manner.
Establishing strong personality rights to protect living peoples' likenesses from being abused in AI generated content, such as "deepfake" videos and images, will be a necessary step forward to prevent the spread of AI-assisted videos and images that could defame people and put their reputations at great risk. On that note, another important step to ensure that video and photo evidence can remain verifiable is to create regulations that require all digital images and photos to have watermarks, metadata, and other encrypted methods that can be used to flag if any information has been tampered with by a generative AI program in any way.
column4: Canada needs to actively engage with workers across all industries affected by AI, such as those in the media, the arts, or in software design. Many workers in these industries feel like governments and the business world have ignored their input on generative AI deployment in spite of strong union efforts and grassroots pro-worker campaigns. Prominent leaders from all industries should be given an input on the future of AI, as it is a technology that has great potential, but can also easily cause vast unemployment and wealth inequality if it is left unregulated. Listening only to AI industry leaders will only lead to AI that benefits them, and since the general public has become very distrustful of CEOs of large AI companies, we need leaders from other industries to help bring balance and nuance to the discussion.

Canada can build public trust in AI by addressing risks, transparency, and literacy across society:

1. Address AI Risks Directly

Safety & reliability: Ensure AI systems are tested for accuracy, robustness, and bias mitigation.

Privacy & security: Enforce strong data protection and responsible data handling practices.

Accountability: Clearly define who is responsible for AI decisions, including liability frameworks.

2. Transparency & Governance

Explainability: Encourage AI models that can be interpreted by users and regulators.

Standards & certification: Publicly certify AI products meeting ethical, safety, and fairness criteria.

Independent oversight: National boards or ethics councils to review high-impact AI deployments.

3. Education & Literacy

Public awareness campaigns: Explain AI capabilities, limitations, and safeguards in plain language.

Business training programs: Help organizations understand safe AI adoption and compliance.

K-12 and post-secondary curriculum: Include AI literacy, ethics, and responsible use.

4. Engagement & Feedback

Participatory design: Involve citizens and communities in AI deployment decisions.

Open consultation: Collect public and industry input on new AI policies and products.
column3:
Canada can ensure trustworthy and responsible AI deployment through multi-layered governance, standards, and oversight:

1. Governance & Oversight

Independent AI oversight bodies: National or multi-institution boards to review high-impact AI systems.

Regulatory sandboxes: Allow controlled testing of AI products in healthcare, finance, defence, and critical infrastructure.

Clear liability frameworks: Define responsibility for AI errors or harms across developers, deployers, and users.

2. Standards & Certification

Ethical and safety standards: Align with OECD AI Principles, ISO/IEC AI standards, and international best practices.

Transparency & explainability metrics: Require models to provide interpretable outputs for critical decisions.

Audit and reporting mechanisms: Mandate third-party verification for AI products in high-risk sectors.

3. Product Integrity & Ethical Compliance

Bias and fairness testing: Systematic evaluation for discrimination or unintended harms.

Data governance: Secure, privacy-preserving access to training and operational datasets.

Lifecycle monitoring: Continuous evaluation of deployed AI for safety, effectiveness, and compliance.

4. Priority Areas for Trust

Health care & life sciences: Diagnostic tools, clinical decision support.

Finance: Fraud detection, credit scoring, and automated trading.

Defence & critical infrastructure: Surveillance, security, autonomous systems.

Public services: AI in justice, social benefits, and civic decision-making.
column4:
Canada can build public and business trust in AI through proactive engagement, education, and inclusive governance:

1. Public-Facing Strategies

Clear communication: Explain AI capabilities, limitations, and safeguards in simple, accessible language.

Transparency portals: Public dashboards showing AI use in government services, key metrics, and audit results.

Case studies & success stories: Highlight ethical, safe, and beneficial AI deployments to build confidence.

2. Inclusive Trust-Building Approaches

Participatory design: Include citizens, Indigenous communities, and industry in AI policy and product design.

Consultations & forums: Town halls, advisory councils, and online platforms for feedback on AI initiatives.

Sector-specific engagement: Tailor efforts for high-impact areas like healthcare, finance, and public services.

3. Balancing Transparency with Innovation

Sandbox environments: Allow safe experimentation while providing public oversight.

Tiered disclosure: Share information on AI systems’ functioning and risk levels without compromising IP or security.

Independent audits: Third-party reviews ensure accountability while allowing firms to innovate.

4. Leadership Roles

Federal government: Sets standards, regulates high-risk AI, and funds public engagement campaigns.

Academia & research institutes: Lead educational efforts, literacy programs, and ethical frameworks.

Industry: Demonstrates responsible deployment, shares best practices, and communicates benefits and safeguards.

Civil society & professional bodies: Advocate for citizen interests, monitor compliance, and facilitate dialogue.

Ensure AI deployed in Canada is safe, ethical, fair, transparent, and accountable, while fostering innovation and public confidence.
1. National AI Governance Framework - Risk-based classification of AI systems (high, medium, low risk).
Clear accountability, liability, and human oversight requirements.
Alignment with international standards (OECD AI Principles, ISO/IEC 42001).
2. Sector-Specific Standards
Health: Clinical validation, bias testing, explainable outcomes.
Finance: Transparency, audit trails, regulatory reporting.
Transportation & Mobility: Safety, human-in-the-loop controls.
Justice & Public Safety: Bias audits, transparency, regular review.
3. Certification & Technical Standards
“Canadian Trustworthy AI Label” for compliant products.
Third-party audits for high-risk AI.
Technical standards for explainability, robustness, bias mitigation, privacy, and cybersecurity.
4. Norms & Ethical Guidelines
Transparency of AI use and decisions.
Fairness and inclusivity; bias mitigation.
Privacy, security, and data protection.
Clear accountability and redress mechanisms.
5. Data Governance & Sovereignty
Secure, high-quality, sector-specific data sandboxes.
Domestic storage of sensitive Canadian data.
6. Public Engagement & Oversight
Citizen consultation for high-impact AI.
Independent AI oversight and audit body.
Incident reporting and redress mechanisms.
7. International Alignment
Compliance with OECD, EU AI Act, and allied frameworks.
Facilitate global credibility and export of Canadian AI solutions.

column2:
Anchor trust in vision: Canadians will only trust AI if they see it serving human agency and shared prosperity, not just efficiency. A national vision that centers on agency and dignity creates this foundation.
Address the three biggest risks (Ethical Machines):
1. Bias — prevent unfair or discriminatory outcomes.
2.Opacity — reduce black-box decision-making in high-stakes areas.
3. Misuse — protect against harmful or unintended applications.
Prioritize high-impact sectors: focus on healthcare, finance, employment, and public services first
— where failures directly affect lives.
column3:
National AI Assurance Framework: Require standardized bias audits, explainability testing, and
misuse risk assessments for high-stakes systems.
Independent oversight bodies: Include government, academia, and civil society in verifying
compliance.
Context-specific transparency:
Mandatory disclosure: when AI decisions materially affect peopleʼs rights, opportunities, or
democratic processes (e.g., hiring, loans, political advertising).
Optional/flexible disclosure: in creative, collaborative, or low-stakes uses where human + AI
partnership is natural and beneficial.
Continuous monitoring: Oversight must adapt as AI systems evolve — a one-time approval is not
sufficient.
column4:
-Community pilots: Involve citizens in testing AI in schools, clinics, and municipalities, with feedback
loops before wide rollout.
-Ethics incentives for businesses: Offer procurement advantages, funding, or certifications to firms
that embed ethical frameworks early.
-Inclusive governance forums: Establish permanent dialogue spaces between government, industry,
academia, and civil society.
-Normalize human + AI collaboration: Public literacy efforts should move beyond “AI vs. humans”
narratives. Instead, highlight everyday examples where AI enhances human judgment and creativity
— showing Canadians that working with AI is both natural and empowering.
-AI literacy programs: Develop workplace training and awareness campaigns that make AI skills as
familiar as digital literacy is today.

column2: Build trust by making safety and transparency tangible in everyday deployments. Require independent evaluations before public use, with simple scorecards that report accuracy, bias, robustness, and energy use. Mandate incident reporting, red teaming, and post deployment monitoring for any funded or procured system, and create liability clarity with capped safe harbors for products that pass certified evaluations. Standardize model and data cards that explain purpose, training sources, limitations, and human oversight, plus provenance tags and labeling for synthetic media. Stand up sector data trusts with consent tools, audit trails, and Indigenous data governance, and give users clear controls to contest or opt out of automated decisions. Use public procurement to showcase responsible AI by publishing use cases, metrics, and audits from hospitals, utilities, and ministries. Pair this with practical literacy programs for citizens, workers, and managers that teach how to evaluate AI outputs and escalate issues. Close the loop with a national evaluations institute, a public registry of deployed systems, and a recognizable certification mark so people can see which tools meet Canada’s standards.
column3: Canada needs a practical, enforceable trust stack that combines standards, oversight, and clear accountability. Establish mandatory pre-deployment evaluations against national benchmarks for accuracy, bias, robustness, and security, with third-party audits and public scorecards. Require model and data cards that disclose purpose, training sources, limitations, and human oversight, plus provenance and watermarking for synthetic media. Create a federal registry of deployed AI systems, incident reporting with root-cause analysis, and continuous monitoring obligations tied to risk tier. Align liability by granting safe harbors to products that pass certified evaluations and maintain audit logs, while enforcing penalties for undisclosed model changes or data misuse. Mandate secure development and ML supply-chain controls including dataset lineage, reproducible training, red teaming, and rollback plans. Give sector regulators prescriptive approval checklists for high-stakes uses in health, energy, finance, transportation, and public safety, and require documented safety cases before procurement. Embed Indigenous data governance and privacy-preserving techniques such as federated learning and differential privacy in any public funding. Cap it with a recognizable certification mark that signals compliance, so institutions and citizens can see which systems meet Canada’s standards.
column4: Canada should engage citizens and businesses through a clear division of roles and visible, recurring touchpoints that show how responsible AI works in practice. ISED and the Standards Council should run a national registry of deployed AI with plain-language scorecards, model and data cards, and a recognizable compliance mark, while sector regulators publish approval checklists and safety cases for high-risk uses in health, energy, finance, transport, and public safety. Provinces, Crown corporations, and municipalities should host open demos of procured AI systems, publish post-deployment audits, and operate incident hotlines with 30-day public summaries. NRC, colleges, and universities should deliver short, credentialed training for managers, clinicians, and operators on evaluating AI outputs and escalation paths, and offer SME clinics for liability, procurement, and data governance. Civil society and Indigenous partners should co-govern sector data trusts, chair citizen juries on high-impact deployments, and set consent norms. Industry consortia should fund red teaming, share failure reports, and adopt standardized disclosure tiers that balance transparency with IP protection, backed by safe harbor for products that pass certified evaluations and report incidents. Together these actions create predictable rules, informed users, and visible accountability, which is how trust is earned.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by shifting the conversation from fear to familiarity. The risks of AI have been exaggerated to the point where they’re holding the country back. Bad actors will use AI no matter what — those risks don’t stop at borders. What we can control is whether Canadians are the ones building, using, and benefiting from the technology. The real danger isn’t that AI will go wrong; it’s that Canada will fall behind by being too cautious to use it.

To build confidence, we need to normalize AI in everyday life. That starts with showing Canadians where it already works — in healthcare scheduling, housing maintenance, fraud detection, and customer service. When people see real results, fear fades. The government should lead by example, adopting AI tools inside public services to demonstrate transparency and accountability. Regulation should focus on punishing misuse, not on smothering innovation before it happens.

Public education should be about understanding, not alarm. Canadians don’t need to be told that AI is risky; they need to see how it can save time, lower costs, and improve outcomes. Simple literacy campaigns that explain what AI actually is — and how it’s already safely embedded in daily systems — will do more for public trust than any new set of rules.

At the end of the day, confidence comes from participation. If Canadians are building the tools, using them, and seeing the benefits firsthand, trust follows naturally. The path forward isn’t to protect people from AI — it’s to empower them with it.
column3:
Canada needs rules that are short, clear, and focused on outcomes. People don’t trust complexity — they trust results. If an AI tool does what it claims, protects personal data, and has someone accountable when things go wrong, that’s enough. Overregulation only signals that the government doesn’t trust its own innovators.

The framework should be simple. Companies publish what their system does, what data it uses, and what its limits are. They test it once through an independent body and get certified for reliability, safety, and transparency. That certification carries across industries so we don’t waste time redoing audits.

Regulation should target bad behavior, not experimentation. Hold companies responsible if they lie, hide problems, or deploy irresponsibly. Reward openness and fast correction when something fails. Align with global standards so Canada doesn’t isolate itself with “Canada-only” paperwork.

Trust comes from visibility, not fear. When Canadians can see where AI is used, understand how it works, and know who’s responsible, confidence will follow. Keep the framework light, the accountability strong, and the innovation moving.
column4:
Canada can build trust in AI by making people part of the conversation, not spectators to it. Right now, most public messaging around AI is defensive — focused on rules, risks, and regulation. That tone signals fear. To change that, the government needs to shift from warning Canadians about AI to showing them how it improves their daily lives.

Start by meeting people where they are. Businesses, tradespeople, and citizens don’t need lectures about algorithms — they need real examples of how AI saves time, cuts costs, and makes their work easier. When Canadians see AI fixing problems they actually face — faster permits, safer job sites, smoother healthcare scheduling — trust grows naturally. Public engagement should happen through use, not theory.

Transparency should be simple, not technical. People should be able to see where AI is used in government, what it does, and how decisions are made. A public dashboard of government AI deployments would build confidence through visibility, not spin.

As for leadership, industry should lead education and adoption — they’re the ones closest to the tools and the people using them. Academia should lead on standards and ethics, testing what “responsible use” means in practice. Government’s role is to fund the infrastructure, buy from Canadian companies, and communicate clearly.

The goal isn’t to convince people AI is safe — it’s to show them that it’s useful, understandable, and built here. When citizens can see and touch the benefits, trust takes care of itself.

column2:
The biggest trust killer isn’t AI doing scary things—it’s AI doing invisible things where people don’t know what’s automated and what’s human, so Canada should mandate simple disclosure: “This decision was made by an AI system” with a link to how it works and how to appeal, because people can handle AI making mistakes if they know it’s AI and have recourse. The real risks are boring but consequential—biased hiring algorithms, opaque credit decisions, healthcare misdiagnoses—and trust comes from showing you’re actively looking for these failures instead of waiting for journalists to find them, so require companies to publish quarterly “AI incident reports” that detail what broke, why, and what got fixed, the way aviation does with near-misses.

Business mistrust is different: companies fear regulatory whiplash where they invest millions then rules change overnight, so government needs to commit to multi-year policy stability with clear “we won’t change these rules for 5 years unless something catastrophic happens” guarantees for companies operating within guidelines. Public mistrust is driven by sci-fi nonsense about sentient robots when the real concern should be whether the AI denying your insurance claim or filtering your resume is fair—so education can’t be “here’s how neural networks work” but rather “here’s how to tell if an AI decision affecting you is bullXXX and what to do about it.”

The confidence-builder that matters most is showing AI working in low-stakes, high-visibility ways first: use it to optimize traffic lights or predict when playgrounds need maintenance, let people see it making life better in ways they can verify, then gradually move to higher-stakes applications as trust builds—trying to deploy AI in healthcare or criminal justice before people trust it in potholes is why every implementation becomes a political fight.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​
column3:
Canada needs risk-based regulation that’s heavy-handed where AI can actually harm people and light-touch where it’s optimizing ad clicks—the EU AI Act got this mostly right by categorizing applications, so adopt a similar framework but move faster on implementation instead of spending three years consulting on definitions. For high-risk domains like healthcare diagnostics, credit decisions, and criminal justice, require third-party audits before deployment and continuous monitoring after, with mandatory reporting when accuracy drops below thresholds or bias metrics spike, because self-regulation doesn’t work when the incentive is to ship fast and fix later.

Standards need to be boring and interoperable: Canada should adopt ISO/IEC standards for AI testing and documentation rather than inventing Canadian-specific requirements that make it harder for companies to sell internationally, but add teeth by making compliance with those standards a safe harbor against liability—follow the standards and you’re protected from lawsuits, ignore them and you’re exposed. The governance mechanism that actually works is sector-specific regulators enforcing AI rules in their domains rather than creating a new “AI regulator” that doesn’t understand healthcare or finance—give the Privacy Commissioner, financial regulators, and Health Canada clear authority over AI in their spaces, with coordination through a lightweight federal office that ensures consistency.

Priority areas where trust is most acute are anywhere decisions affect vulnerable populations: welfare benefits, immigration, healthcare access, and predictive policing, so these should have the strictest requirements including human review of adverse decisions, explainability that’s actually useful not just technical documentation, and regular bias audits with public reporting. The norm that matters most is shifting burden of proof—right now if AI harms you, you have to prove it; flip that so companies deploying high-risk AI h
column4:
Stop having government bureaucrats explain AI to the public—nobody trusts them and they’re terrible at it anyway—instead fund independent “AI explainer” organizations modeled on Consumer Reports that test systems, publish plain-language findings about what works and what’s sketchy, and aren’t beholden to either government or industry. The Canadian equivalent of Which? magazine but for AI would do more for trust than a thousand government white papers, because people believe independent testers who show them evidence, not officials telling them everything’s fine.
For businesses, trade associations in specific sectors should lead trust-building in their domains—the Canadian Medical Association explaining AI in healthcare, banking associations for financial AI—because companies trust peer guidance more than government mandates, and sector groups can create norms that become de facto standards faster than regulation catches up. Government’s role is convening and funding these efforts, not running them, plus being the transparency enforcer: require companies to publish “nutrition labels” for high-risk AI systems showing accuracy rates, error types, and what data it was trained on, then let civil society and media do the accountability work.
The inclusive piece means going where people are instead of expecting them to come to consultations—partner with libraries, community centers, and unions to run “AI literacy” sessions that focus on practical questions like “how do I know if AI rejected my loan application” or “what rights do I have if my employer uses AI hiring tools,” not theoretical ethics discussions. Indigenous communities and disability rights groups should co-design governance for AI affecting their populations, with actual decision-making power not just advisory roles, because they understand the failure modes better than anyone in Ottawa.

As the CEO of North Vancouver City Library, serving the City of North Vancouver in BC, I am responding to highlight the essential role of public libraries in Canada's AI strategy. Building public trust in AI begins with education from trusted, accessible sources. Canada currently ranks 42nd in AI trust and 44th in AI training (according to KPMG), yet has a powerful network ready to respond: 652 public library systems with over 3,300 locations that serve tens of millions of Canadians annually. Public libraries, consistently ranked among Canada’s most trusted institutions, offer judgment-free spaces for safe exploration, transparent learning, and privacy-protected inquiry. Librarians, who are experts in information literacy, can help to equip Canadians with the critical thinking skills essential for AI literacy. As champions of democracy, libraries ensure everyone can freely inform themselves and participate fully in society. Grounded in equity and openness, libraries support all ages, incomes, and backgrounds. 

As the CEO of of public library, I also believe that AI regulation should reflect Canada’s democratic values - leveraging innovation while mitigating harm. By investing in public AI education through libraries, Canada can nurture informed confidence, strengthen social cohesion, and build an inclusive, AI-ready future for all.
column4:
As the CEO of North Vancouver City Library, serving the City of North Vancouver in BC, I am responding to highlight the essential role of public libraries in Canada's AI strategy. To promote responsible AI use and public trust, Canada must engage citizens and businesses through accessible, community-based education. Public libraries- trusted, democratic institutions - are ideally positioned to lead this effort. At North Vancouver City Library, more than 3,600 residents attended technology classes in 2024, and over 40,000 online courses were completed through LinkedIn Learning (this in a community of 60,000 residents). 

Libraries like NVCL help Canadians develop essential AI literacy, digital skills, and critical thinking for workforce adaptation and lifelong learning. Every month, 16,000 people nationwide attend library technology programs, demonstrating strong public demand for digital education. Libraries also uphold the highest data privacy standards and defend users’ rights to safe, judgment-free learning. By partnering with public libraries, governments can ensure AI regulation reflects Canada’s democratic values, while equipping all Canadians to confidently and responsibly engage with emerging technologies.

column2:
The Canadian government has a moral imperative to NOT build public trust in AI technologies. AI technologies can assist people in interesting ways, but by their very nature they can never be guaranteed to be correct. The AI does not know what it's talking about, it guesses based on previous information. There is, in fact, a lot of misinformation on the internet. I am a software developer. I use and encounter AI every day at work. It is an interesting tool to use while being VERY aware of its limitations, and the general public does not have this knowledge and expecting them to acquire it on their own is very unrealistic. 

Canada should build people's confidence instead that it is committed to keeping AI error and misinformation out of key industries like healthcare, education, and government services, by banning the use of AI without human oversight (e.g. having an AI give a human information directly, not assisting a worker in doing their job where they then review the AI's outputs)--at least until it can be guaranteed to be 100% accurate. (I do not think it can be, given the current research and my knowledge of how it works, but this should be the bar for its use for the public).
column3:
Canada should ban the use of AI in key industries like healthcare, education, and government services. It should ban the use of AI in these industries especially in direct communication with the public. It is irresponsible to let a program so prone to misinformation (no matter how carefully it is trained) represent our government and misinform our citizens. 

Showing AI generated information to people in ANY instance should include explicit and visible disclaimers that the information is given by an AI, it cannot be relied upon to be 100% accurate. This should especially be required for industries that deal with sensitive information or people's lives and well being.
column4:
Canada should build public trust in its governance by banning the use of AI to communicate directly with the public in at least key industries such as healthcare, government services, and education. See previous comments. 

Education is particularly key. Studies have shown that the use of AI decreases people's problem solving skills. More than anything, schools are places where kids are taught to learn, where their brains are developed. If the Canadian government permits the use of AI in these settings it is failing to set its children up for the future. 

The place for innovation is use in the back ends of business where the people interacting directly with the AIs are people who are very aware they do not always provide accurate information and have enough context to deal with this.

Based on what I see on "my socials," not everyone is anti-AI.  But there are many reasons folks -- tech-savvy and not -- to genuinely fear "AI":

* Racialised folks profiled and arrested for the crime of being someone they're actually not or doing something they're actually not doing
* (Mostly) Women having their photo used to generate porn
* Website hosts thoroughly sick of servers being brought to their knees by LLM scraper-bots
* Already-stretched teachers wasting time scrutinising every assignment for GenAI use
* Artists, Writers, Musicians, Animators, etc. seeing their work stolen, mashed up, and fenced
* Writers seeing Amazon peddling AI-hallucinated work under their names
* Third- and Fourth-year University students wondering if they will be employable
* Senior-level employees pressured to just eat the time they waste correcting the mistakes of the GenAI tools being shoved down their throats
* Anyone wondering why their hydro bill went through the roof when their statement shows that they haven't used more electricity vs. last year
* Folks seeing someone they care about drift away into a "relationship" with a chat-bot
* Managers frustrated b/c their direct-reports never seem to level up their skills
* Search engine user who have turned off the "Show AI-summarised results" setting a bajillion times already...looking at more AI-generated results
* Courts shouldering the extra work of double-checking every citation to make sure it wasn't hallucinated
* ANYBODY wondering why they bothered to get an education when management is forever threatening to replace them with "AI"
* ANYBODY exhausted by reading yet another news item where GenAI sent someone driving into a river or told them to mix bleach and vinegar or whatever.
* ChatGPT -- now with ads!  [mimes shooting self]

That's what you're up against.  And, honestly, I don't have a comprehensive answer for you.  You're fighting a battle on a dozen fronts here.  A dozen is above my pay-grade.  Good luck.

column2:
To build public trust in AI technologies while addressing their risks, Canada must prioritize a regulatory approach that harnesses AI’s benefits and actively mitigates its harms. Public libraries advocate for AI regulations rooted in Canadian values and democratic principles, emphasizing transparency, accountability, and inclusivity to ensure AI adoption serves all Canadians.

Recent research highlights this urgency: a KPMG study found Canada ranks low globally for public trust in AI systems and AI training, signaling the need for focused strategies to build confidence.

As widely trusted community institutions, public libraries are uniquely positioned to lead educational and literacy initiatives that foster informed confidence in AI. Through accessible resources, comprehensive training, and reliable information, libraries serve as safe spaces for the public to explore AI technologies. Librarians provide expert guidance, facilitating transparent learning experiences while upholding privacy protections.

By leveraging our expertise in digital literacy and privacy—and offering opportunities for safe exploration and open dialogue—public libraries empower Canadians to learn how to critically evaluate AI tools and services. This helps individuals and businesses navigate risks, fosters ongoing engagement, and supports AI literacy across all demographics.

Building public trust in AI requires collaboration: thoughtful regulation, expanded education and literacy programs, and continued leadership from trusted institutions like public libraries. These steps are vital to address mistrust and ensure Canadians confidently engage with AI in ways that reflect shared values and aspirations.
column4:
Responsible engagement with artificial intelligence (AI) is crucial for building public trust and ensuring technological advancements reflect Canadian values. As AI becomes more prevalent in daily life and business, Canada must proactively promote responsible use and foster confidence in AI governance.

Public libraries are recognized as trusted institutions nationwide, with over 16,000 monthly participants in technology programs. Libraries provide equitable access to resources and training, positioning them to lead AI literacy and lifelong learning efforts. Canadians turn to libraries for guidance in safely navigating new technologies.

To prevent the digital divide from widening, AI literacy and training must be accessible to all, regardless of age or background. Public libraries offer inclusive programs that foster digital skills and critical thinking, empowering citizens and businesses to understand and evaluate AI systems.

Public libraries advocate for AI regulations that harness technology’s benefits while mitigating risks. Effective governance must reflect Canadian values—transparency, accountability, and inclusivity—and protect data privacy. Libraries uphold high standards of data protection and educate communities on privacy rights.

Building trust in AI governance requires collaboration among libraries, government, industry, and community organizations. Libraries are best placed to lead educational initiatives, while policymakers must develop frameworks that protect privacy and uphold democratic principles. Businesses can support these efforts through transparent practices and open dialogue.

By investing in inclusive, transparent, and community-driven strategies—anchored by public libraries’ leadership—Canada can build lasting trust in AI technologies and governance. This ensures all Canadians have the skills and confidence to navigate AI’s opportunities and challenges, advancing innovation responsibly.

As the CEO of the Powell River Public Library, serving Powell River, qathet Regional District and the Tla'amin Nation, I believe that building public trust in AI technologies depends on regulation, education, transparency, and access through trusted institutions. Public libraries—consistently ranked among the most trusted organizations in Canada—are ideally positioned to lead public AI education, foster transparent literacy, and create safe spaces for Canadians to explore and understand this rapidly evolving technology.
A recent KPMG study found that Canada ranks 42nd out of 47 countries in public trust in AI systems and 44th in AI training. These results point to a clear need for education from credible, trusted sources. Libraries already fulfill this role by providing free, judgment-free environments where people can ask questions, experiment with new tools, and develop the critical thinking and information literacy skills essential for navigating the age of AI.
Librarians—trained experts in evaluating and interpreting information—are natural guides in helping communities understand how AI systems work, what their risks are, and how to use them responsibly. Libraries also uphold foundational values of privacy protection, intellectual freedom, and universal access—principles that mirror the ethical standards needed for responsible AI adoption.
Public libraries believe in AI regulation that leverages the benefits of innovation while mitigating potential harms. We support policies that reflect Canada’s democratic values: fairness, accountability, transparency, and inclusion.
To build true public confidence, Canada must invest in library-led AI literacy programs and empower libraries as trusted, neutral hubs for public learning. By coupling sound regulation with education and safe, inclusive spaces for exploration, Canada can ensure that AI adoption enhances, rather than erodes, public trust and democratic engagement.
column4:
As the CEO of the Powell River Public Library, serving Powell River, qathet Regional District and the Tla'amin Nation, I believe that building trust in AI governance and promoting responsible AI use must begin with inclusive, community-based engagement. Canadians already recognize public libraries as trusted, democratic institutions—neutral spaces dedicated to learning, privacy, and intellectual freedom. Every month, more than 16,000 people attend technology programs in public libraries across the country. This reach and trust make libraries essential partners in Canada’s AI strategy.
Public libraries are uniquely positioned to lead AI literacy and digital skills training, helping Canadians of all ages develop the critical thinking and technical understanding needed to navigate an AI-enriched society. Our programs support lifelong learning, workforce adaptation, and accessible training without cost barriers. By embedding AI education within familiar, local spaces, libraries can help ensure that the digital divide does not widen and that every Canadian has the opportunity to engage meaningfully with emerging technologies.
Libraries also believe in strong, transparent AI regulation such as frameworks that leverage the benefits of innovation while mitigating potential harms. We support policies that reflect Canada’s democratic values and defend data privacy rights. As community education centers operating under the highest standards of data protection, we not only safeguard personal information but also teach citizens how to protect their own.
To fuel trust, government and industry should collaborate with libraries to deliver public-facing AI education, ensure clear communication about how AI systems work, and balance transparency with innovation. By investing in libraries as hubs of AI literacy and civic engagement, Canada can empower citizens and businesses alike to use AI responsibly—strengthening both our democracy and our shared digital future.

column2: As a Library Manager for Vaughan Public Libraries, serving Vaughan, Ontario, I am responding to highlight the essential role of public libraries in Canada's AI strategy. The public already distrusts AI. a recent study by KPMG found that ranks 42 out of 47 nations when it comes to trust in AI systems. The same study also found that Canada ranks 44 out of 47 for AI training. It seems likely that those two things go hand in hand - how can you expect the public to trust something it has no training on and little awareness of? Public libraries have the trust that AI systems don't, and we have the capacity to do the training that Canadians need. column4: As a Library Manager for Vaughan Public Libraries, serving Vaughan, Ontario, I am responding to highlight the essential role of public libraries in Canada's AI strategy. The advent and continued expansion of AI threatens to open another digital divide amongst Canadians beyond just access to high-speed internet. As Canadian public libraries provide access to high-speed internet and the devices needed to access it, so too can public libraries provide accessible, community-based lifelong learning infrastructure reaching all ages and learning levels without cost barriers.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by making safety, transparency, and accountability the default—recognizing that confidence is earned through real-world performance, not promises.

1. Make AI Safe by Default
Set clear, enforceable standards for privacy, security, bias, and safety testing, mandatory for all publicly funded or procured AI. Require plain-language model and data cards, risk assessments, and post-deployment monitoring with public incident summaries. Introduce a visible trust label—like an energy-efficiency sticker—so users can identify independently verified AI systems. Implement content provenance and watermarking to flag synthetic media, curb misinformation, and prevent fraud.

2. Embed Accountability and Rights
Guarantee users’ rights to explanation, opt-out (where feasible), human review, and clear complaint channels. Mandate incident disclosure and redress timelines for public-sector AI. Establish an independent AI Safety and Assurance Lab to test and stress-test high-impact systems, publishing results for public scrutiny.

3. Build Confidence Through Practice and Literacy
Have the public sector lead by publishing audits, bias reports, and real-world metrics (e.g., accuracy, wait-time reductions, incident resolution). Fund AI literacy programs for the public and training for frontline workers to ensure confident, safe use. Partner with trusted clinical and professional bodies—such as OMA, AMA, and OntarioMD—to co-design and validate healthcare AI tools.

4. Measure Trust and Track Progress
Adopt practical indicators: percentage of deployments with audits, number of incidents resolved, privacy/security audit pass rates, and user satisfaction, especially in underserved areas.

Through transparent governance, user rights, oversight, and partnerships, Canada can make AI safe by design, accountable in use, and understandable in practice, securing lasting public and professional confidence.
column3:
Canada should create a simple, consistent, and scalable AI trust framework that balances public protection with innovation speed. The goal is to make responsible deployment clear, uniform, and faster than today’s fragmented approach—while ensuring ongoing accountability after release.

1. Tiered “Trust by Design” Framework
Develop a national assurance model with three pillars:
- Pre-deployment risk assessment to classify intended use, risk level, and mitigation steps.
- Plain-language model and data cards outlining purpose, limits, data sources, and known risks.
- Post-deployment monitoring and incident reporting with transparent error tracking and corrective actions.
Pair this with a visible trust label or certification mark to identify systems meeting Canadian safety standards. Link safe-harbour protections to compliance, rewarding responsible developers and raising accountability.

2. Build on Existing Standards
Align with recognized frameworks such as ISO/IEC 42001, NIST AI RMF, PHIPA, and SOC-2. Mandate interoperability and audit logging, with funding support to help startups comply. Publish sector-specific playbooks for healthcare, finance, energy, and critical infrastructure to guide consistent adoption.

3. Focus Oversight on High-Risk Uses
Apply stricter controls for areas like healthcare, credit, infrastructure, biometrics, child-facing tools, and election systems. Require human-in-the-loop validation, explainability, data minimization, and strong access controls modelled on PHIPA.

4. Enable Independent Assurance and Reuse
Create a national AI assurance network of accredited labs and testbeds so companies can “audit once, use many times,” reducing duplication and cost while ensuring consistent evaluation.

5. Embed Transparency and User Rights
Mandate public AI scorecards (incidents, fixes, uptime, error rates) and guarantee user rights to explanation, human review, and redress.

This submission is a great start! Canada can build lasting public and business confidence in AI by making engagement practical, participatory, and transparent. Citizens and companies need clear information, visible accountability, and simple ways to interact with the systems and rules that shape AI’s impact.

1. Communicate Clearly and Openly
- Launch plain-language explainers and short videos showing how AI systems work, their limits, and user rights.
- Publish simple public scorecards for AI systems used by the government, tracking accuracy, incidents, and corrective actions.
- Create a single national help portal where citizens can find information, raise complaints, or opt out of AI-driven processes where feasible.
- Use citizen panels, public consultations, and community workshops to co-design guidelines for data use and high-risk applications (e.g., healthcare, education, credit).

2. Equip Businesses for Responsible AI
- Provide ready-to-use toolkits, templates for risk assessments, model/data cards, and privacy checklists, to help SMEs comply efficiently.
- Expand regulatory sandboxes that let innovators test responsibly before full rollout.
- Introduce a “Trusted AI Vendor” seal or certification so customers and the public can easily identify companies following Canadian safety and ethics standards.
- Facilitate ongoing dialogue between industry, government, and clinical agencies (e.g., provincial medical associations and OntarioMD) to ensure transparent, evidence-based governance.

3. Clarify Roles and Responsibilities
- Government (federal and provincial): Set and enforce rules, manage procurement standards, and oversee accessible complaint and appeal routes.
- Industry: Build to these standards, display the trust seal, publish user-friendly explanations, and lead by example in inclusive design and data governance.
- Education sector: Integrate AI literacy into high school and post-secondary curricula to foster informed citizens and future innovators.

column2: AI technologies should not be touted as a panacea solution for all labour needs. AI needs to be developed in an ethical and climate-conscious way. There must be strong economic incentives to ensure AI is developed and deployed responsibly. column2:
Public trust is the foundation of successful AI adoption. Canadians will only embrace AI if they believe it is safe, transparent, fair, and accountable. Building confidence requires proactive governance, education, and visible leadership that show AI can serve people—not replace or exploit them.

1. Strengthen Transparency and Accountability.
Mandate clear disclosure when AI systems are used, especially in sensitive areas like health, employment, and finance. Implement consistent algorithmic impact assessments and require organizations to explain decisions in plain language. Combat AI fakes, deepfakes, and misinformation by empowering the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) to advance watermarking, content provenance, and public labeling of AI-generated media.

2. Protect Data Privacy and Consent.
Enforce strict safeguards under the AI and Data Act to prevent data use without consent. Publicly funded projects must follow opt-in transparency standards and disclose how data is collected, used, and stored.

3. Advance Safety and Risk Management.
Enable CAISI and the Safe and Secure AI Advisory Group to test and evaluate high-risk models, publish safety guidance, and coordinate with global partners. Public reporting of incidents and independent audits will reinforce accountability.

4. Educate and Empower Canadians.
Launch a National AI Literacy and Awareness Program led by CAISI, CIFAR, and the Pan-Canadian AI Institutes to help Canadians recognize AI-generated content, understand data rights, and build informed confidence.

5. Demonstrate Ethical and Public Benefit.
Expand adoption of the Voluntary Code of Conduct and deploy AI visibly in areas like healthcare, clean energy, and accessibility—showing tangible benefits with clear safeguards.

By combining safety science, transparency, consent, and education, Canada can make trust its global competitive advantage in responsible, human-centered AI.
column3:
To ensure AI systems are safe, transparent, and aligned with Canadian values, Canada needs a comprehensive governance framework that blends legislation, technical standards, independent oversight, and ethical norms. Trust must be built into every stage—from design to deployment.

1. Strong Regulatory Foundation.
Implement the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) as Canada’s core framework for AI accountability, requiring risk assessments, transparency, and clear lines of responsibility. Pair AIDA with privacy modernization and human-rights protections to cover all stages of the AI lifecycle.

2. National Standards for Safety and Assurance.
Through the Standards Council of Canada, develop and adopt Canadian AI safety and assurance standards aligned with ISO/IEC and OECD guidelines. These should cover model testing, explainability, bias mitigation, red-teaming, and secure deployment.

3. Independent Oversight and Certification.
Empower the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) to evaluate high-impact systems, publish safety benchmarks, and accredit independent auditors. Establish a national AI Assurance Registry for certified systems used in healthcare, finance, defence, and public services.

4. Governance and Ethics by Design.
Mandate algorithmic impact assessments, human oversight, and incident reporting for public and private deployments. Ethical design principles—fairness, transparency, and accountability—should be embedded into funding and procurement requirements.

5. Priority Risk Domains.
Focus oversight on areas with the greatest trust implications: synthetic media and misinformation, automated decision-making, biometrics and surveillance, and critical infrastructure AI.

By integrating law, science, and standards, Canada can build an AI governance ecosystem that ensures every product deployed is safe, explainable, and worthy of public trust.
column4:
Building lasting trust in AI requires open dialogue, inclusive participation, and visible leadership. Canadians must feel that AI is governed for the public good, not just for efficiency or profit. Engagement should empower both citizens and businesses to understand, question, and shape how AI is used in society.

1. National AI Awareness and Engagement Strategy.
Launch a coordinated effort—led by the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) with partners like CIFAR, the Pan-Canadian AI Institutes, and Innovation, Science and Economic Development (ISED)—to explain how AI systems work, where they are used, and what protections exist. Public campaigns, interactive dashboards, and community dialogues can make AI governance visible and understandable.

2. Inclusive and Ongoing Dialogue.
Establish citizen assemblies on AI and sector-specific advisory panels to include Indigenous, youth, and under-represented voices. Public consultation should be continuous, not episodic, ensuring policy reflects lived experience and diverse cultural values.

3. Partnering with Business for Responsible Adoption.
Industry associations, SMEs, and large firms should promote responsible AI codes of practice, model transparency, and participate in safety testing with CAISI. Government can incentivize participation through recognition programs and procurement preferences.

4. Balancing Transparency and Innovation.
Implement clear disclosure standards and algorithmic impact assessments while protecting legitimate trade secrets. Transparent risk communication—not full code disclosure—builds confidence without slowing innovation.

5. Leadership and Coordination.
CAISI should lead on safety science; ISED on commercialization; TBS on public-sector AI use; and civil society organizations on literacy and oversight.

By engaging Canadians as partners, not bystanders, Canada can create a shared culture of responsible, trustworthy, and human-centered AI innovation.

column2: Public libraries are trusted institutions that are located in almost every community in Canada.  As CEO of Strathcona County Library, which serves rural and urban residents, we are the community hub where people come to understand new technologies and get hands-on help in a safe and supportive environment.  Trust has to be earned, and public libraries have a proven track record.  Surveys show that public libraries are trusted by Canadians as a place to access information, training, and resources.  Public library librarians have the skills, expertise and  experience in explaining the pros and cons of new technology so Canadians are empowered, informed, and more confident about the project they are undertaking.  Public libraries help ordinary Canadians ask questions without judgement, experiment with new technologies and develop critical thinking skills.  Federal investment in public libraries would allow us to have the tools, technology access, curriculum development, and programming resources to provide training to our rural and urban residents regardless of the level of understanding they start with on their learning journey.  Public libraries want to see AI regulations that leverage the benefits of AI, that reflect Canadian values, that bolster our democracy, but that also try to mitigate the potential harms to privacy, intellectual property, and intellectual freedom. A recent study by KPMG found Canada near the bottom of the list in trust in AI systems and in AI training. Federal investment in public libraries is one way to increase trust in AI technologies by not glossing over the risks these technologies could pose.
column4: Public libraries are well positioned to lead efforts to build trust in AI technologies because Canadians already recognize public libraries as trusted, democratic institutions.  Public libraries are already recognized a centres of community education with the highest standards of protecting data and personal information.  Canadians know that public libraries protect their personal information and provide training on how to protect themselves in a digital environment.  There are public libraries in every community, and they are already community hubs that individuals and small businesses turn to.  In my library, Strathcona County library, we serve urban residents from our library, but we also take technology training out to rural residents on our Bookmobile.  Public libraries provide infrastructure in a social space where Canadians can gather and debate and diverse voices could discuss the development of AI to reflect the Canadian values..  Public libraries are a judgement-free space where Canadians can hold discussions about AI ethics.  As well, public libraries have a track records of facilitating community discussions, so this could happen with AI policies as well.  My library, Strathcona County Library, have librarians who can help Canadians to understand the biases in AI technology and the limitations, including deepfake detection and information verification.  Librarians are information literacy experts and this is exactly the background needed when helping all Canadians understand biases and limitations while developing critical thinking skills. Federal investment in public libraries would create a national AI literacy infrastructure across Canada in institutions that are trusted by Canadians to provide unbiased information about technologies.

column2:
Public trust is Canada’s greatest competitive advantage in AI. To sustain it, Canada must combine strong safeguards, transparent governance, and broad AI literacy that empowers citizens to understand—not fear—AI systems.

1. Make responsible AI visible and verifiable.
Establish a National AI Assurance and Safety Hub to test and certify models for bias, robustness, privacy, and explainability. Publish plain-language “AI nutrition labels” showing how systems work, what data they use, and how accountability is enforced. Public registries of high-impact AI systems, as proposed under AIDA, would strengthen oversight.

2. Regulate for accountability, not fear.
Pair AIDA with clear liability frameworks so users know who is responsible for harms. Require organizations to conduct and disclose impact assessments and enable independent audits. Transparency, rather than opacity, builds confidence.

3. Embed education and AI literacy.
Expand AI literacy across schools, colleges, and workplaces, focusing on practical understanding of how AI supports—not replaces—human decision-making. Public libraries, community centres, and colleges can host accessible workshops emphasizing digital rights, data privacy, and ethical use.

4. Engage communities and underrepresented voices.
Include Indigenous, newcomer, and equity-seeking communities in shaping AI policies and datasets. Co-governance creates legitimacy and reflects Canada’s diversity.

5. Celebrate trustworthy innovation.
Highlight certified “Trusted AI Made in Canada” solutions in public communications and procurement to reinforce that safety and competitiveness go hand-in-hand.
column3:
To make AI safe, fair, and reliable, Canada needs a coordinated framework that blends regulation, technical standards, and ethical norms—anchored in transparency, accountability, and human oversight.

1. A coherent regulatory foundation.

Enact and operationalize the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) as Canada’s central framework for high-impact AI, defining risk tiers, audit obligations, and enforcement powers.

Align AIDA with privacy (PIPEDA modernization) and human rights legislation to ensure consistent protection across jurisdictions.

2. National assurance and testing infrastructure.

Establish a National AI Assurance and Safety Hub to independently test systems for bias, robustness, and explainability, providing pre-market certification and post-deployment monitoring.

Require impact assessments and “model cards” documenting training data, risks, and limitations.

Create open incident reporting and redress mechanisms to address harm transparently.

3. Adoption of international standards.

Harmonize with OECD, ISO/IEC, and NIST AI frameworks on safety, governance, and ethics, while contributing Canadian leadership on equity, accessibility, and Indigenous data sovereignty.

Mandate compliance with technical standards for cybersecurity, data integrity, and human-in-the-loop oversight in critical sectors (health, energy, defence).

4. Ethical and social norms.

Institutionalize ethics-by-design through funding criteria and procurement rules that reward transparency, inclusion, and environmental responsibility.

Involve public, academic, and Indigenous partners in ongoing governance reviews.

5. Priority risk areas.
Focus regulation and testing first on biometrics, healthcare AI, generative content, and automated decision systems—domains with the highest human and societal impact.
column4:
To foster lasting public and business confidence, Canada must treat AI governance as a shared civic project, not only a technical or regulatory one. Building trust requires visible transparency, inclusive dialogue, and clear leadership across sectors.

1. Make AI understandable and accessible.

Launch a national “AI in Everyday Life” public-education campaign led by the Canadian Digital Service and Innovation, Science and Economic Development (ISED), using plain-language tools, explainer videos, and case studies.

Require major public-sector AI deployments to publish easy-to-read impact summaries—how the system works, its limits, and who is accountable.

2. Build inclusive participation channels.

Create AI Citizens’ Assemblies and regional dialogue hubs (through libraries, colleges, and Indigenous governance organizations) to involve communities in setting priorities and assessing ethical risks.

Support community data stewards and Indigenous data-sovereignty initiatives to ensure diverse voices shape datasets and decisions.

3. Mobilize trusted intermediaries.

Government should set policy, fund literacy, and ensure accountability.

Colleges and universities should drive education, research, and skills training.

Industry and start-ups should operationalize ethical design and transparency practices, reporting results through shared assurance frameworks.

Civil-society organizations should monitor equity and accessibility, translating technical debates into public dialogue.

4. Balance openness with innovation.
Establish safe-to-learn sandboxes that allow testing under transparency and oversight, ensuring experimentation doesn’t erode trust.

column2:
Public trust in AI starts with transparency, accountability, and human benefit. Canadians will trust AI when it feels understandable, controllable, and aligned with their values — not when it feels imposed or hidden behind algorithms.

Canada should require clear disclosure when AI tools are used, ensure users retain ownership of their data, and establish strong accountability for harm or bias. At the same time, we must invest in AI literacy — practical education for citizens, businesses, and creators that demystifies AI and highlights safe, beneficial use cases.

Government can lead by example: adopting ethical AI standards in public services and showcasing responsible Canadian AI success stories. When people see AI improving daily life — simplifying work, protecting privacy, and creating opportunity — confidence will naturally follow.
column3:
To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy, security must be treated as core to ethics — not an afterthought. Fraud, identity theft, and AI-enabled scams are now the biggest threats to public confidence.

Canada should establish a national AI security and integrity framework that sets clear, practical standards for:
	•	Data protection and encryption across all AI-powered platforms.
	•	Fraud and identity-verification protocols for apps using payments or personal data.
	•	AI-assisted threat monitoring tools accessible to small businesses, not only large corporations.
	•	Rapid response coordination between government, law enforcement, and developers to address AI-driven fraud.

By making cybersecurity and fraud prevention a foundation of responsible AI, Canada can protect citizens, empower innovators, and build genuine public trust in AI technologies.
column4:
Canada can build lasting trust in AI by making its governance open, understandable, and inclusive. People and businesses need to see how AI works, who’s accountable, and how it benefits them — not just be told it’s safe.

The federal government should lead with transparent communication: clear labeling when AI is used, plain-language explanations of decisions, and public dashboards showing how AI systems in government are tested for fairness and accuracy.

Businesses and creators should be partners in this process — developing ethical design standards, sharing best practices, and offering real examples of responsible AI in action.

Education institutions should embed AI literacy early, teaching Canadians how to question and use AI wisely.

When government ensures transparency, industry models responsible use, and educators empower citizens, trust becomes a shared national asset — not a compliance checkbox.

column2:
Set clear rules of the road. Use a risk based framework aligned to NIST and ISO with plain language obligations for testing, monitoring, and incident reporting.

Fund independent evaluation. Give the Canadian AI Safety Institute a mandate to run audits, red teams, and system cards and to publish results that the public can understand.

Require transparency to users. Provide clear notices, purpose limits, human contact for appeals, and easy ways to opt out when feasible.

Protect privacy and data rights. Use strong consent, data minimization, and secure data sharing through data trusts with independent governance.

Make government a model user. Publish algorithmic impact assessments, bias and robustness test results, and post deployment audits for all public sector AI.

Create safe harbor tied to good practice. Offer liability limits and insurance options when companies follow accredited evaluation, monitoring, and incident reporting.

Focus on children and sensitive contexts. Require extra safeguards and content controls in education, health, finance, and public safety.

Build skills and literacy. Fund national micro courses for workers and managers, teacher toolkits for schools, and a public information campaign that explains benefits and limits with real examples.

Support trusted certification. Stand up a voluntary trust mark for consumer facing AI that meets security, privacy, and fairness standards and is verified by independent labs.

Engage communities early. Use civic panels and patient or citizen advisory boards for deployments that affect rights or access to essential services.
column3:
Anchor on recognised norms. Adopt the OECD AI Principles as the national north star and align all federal guidance to them.

Use proven risk frameworks. Require alignment with the NIST AI Risk Management Framework for lifecycle risk management and plain language controls.

Certify governance systems. Encourage or require ISO IEC 42001 certification for organisational AI management systems, along with ISO IEC 23894 for technical risk processes.

Join global conduct for advanced models. Endorse the G7 Hiroshima Process code of conduct and integrate its testing and red teaming expectations into federal procurement.

Stand up independent evaluation. Resource the Canadian AI Safety Institute to run model evaluations, red teams, and system cards that are publishable and reusable by provinces and regulators.

Provide clear oversight rules. With the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act no longer moving forward, issue an interim federal directive that sets obligations for impact assessments, incident reporting, post deployment monitoring, and audit trails in line with global standards.

Create sector playbooks where trust is most sensitive. Begin with health, finance, critical infrastructure, education, and elections. Each playbook should define approved uses, dataset documentation, evaluation thresholds, and human escalation paths.

Tie incentives to assurance. Offer safe harbour and liability limits when products meet accredited evaluations and maintain ISO IEC 42001 certification, and require disclosure and remediation plans for residual risks.

Make government a model user. Mandate publication of algorithmic impact summaries, evaluation results, and independent audit findings for federal deployments, and reuse Canadian AI Safety Institute test suites.

Keep international interoperability. Ensure Canadian guidance stays compatible with allied regimes and voluntary codes so firms can export without duplicative compliance.
column4:
Launch a national AI literacy and engagement strategy that makes AI understandable to all Canadians. Use plain language campaigns, school curricula, and public workshops showing how AI helps in areas like health, transit, and climate while explaining risks and safeguards.

Create an open AI transparency portal that lists government AI systems, their purposes, evaluations, and impact assessments in one place. Give citizens a way to ask questions, flag concerns, and see how feedback leads to action.

Build community participation into policy design. Use citizen panels, patient and consumer advisory boards, and public consultations before deploying AI in sensitive areas such as health, employment, or justice.

Encourage businesses to adopt voluntary trust marks verified by independent evaluators. Recognize certified companies publicly to reward accountability.

Support journalists, educators, and civil society to act as translators who explain AI implications and hold both industry and government accountable.

Government should lead on setting standards and modeling responsible use, the Canadian AI Safety Institute should lead independent evaluation and guidance, academia should lead research and public education, and industry should lead transparent implementation and communication with customers.

Balance transparency with innovation by publishing clear risk categories and data disclosure expectations, ensuring startups can innovate without regulatory uncertainty while still maintaining accountability.

column3:
See Research and Talent also “ensure” is a strong word. We should be realistic about AI products are hard to be trustworthy and safe:
-Unlike physical products, the threshold required for AI entry is extremely low, and incompetent or bad actors have already surfaced. Like fintech, when there can be literally one-man shops propping up (think Quadriga), it is extremely hard to keep bad products out even if it is produced and deployed in Canada.
-Bad GenAI producers can deploy overseas or on the dark web where government authorities have few tools to cut them off. 
-Even with big tech, LLM goes wrong often, where in its entirety, harm may occur in low frequency but the consequences has already proven deadly. So can we really call general use AI safe and trustworthy? If we look at the EU AI Act, we can see that categorizing LLM/GenAI as general purpose AI that requires robust data governance and identifying AI output would not sufficiently eliminate the worst of the user outcomes.
-And how would the government ensure discrimination doesn’t happen in financial services or housing rental AI decisions, given what exists in the data and generally in our society? How do governments enforce on discriminatory practices? The onus is always on the organization to ensure the outcome is not discriminatory. Human should be responsible for the decision. In that case, do we need any laws on the AI being used? Rather, it should be ensuring human is able to justify a decision regardless of what the AI says. Unjustifiable AI decisions need to be supplemented or should not be used. This maybe where the law lies. This is especially important for government institutions that uses AI to help determine the outcome of the liberty and welfare of individuals.
column4:
This must be a multi-pronged strategy:
•	Children and parents are starved of information. (see Andy Phippen’s research) We must provide information to children and parents about the risks in AI and what proper use looks like, e.g., when AI is helpful and when it is cheating, when AI can produce harmful output; there needs to be some real-life demos to show the effects; let local communities figure out who is trusted by their population to deliver such information
•	But, of course, teach children and workers how to use AI for their benefits.
•	Must also present the complex views existing within the industry and among leading figures. Create major exhibitions in science museums and create information sessions in libraries, community centers and retirement homes.
•	And let’s not be all doom and gloom, promoting stories where AI has improved lives would show the promise of a better future.
•	Create AI literacy amongst the population to the point that AI is general knowledge like Sun, earth, and air! Treat the general public intelligently. Whatever the focus is, it needs to be for individual liberty and the better future of children because these are what people are really about.
