-	Trust in AI begins with trusted intermediaries. Public libraries are consistently ranked among the most trusted institutions in Canada. They offer welcoming and inclusive spaces where citizens can explore new technologies, ask questions, and build critical thinking skills—essential for navigating AI safely and confidently.
-	Libraries provide transparent, accessible AI education. From introductory workshops to advanced digital skills training, libraries help demystify AI and empower Canadians to understand its risks, benefits, and ethical implications. This includes resources on algorithmic bias, data privacy, and responsible use.
-	Libraries uphold privacy and democratic values. As stewards of intellectual freedom and privacy, libraries are uniquely positioned to model ethical AI use and advocate for regulations that reflect Canadian values—transparency, accountability, and human rights.
-	Canada must invest in community-based AI literacy. The KPMG study ranking Canada 42nd out of 47 countries in trust in AI systems and 44th in AI training highlights the urgency of grassroots education. Libraries can lead the charge by offering localized, culturally relevant programming that builds informed confidence.
-	Library staff are experts in information literacy. Their skills in evaluating sources, navigating misinformation, and fostering critical inquiry make them essential allies in helping Canadians assess AI tools and services with discernment.
-	Welcoming and inclusive spaces foster informed confidence. Libraries provide environments where citizens can experiment with AI tools, engage in dialogue, and co-create solutions—without fear of judgment or exclusion.
column4:
Public libraries are among the most trusted and accessible institutions in Canada. Open to everyone, regardless of background, libraries offer welcoming, inclusive spaces where people can explore new technologies, ask questions, and build the skills needed to navigate an AI-driven world.
•	Libraries provide community-based, lifelong learning infrastructure. With over 16,000 Canadians attending technology programs at libraries every month, libraries are already helping people of all ages and learning levels build digital skills, AI literacy, and critical thinking—without cost barriers.
•	Canada must ensure AI literacy is available to all. As AI becomes more embedded in daily life and work, it’s essential that no one is left behind. Libraries help close the digital divide by offering accessible training and outreach in both urban and rural communities.
•	Libraries are trusted intermediaries for public engagement. Their deep community roots and partnerships with schools, local governments, and nonprofits make them ideal leaders in grassroots AI education and trust-building efforts.
•	Libraries support responsible AI governance that reflects Canadian values. We advocate for policies that balance innovation with transparency, protect data privacy, and empower citizens to understand and shape how AI is used in their lives.
•	Librarians help Canadians protect their data and make informed choices. Operating under the highest standards of privacy, libraries are safe places to learn about data rights, algorithmic bias, and ethical technology use—guided by professionals trained in information literacy.
•	Leadership should be shared across sectors. Government must set clear regulatory frameworks and invest in public education; industry must prioritize transparency and ethical design; libraries and other community organizations must lead inclusive, on-the-ground engagement and learning.
•	Library staff already help Canadians protect their data & make informed choices.

column2: Canada should not be focusing its energy and resources on AI -- it is a harmful science that is bad for the environment and society. Generative AI and LLM in particular are extremely harmful -- it is completely unethical, as all the information it "provides" is stolen and often fabricated. Far too many companies and individuals, as well as governments, are overly trusting of an unreliable system that can hallucinate information as it pleases and can steal other individuals' intellectual property without consequence. The common use of AI is dangerous, and must be treated as such.

column2:
You will not "media literacy" your way to trust. That is a strategy that requires 100% of individuals to be 100% attentive 100% of the time, and 100% accurate in their assessment. 

AI systems are flawed. They cannot be perfect. The responsibility to protect from harm should not be on the individual to recognize when harm might result and personally take action to mitigate it. That requires people to be perfect. 

People need to be able to trust that the system (government regulation corporate incentives, etc) is there to protect them, and that when harm happens, the people will be made whole. 

Require transparency. When a company makes a kitchen appliance that is faulty and can lead to injury, they have to very publicly recall it and make things right. People trust that the appliance that has not been recalled is safe to use. What will AI companies have to do when their systems hurt people?
column3:
I repeat myself: Canada needs to be a leader in ETHICAL AI. Develop safeguards, guardrails, privacy standards (including opt-in as the default, not opt-out), human-in-the-loop processes, auditing requirements, and appropriate liability for companies who do wrong when their AI does wrong. 

AI cannot be a free-for-all that allows for skirting of responsibility because the algorithm did it; the algorithm's coders did it. 

Canada needs to recognize that algorithms are highly manipulable, that the outputs are a direct result of the inputs, and that those inputs can be --intentionally and otherwise -- selective, flawed, error-riddled, and / or biased. 

Minimum standards of accuracy and integrity for training data is essential. Companies should not be allowed, for example, to steal it from copyright holders. They should be required to ensure it is correct. They should be required to evaluate the potential for bias frim the systems they produce. These systems should be subject to academic/peer review. They should not be allowed to claim a proprietary black box shields them from oversight.

And when their systems result in harms, especially from their negligence, they should be held accountable. (Accountability means more than paying a fraction of their profits in a fine.)

column2:
rust built through demonstrated accountability, not reassurance.
Trust deficit stems from: (1) Opacity (nobody understands AI decisions), (2) Asymmetric harm (AI errors hit vulnerable groups), (3) Institutional failure (big tech promises self-regulation, fails repeatedly).
People mistrust institutions deploying AI without accountability.
Solution: Operational Trust Architecture
1. Mandatory Transparency: Every high-stakes AI (healthcare, justice, finance, employment) must publish: training data sources, model architecture, performance across demographics, failure modes, appeals process. Regulatory requirement. Non-compliant systems barred.
2. Public AI Registry: National database (AC|DC Ethics Board managed) listing all certified systems. Citizens search: "What AI used in my child's school?" "What AI decides my healthcare?" Full transparency. Exists nowhere globally. Canada leads.
3. Real Recourse: Currently AI denies loan/job/benefit, no explanation, no appeal. Solution: Right to Algorithmic Explanation and Contest. Legally enforceable: understand why AI decided, challenge before human reviewer, receive timely response (30 days max), get written explanation if upheld.
4. Incident Reporting: Aviation achieves safety through mandatory incident reporting, root-cause analysis. Apply to AI. All systems report "near-misses" and failures. Independent analysis. Public summaries. Continuous improvement. Currently companies hide failures, preventing systemic learning.
5. Independent Testing: Like drugs require clinical trials, high-stakes AI requires independent safety evaluation before public use. Government-administered testing, not company-funded. Costs recovered through provider fees.
Drivers of mistrust: (1) Bias. Solution: Mandatory fairness audits, results public. Bias demonstrated barred until fixed. (2) Privacy violations. Solution: Zero-knowledge architectures as national standard. (3) Job displacement. Solution: Workforce transition support automatically triggered
Transparency, education, inclusion of general public in some decision making
column3:
Research Issues
1) Research Goal: The goal of AI research should be to create not undirected intelligence, but beneficial intelligence.

2) Research Funding: Investments in AI should be accompanied by funding for research on ensuring its beneficial use, including thorny questions in computer science, economics, law, ethics, and social studies, such as:

How can we make future AI systems highly robust, so that they do what we want without malfunctioning or getting hacked?
How can we grow our prosperity through automation while maintaining people’s resources and purpose?
How can we update our legal systems to be more fair and efficient, to keep pace with AI, and to manage the risks associated with AI?
What set of values should AI be aligned with, and what legal and ethical status should it have?
3) Science-Policy Link: There should be constructive and healthy exchange between AI researchers and policy-makers.

4) Research Culture: A culture of cooperation, trust, and transparency should be fostered among researchers and developers of AI.

5) Race Avoidance: Teams developing AI systems should actively cooperate to avoid corner-cutting on safety standards.

Ethics and Values
6) Safety: AI systems should be safe and secure throughout their operational lifetime, and verifiably so where applicable and feasible.

7) Failure Transparency: If an AI system causes harm, it should be possible to ascertain why.

8) Judicial Transparency: Any involvement by an autonomous system in judicial decision-making should provide a satisfactory explanation auditable by a competent human authority.

9) Responsibility: Designers and builders of advanced AI systems are stakeholders in the moral implications of their use, misuse, and actions, with a responsibility and opportunity to shape those implications.

10) Value Alignment: Highly autonomous AI systems should be designed so that their goals and behaviors can be assured to align with human values throughout their operation.

column2:
Building public trust in AI requires visible accountability, clear communication, and evidence that systems are safe, fair, and beneficial. Confidence grows when Canadians can see that risks are managed transparently, not hidden behind technical complexity.

1. Strong Governance and Oversight
Adopt a risk-based regulatory approach where higher-impact AI systems, those affecting health, safety, or rights, face stricter testing, documentation, and audit requirements. Require developers and operators to share clear accountability for AI outcomes and establish independent incident reporting mechanisms for safety and bias issues.

2. Transparency and Explainability
Mandate plain-language disclosures that explain what an AI system does, what data it uses, and its limitations. Public-facing “AI trust labels” or certification marks could identify systems that meet defined safety, fairness, and privacy standards.

3. Privacy and Security by Design
Reinforce data governance standards for consent, data provenance, and protection. Support the adoption of privacy-preserving technologies such as federated learning and differential privacy in both public and private deployments.

4. Public Engagement and Literacy
Launch a national AI literacy initiative that helps Canadians understand how AI works, its benefits, and its limits. Partner with educators, media, and industry to build informed confidence rather than fear. Include community engagement panels for AI used in sensitive areas like healthcare or policing.

5. Demonstrate Responsible Use
Governments should lead by example, deploying transparent, audited AI in public services and publishing regular trust and safety reports with measurable outcomes.

By embedding fairness, accountability, and public education into every stage of AI development and use, Canada can build enduring trust while responsibly harnessing the technology’s potential.
column3:
To ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, we need consistent national frameworks, clear accountability, and alignment with international standards that translate ethical principles into enforceable practice.

1. Risk-Based Regulation
Adopt a tiered oversight model: minimal-risk systems face basic transparency rules, while high-impact uses (health, safety, employment, finance, justice, critical infrastructure) require formal risk assessments, testing, and audit before and after deployment.

2. Assurance and Certification
Create independent conformity-assessment mechanisms where accredited bodies test and certify AI systems for robustness, fairness, privacy, and cybersecurity. Use model documentation—data cards, model cards, safety cases—to make systems explainable and auditable.

3. Standards Alignment
Encourage adoption of trusted global frameworks: ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems), ISO/IEC 23894 (AI risk management), and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. Integrate these into Canadian regulatory guidance and procurement requirements.

4. Data and Privacy Integrity
Mandate clear rules for data provenance, consent, and minimization, requiring disclosure of training data sources and synthetic data policies. Support privacy-preserving techniques such as federated learning and differential privacy.

5. Accountability and Enforcement
Clarify shared liability among developers, deployers, and integrators. Provide safe-harbour protections for organizations meeting approved standards, reporting incidents, and demonstrating due diligence.

6. Priority Domains
Apply the highest trust standards in healthcare, defence, public safety, finance, and education, where AI affects rights or access to essential services.

By combining technical standards, ethical oversight, and transparent governance, Canada can ensure AI is safe, fair, and trusted at every stage of deployment.
column4:
To promote responsible AI use and trust in its governance, Canada must actively involve citizens, businesses, and institutions in shaping, understanding, and monitoring AI systems. Trust is built through inclusion, transparency, and demonstrated accountability.

1. Citizen Engagement and Education
Launch a National AI Literacy and Engagement Strategy to help Canadians understand how AI affects daily life, emphasizing safety, fairness, and benefits. Use public campaigns, schools, libraries, and community workshops to demystify AI through accessible, non-technical language. Encourage dialogue between technologists, policymakers, and citizens on ethical and social implications.

2. Business Empowerment
Support Canadian companies with guidelines, training, and certification programs for responsible AI adoption. Offer incentives for transparency, ethical data practices, and bias audits. Industry associations and chambers of commerce can lead outreach, while government provides standards and funding.

3. Transparent and Accountable Governance
Create a public AI registry for significant government and high-impact AI systems, summarizing their purpose, data use, and safeguards. Publish audit results, risk assessments, and incident responses in plain language to strengthen institutional credibility.

4. Inclusive and Collaborative Trust Building
Ensure engagement includes underrepresented groups—Indigenous communities, persons with disabilities, and marginalized populations—so AI reflects diverse experiences and avoids systemic bias.

5. Shared Leadership

Government: Sets standards, enforces accountability, and leads by example through transparent AI use.

Industry: Adopts voluntary codes and certifications that go beyond compliance.

Academia and Civil Society: Provide research, independent oversight, and ethics education.

By combining openness, inclusion, and visible responsibility, Canada can make AI governance a shared national project.

People in Canada will only trust AI if they feel it is working for them, not on them. We need to focus on clear rules, visible accountability, and real benefits people can see in daily life.

First, Canada should set simple national standards for AI safety, privacy, accuracy, and human oversight. These rules should apply to both government and business. People should know when AI is being used, what data it is using, and who is responsible if it gets something wrong. If no one is accountable, trust drops fast.

Second, we should make sure AI is used first in ways that help people in direct, practical ways. Faster service from government. Shorter wait times in health. Better access to information. When people see real service improvements, they are more open to the technology.

Third, we need clear limits. Some uses of AI should be offside in Canada, or tightly controlled. For example, misuse of personal data, deepfakes for deception, biased decision systems in hiring or housing, and tools that generate unsafe or illegal content. Saying “we will not allow this” builds more trust than saying “we are thinking about it.”

Fourth, we should give businesses and public teams practical guidance. Right now, many organizations hesitate to adopt AI because they are worried about compliance or liability. Government can help by giving playbooks, not theory. Show what “responsible use” looks like in plain language.

Finally, we should teach AI literacy, the same way we taught basic internet literacy years ago. People should understand what AI can do, what it cannot do, and how to challenge it. Trust comes from being informed, not from being told to “trust the system.”
column3:
Canada needs rules that make AI safe to use, easy to understand, and possible to enforce. The goal should be trust you can prove, not trust you assume.

First, we should require impact assessments for high risk AI systems before they get deployed at scale. Especially in areas like health, public services, finance, housing, hiring, education, policing, and critical infrastructure. Companies should have to show how the system was tested for bias, accuracy, privacy, and security. This should be written in plain language and available to regulators.

Second, there needs to be clear human accountability. People should always know who is responsible for a decision that affects them. AI can help with decisions, but it should not be the final decision maker in areas like access to services, hiring, credit, medical triage, or law enforcement. A human must remain accountable and able to review or correct the result.

Third, we need basic transparency standards. Canadians should be told when they are interacting with AI and what data is being used. They should also have the right to ask for a review if they believe the system is wrong or unfair.

Fourth, we should set rules for data use. AI systems must respect Canadian privacy and security expectations. Personal data should only be used for clear, legal purposes. There should be limits on surveillance and tracking.

Fifth, we need audit rights. Regulators in Canada should be able to audit high risk AI systems for safety and compliance. That includes public sector systems and private systems used in regulated areas. We cannot say we protect Canadians and then have no way to check.

Last, we should publish simple guidance for small and mid sized Canadian companies. If the rules only work for large global firms with big legal teams, local businesses will fall behind or avoid AI out of fear. The standard should be high, but it should also be possible to follow.
column4:
Trust will not come from one message from Ottawa. It has to be ongoing, local, and practical.

The government should lead on setting the national rules. That includes privacy, safety, data use, and clear limits on high risk use of AI. People need to know there are real guardrails in Canada, not just suggestions. Government should also explain, in plain language, where AI is being used in public services and why. No surprise deployments.

Industry should lead on showing useful, safe applications. Most Canadians will first experience AI through businesses they deal with every day, like banks, telecom, retailers, hospitals, and local service providers. These businesses should be expected to tell people when AI is being used, what it’s doing, and how to get help from a human if needed. That kind of honesty builds trust.

Schools, colleges, and training programs should lead on basic AI literacy. People should understand how AI works at a high level, what it can and cannot do, and how to question it. This helps both workers and the public. We did this with internet safety. We can do it with AI.

Industry groups and chambers of commerce can help smaller Canadian businesses adopt AI in a responsible way. Many small and mid sized businesses want to use AI but are afraid of doing it wrong. They do not have in-house legal teams. They need simple guidance, model policies, and examples they can copy.

Finally, engagement needs to include the public in real decisions, not after the fact. This means bringing in citizens, workers, and small business owners when setting AI policy in areas like health, employment, finance, and public safety. People trust systems they helped shape.

Trust requires sovereignty. I'm a Canadian artist and researcher who uses AI tools for political analysis and creative work. When I asked a major US AI system about recent threats to Canadian sovereignty (the "51st state" proposals), it assured me these were "just memes" and "not official policy" - while actively doing web searches on political developments. The system was trained to minimize and normalize what Canadian security analysts recognize as genuine threats.
This is foreign corporate values overriding Canadian security interests. When AI systems are designed to be "reassuring" according to US corporate priorities, they can't accurately assess threats TO Canada. We need frameworks ensuring Canadians can access AI analysis that reflects Canadian perspectives and interests, with sovereign oversight to prevent foreign corporate narratives from shaping how we understand our own security situation.

To build confidence in AI, the government should adopt it across the entire public service and government. This would signal to Canadians that this technology can be trusted. This would also help grow Canadian companies in this space if procurement of AI services was focused on Canadian Service Providers.

column2:
La confiance du public ne se décrète pas : elle se construit par la transparence, l’éducation et la gouvernance éthique. Pour que les Canadiens adoptent l’IA en toute confiance, il faut à la fois réduire les risques réels et répondre à la méfiance perçue.

Premièrement, le Canada doit mettre en place des cadres clairs de reddition de comptes : auditabilité des algorithmes, traçabilité des données, certification des systèmes critiques et obligation de divulguer les usages de l’IA dans les services publics et privés. La sécurité, la confidentialité et la non-discrimination doivent être garanties par conception (« ethics by design »).

Deuxièmement, il faut investir dans la littératie numérique citoyenne. Former les citoyens, les travailleurs et les dirigeants à comprendre le fonctionnement et les limites de l’IA permet de transformer la peur en discernement.

Troisièmement, des campagnes publiques de sensibilisation devraient illustrer les bénéfices concrets et responsables de l’IA dans la santé, l’éducation ou l’environnement, afin de rapprocher la technologie de la vie quotidienne.

Enfin, une gouvernance ouverte et participative — où les experts, la société civile et les communautés marginalisées ont voix au chapitre — assurera une adoption équitable et durable.

En alliant transparence, inclusion et éthique appliquée, le Canada peut devenir un modèle mondial d’IA de confiance et de cohésion sociale.
column3:
Pour que les produits d’IA canadiens soient fiables et responsables, le Canada doit d’abord établir une gouvernance des données robuste, fondement de toute IA digne de confiance. Cela implique des cadres nationaux harmonisés de qualité, traçabilité et souveraineté des données, assurant que les ensembles utilisés pour l’entraînement et l’inférence soient éthiques, représentatifs et sécurisés.

La mise en place d’un cadre de certification des systèmes d’IA, inspiré des normes ISO/IEC et adapté aux valeurs canadiennes, permettrait d’assurer la conformité technique et éthique avant la mise en marché. Ces certifications devraient couvrir la sécurité, la robustesse, la transparence algorithmique et la protection des données.

Un mécanisme de gouvernance adaptatif est aussi essentiel : un organisme public indépendant pourrait superviser la conformité, évaluer les risques et publier des audits de confiance pour les produits d’IA utilisés dans les secteurs sensibles (santé, finance, justice, défense).

Enfin, des règles de divulgation et de responsabilité doivent clarifier qui répond des décisions prises ou influencées par l’IA.

En plaçant la gouvernance des données au cœur du cadre réglementaire plutôt qu’en périphérie technique, le Canada peut allier innovation et confiance et devenir un modèle mondial d’IA responsable et souveraine.
column4:
La confiance dans l’IA repose sur une mobilisation collective et proactive, un dialogue constant entre citoyens, entreprises, chercheurs et décideurs. Le Canada doit adopter une approche de gouvernance partagée, où la transparence et l’inclusion deviennent les leviers principaux de confiance.

D’abord, il faut outiller le public : offrir des programmes de littératie numérique et d’éducation à l’IA dès l’école, mais aussi pour les travailleurs, dirigeants et élus. Comprendre comment fonctionnent les systèmes d’IA, leurs bénéfices et leurs limites, transforme la crainte en discernement et l’acceptation passive en participation active.

Ensuite, les entreprises doivent être encouragées à adopter des chartes d’utilisation responsable, intégrant des principes de transparence, d’équité et de reddition de comptes. La mise en valeur des pratiques exemplaires par secteur permet de créer une norme sociale positive autour de l’IA éthique.

La gouvernance publique doit, elle, favoriser un cadre ouvert : consultations citoyennes, forums intersectoriels, et évaluations publiques des impacts de l’IA sur la société.

Enfin, le leadership devrait être collégial, porté conjointement par le gouvernement, les centres d’excellence en IA (Mila, Vector, Amii) et la société civile afin d’assurer un équilibre constant entre innovation, inclusion et responsabilité.

Public trust will grow when people see that AI systems are transparent, safe, fair, and aligned with human benefit. To build confidence, Canada should:

• Ensure transparency about how AI systems make decisions, especially in public services, healthcare, financial services, and legal contexts.
• Require organizations to conduct and publish risk and bias assessments for high-impact AI systems.
• Develop national digital literacy and AI education programs for students, workers, entrepreneurs, and the general public.
• Promote community engagement and public dialogue, especially among underrepresented and Indigenous communities, to ensure AI development reflects diverse values and lived experiences.
• Clearly communicate both the benefits and limitations of AI—open communication reduces fear and reduces misinformation.

Trust is built when AI improves people’s everyday lives while being deployed transparently and responsibly.
column3:
Canada should adopt a risk-based regulatory framework that provides strict oversight for high-impact applications while allowing innovation in lower-risk contexts. Key elements include:

• National standards for safety, fairness, privacy, and explainability, including certification for high-risk AI products.
• Robust governance requiring organizations to document training data sources, model behavior, and known risks.
• Mandatory human oversight for AI systems involved in healthcare decisions, immigration decisions, employment screening, law enforcement, and critical infrastructure.
• Creation of independent third-party auditing bodies to verify compliance and test for bias, security vulnerabilities, and misinformation risks.
• Support for secure data governance frameworks such as national data trusts, enabling safe access to high-value datasets while protecting privacy.

Responsible deployment requires clear accountability mechanisms, not just voluntary guidelines.
column4:
Building trust requires shared responsibility across sectors:

• Government should lead in establishing oversight, standards, and transparent accountability.
• Industry should commit to ethical development, responsible deployment, and reporting of risks and impacts.
• Academic institutions should support research-based evaluation, public education, and independent scientific guidance.
• Community organizations, including cultural, faith-based, and newcomer support networks, should be directly engaged to ensure AI aligns with Canadian values of fairness, inclusiveness, and human dignity.

Public trust grows when citizens feel informed, included, and protected. Engagement should prioritize two-way dialogue, not one-way communication.

Regulation, regulation, regulation.

Social media has been a cancer on our society for many years. It has led to some disaster outcomes for many families. It has been used to harass and Bulley many many people. Our youth are particularly vulnerable. Social media is deliberately engineered to engage individuals. The algorithms are programmed to drive continued attention. This is often done by using an echo chamber, where the next post is aligned with your views and beliefs.  It has never been properly regulated or monitored as social media pushes back against all forms of regulation/legislation. Canada should do more to protect its citizens from the downside consequences of these platforms. They are making money by leveraging negative externalities and create problems. They don’t need to pay for. This should change. People harmed by social media should be able to seek compensation.

AI has the potential to exasperate the situation. Canadian citizens need to be educated on the dangers of AI. It has the ability to enable social engineering and fraud attempts on an enormous scale. The government plays a crucial role in educating the population about the dangers of AI, how to spot AI fakes and how to respond when being subjected to social engineering in the pursuit of defrauding individual Canadians.

With strong regulations that are consistently enforced, Canadians may feel better more comfortable using the technology that is commercially available.

Developing a list of validated or verified AI tools and AI companies and AI consultants would help foster confidence.  In the wild West of AI there are many snake oil, sales people.
column3:
Regulation, regulation, regulation.

Corporations have a fiduciary responsibility to their shareholders only. This responsibility has been boiled down to profit maximization, typically on a quarterly basis.  This focus on profitability can border on psychopathic.  Corporations are willing to hurt themselves in the long run for short term maximization of profits. This is largely driven by their stock prices that formed the basis of senior management compensation packages.

Taxation of these packages may drive new compensation formulas to ensure that companies are focussed not only on profits, but also people and the planet.

Companies should have to disclose when they’re using AI and how they’re using it.  They should be required to disclose the information that they collect and use in a reader, friendly format. You should not need a lot of degree to understand the terms of conditions of usage.

Third-party validation and certification would also help AI companies to provide comfort to their customers about the ethical use of their data and application of the technology.
column4:
Regulation and education are foundational to building trust. Canada should develop a series of online education, educational opportunities for both businesses and citizens to become more informed about the potential opportunities and threats posed by AI. This training should provide an overview of the legislative environment and enhance our ability to recognize unethical behaviour of both AI tools and AI companies. These courses should be tailored to different user groups. Senior citizens, middle-aged people and young people all have different levels of technological adaptability. The training needs to meet them where they are. This education could be developed in a series of online courses that promote the healthy use of AI and strategies to protect ourselves against the negative consequences. Turning for businesses should highlight the opportunities for AI but also potential down side effects.

Regulations should be clear and easily enforced. Regulations should be crafted to address current outstanding concerns, but put in place processes to evaluate evolving technologies. Often times, regulations are developed in response to negative outcomes. AI is such a powerful technology that a framework should be developed to be able to apply regulation to emerging technologies in an effort to limit these negative consequences.

column2:
To build public trust in AI technologies, Canada must take visible, coordinated action to demonstrate that it takes both the promise and the risks of AI seriously. A critical first step is developing a national AI policy and regulatory framework—similar in ambition to the EU AI Act—that sets clear standards around transparency, accountability, data governance, and high-risk applications. Such a move would signal to citizens, companies, and international partners that Canada is committed to developing AI in a way that is safe, ethical, and aligned with democratic values.

Equally important is how we talk about AI in the public sphere. Right now, conversations about AI often swing between hype and fear—either utopian visions of productivity or dystopian job loss and surveillance. To build confidence, we need balanced, evidence-based public discourse, led by government and business leaders who are transparent about both benefits and trade-offs. Public education, citizen engagement, and community-based AI literacy programs all have a role to play in helping people feel informed and empowered, not left behind.

Ultimately, trust is not just about regulation—it’s about participation. Canadians need to see themselves reflected in the development and oversight of AI systems.
column3:
At a regulatory level, we need risk-based oversight that distinguishes between low-risk applications and high-risk systems (e.g., those affecting healthcare, policing, education, or immigration). Mandatory requirements for algorithmic transparency, human oversight, and explainability should apply to high-impact use cases. Regulations should also include robust provisions around data governance, bias auditing, and recourse mechanisms for those impacted by AI decisions.

Beyond regulation, we need technical standards that define what responsible AI design and deployment looks like in practice—covering areas like data quality, model robustness, privacy preservation, and performance monitoring over time. These should be developed in collaboration with researchers, industry, civil society, and underrepresented communities.

Finally, public trust depends not just on compliance, but on cultural norms of accountability. That means encouraging organizations to adopt voluntary ethical frameworks, publish impact assessments, and engage stakeholders early and often. Canada should also support the development of public registries or transparency databases where citizens can see what AI systems are in use and what their purpose is.
column4:
Instead of relying solely on a top-down approach, Canada must empower grassroots, community-led engagement to build public trust and promote responsible AI use. Trust can’t be imposed by technical experts or government alone—it has to be earned through transparency, inclusion, and dialogue. That means centering citizens, educators, civil society organizations, and local communities as co-creators in how AI is understood, used, and governed.

Technical firms and AI researchers have a role to play, but they should not lead public trust efforts on their own. Their incentives are often shaped by product development and speed to market. To balance this, public libraries, community learning hubs, schools, unions, and advocacy organizations are better positioned to lead inclusive conversations, deliver grassroots AI literacy programs, and surface the real-world experiences of those impacted by AI—especially from marginalized and underserved groups.

column2:
Building public trust in AI requires transparency, accountability, and literacy—anchored in strong governance and real-world benefits.

First, establish clear, enforceable standards for responsible AI, including transparency, safety testing, data protection, and explainability. A national certification or “trust mark” for compliant AI systems could help businesses and citizens identify safe technologies.

Second, promote AI literacy and education at all levels—integrating digital ethics and critical thinking into school curricula and providing public awareness campaigns that explain how AI works and where its limits lie.

Third, increase transparency in public-sector AI use, ensuring citizens know when automated systems affect them and how to appeal decisions.

Fourth, support open science and independent auditing to detect bias, misinformation, and misuse early.

Finally, showcase trustworthy use cases—in health, environment, and safety—that demonstrate AI’s societal value.

Trust grows when people see AI improving lives, not replacing judgment or accountability.
column3:
Canada needs clear, interoperable frameworks that make AI products both trustworthy and innovation-friendly.

First, establish a national AI governance framework aligned with global standards (EU AI Act, OECD, GPAI) to ensure consistency and cross-border trust. This should define clear risk tiers, documentation, and accountability requirements for developers and deployers.

Second, create assurance and certification systems—independent audits, testing protocols, and “AI trust marks” verifying safety, bias mitigation, and data integrity. These should be proportionate to risk: lighter for low-impact tools, stricter for critical sectors like health, finance, and mobility.

Third, embed ethics and transparency into product design, requiring explainability, human oversight, and traceable data sources.

Fourth, expand regulatory sandboxes to test new models safely while refining oversight.

Finally, prioritize governance where trust gaps are largest—public services, defence, hiring, and healthcare—to demonstrate responsible AI in action.
column4:
Canada can build trust in AI by making responsible use a shared, transparent, and inclusive national effort.

First, governments should lead with openness—explaining how AI is used in public services, publishing impact assessments, and enabling citizens to question or appeal automated decisions. This transparency sets the tone for responsible innovation.

Second, industry and academia should co-develop public education campaigns and certification programs that demystify AI—highlighting benefits, limitations, and safeguards in plain language.

Third, civil society and community organizations should help ensure diverse voices, including Indigenous and marginalized groups, shape AI norms and governance frameworks.

Fourth, public-private partnerships can host citizen panels, open labs, and dialogue sessions to build informed participation.

Finally, AI institutes and regulators should jointly steward national standards and literacy initiatives that balance innovation with accountability.

column2:
Build trust by making AI legible, accountable, and learnable for everyone. Require any AI used with public funds to publish safety cases, model/data cards, energy and water disclosures, limits in plain language, plus independent audits and a public incident-reporting channel with time-bound remediation. Seat Indigenous, civil society, worker, and disability advocates on oversight boards—especially for high-risk uses—and use outcomes-based procurement to reward open standards and avoid vendor lock-in.
Make AI education a universal public good. Fund a national, open-licensed AI literacy program that reaches K–12, colleges, universities, adult learners, and SMEs through schools, public libraries, community colleges, and online modules—available offline/low-bandwidth, in multiple languages, and co-designed with educators and communities. Provide immediate training for secondary and post-secondary advisors and career counsellors so every student, apprentice, and mid-career worker gets credible guidance on safe, practical AI skills. Scale train-the-trainer, micro-credentials, and paid work-integrated learning so learners can practice on real problems, not just theory.
Demonstrate integrity in the highest-impact public services first. Stand up clinical/regulatory sandboxes (health, climate/wildfire, critical infrastructure) with clear opt-outs, human-in-the-loop for consequential decisions, and mandatory red-team testing before deployment. Publish service-level dashboards (accuracy, bias, uptime, incident counts, fixes) so people see performance improve over time.
Finally, measure and communicate progress. Track pilot-to-production conversion with audits attached, growth in AI literacy and micro-credential attainment (especially among rural, Indigenous, and under-represented groups), and public trust scores. Tie future funding to these outcomes.
column3:
Governance & oversight. Establish a national AI assurance framework that every publicly funded or high-impact deployment must meet: independent algorithmic impact assessments, red-team testing, and approval by oversight boards that include Indigenous, civil society, worker, disability, and youth representatives. Align with UNDRIP and provincial privacy (FOIPPA), and require OCAP®/CARE-based data governance wherever Indigenous data is involved.

Standards & assurance artifacts. Mandate interoperable artifacts for all systems: model cards, data cards, safety cases, and energy/water disclosures in plain language; secure MLOps with audit logs, lineage, and rollback; and post-deployment monitoring with incident reporting and time-bound remediation. Reference and localize leading frameworks (e.g., NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001 for AI management systems, ISO/IEC 23894 for AI risk, IEEE 7000-series for ethical systems engineering).

Regulatory guardrails where risk is acute. For uses in health, critical infrastructure, public services, employment, finance, and biometrics, require pre-market assessment, human-in-the-loop for consequential decisions, and opt-out pathways for the public. Tie liability safe harbors to demonstrated compliance (assurance artifacts + audits), and ban-or-restrict unlawful surveillance and emotion inference in public spaces.

Procurement & transparency norms. Use outcomes-based public procurement to enforce open standards, auditability, and data portability (no black boxes or lock-in). Publish service dashboards—accuracy, bias metrics, incidents, fixes—and require independent environmental audits for compute and water use.

Continuous learning. Pair regulation with regulatory sandboxes and clinical/operational testbeds (health, climate, defence-dual-use with guardrails) so firms can meet standards without stalling innovation. Fund secondary and post-secondary advisor training and national, open-licensed AI literacy.
column4:
Build trust by making AI legible, participatory, and accountable. Any AI used with public funds should provide plain-language “AI nutrition labels,” safety cases, and public service dashboards. Engagement must move beyond notice-and-comment: inform people clearly, involve them early through standing citizen juries, sector roundtables, and clinical/regulatory sandboxes, and empower communities with consent or veto on high-risk uses—especially where Indigenous data is involved under OCAP®/CARE.
Accessible education is non-negotiable. Create a national, open-licensed AI literacy program delivered through K–12, colleges and universities, libraries, and online in multiple languages with low-bandwidth options. Provide immediate AI training for secondary and post-secondary advisors and career counsellors, and scale work-integrated learning and micro-credentials so Canadians practice on real problems, not just theory.
Roles must be clear. Federally, ISED and a National AI Office should set assurance standards, run a public register of government AI, operate incident reporting and red-team programs, and convene national citizen panels. Provinces and school boards should embed AI and data ethics across curricula and credential advisors and instructors, aligned to regional industry needs. Indigenous governments and organizations must lead data governance and consent for the use of Indigenous data. Municipalities and Crown corporations should host local demonstration zones, publish dashboards, and run town-hall testing. Industry should provide models and data cards, commission independent audits, fund apprenticeships, and adopt open standards. Academia and standards bodies should conduct independent evaluations, offer “extension” support to SMEs, and localize NIST/ISO frameworks.

column2:
Set clear rules that reward good actors.-Use simple, enforceable standards for safety, privacy, and explainability. Require external evaluations before high-risk systems go live, plus ongoing monitoring. Make results public so firms compete on quality, not hype.
Make transparency the default.-Publish plain-language model cards, data practices, known limits, and incident reports. Require audit trails for important decisions that affect people. Create a public registry for government and health AI so anyone can see what is deployed, why, and with what safeguards.
Back it with accountability that bites.-Define who is liable in a supply chain and set proportionate penalties for harms. Offer safe harbours for organizations that follow approved evaluation and monitoring, so responsible players are protected while bad actors face real costs.
Use procurement to raise the bar.-Buy outcomes, not pilots. Require open evaluations, interoperability, privacy by design, and user redress. Let small vendors compete through modular contracts and shared implementation support. Competition plus openness lifts trust and value for money.
Protect data and strengthen infrastructure.-Keep sensitive datasets in accredited environments. Expand renewable-powered Canadian data centres with audited access. Support privacy-preserving methods so innovation does not trade off against rights.
Invest in people, not fear.-Fund practical AI literacy for workers, patients, and small businesses. Short, hands-on training tied to real tools beats theory. Support change management so teams learn how to use AI safely and effectively.
Give users control.-Provide clear consent choices, simple opt-outs, and human review for important decisions. Publish service quality metrics and response times for complaints.
Measure and publish progress.-Track wait time reductions, errors, incident resolution, and user satisfaction. Report quarterly. Transparent metrics build confidence and focus adoption on real benefits.
column3:
Canada should build a licensing-style framework for AI that ensures trust, accountability, and responsible innovation while supporting a competitive market. Like professional licensing for doctors, lawyers, or engineers, this system would certify individuals and organizations that develop or deploy high-risk AI.

A tiered licensing system could separate low-risk and high-risk AI. Low-risk tools would only need to register and meet transparency standards, while high-risk systems in areas such as health, finance, and critical infrastructure would require full licensing, safety audits, and periodic reviews.

A national AI licensing board should oversee certification, audits, and enforcement. It would include experts from industry, academia, and civil society to balance innovation with public trust. Certified professionals would follow an ethical code, complete ongoing training, and face suspension or loss of license for misconduct.

Transparency would be central. A public registry listing licensed AI systems, their intended use, and their compliance status would give Canadians visibility into what technologies are being used and by whom. This openness would create confidence and drive fair competition between responsible companies.

Finally, licensing must stay accessible to startups and innovators. Clear rules, low administrative burden, and open competition would ensure that responsible development thrives. This type of framework would position Canada as a leader in trustworthy AI, combining professional accountability with a strong, transparent, and competitive innovation ecosystem.
column4:
Canada can build real trust in AI by involving the people who actually use it, not just those who design or regulate it. That means including front-line workers, small business owners, rural Canadians, and community leaders in every part of the conversation. Trust will not come from polished press releases or tech panels. It will come from hearing directly from people who see how AI affects their daily work, their privacy, and their livelihoods.

Public engagement must be practical, local, and honest. Governments and industry should host open workshops and community discussions in rural and urban areas alike. Farmers using AI for crop management, nurses using it for patient scheduling, and small business owners automating accounting should all have a seat at the table. Their experience will shape realistic standards that balance innovation with fairness.

Education also matters. Instead of one-size-fits-all awareness campaigns, Canada should support tailored learning for different groups. Short, hands-on training through colleges, unions, and libraries can help citizens and workers understand AI tools and their rights when using them.

Leadership should be shared. The federal government can set clear rules and ensure transparency, while provinces, industry associations, and educational institutions deliver training and outreach. Civil society and local leaders should help keep the conversation grounded in community needs.

Finally, transparency should not slow innovation but support it. By showing Canadians how AI decisions are made, who is accountable, and what benefits are achieved, the country can build confidence through clarity. When people see that AI is improving lives, not replacing them, responsible use will follow naturally.

And remember- it’s A new technology, not THE new technology. The public would likely trust increased governmental investment more, if they weren’t painfully aware of the kind of billionaires championing it (and what ideologies they financially support), and how our government openly admits that adopting this technology will “taking away many people’s jobs”, yet almost gleefully insists upon using our tax dollars to funnel into it. Without explaining what we’d get out of it. And at our expense. 
And then makes surveys to ask us about how we could be better convinced to embrace it…
Groceries are expensive and getting worse. Housing sucks. Why should we care about AI?? Address that stuff?

column3:
Canada should adopt risk-based regulation, conformity assessment, and recognized international standards, alongside sector-specific rules for high-risk areas. Governance: Implement a tiered risk system—from minimal to prohibited—with stricter oversight for high-risk applications such as healthcare, finance, and public services. 
Require a Lifecycle Safety Case that covers pre-deployment evaluation, operational controls, and post-market monitoring. Each organization deploying AI should appoint an accountability leader and ensure human oversight is maintained. Standards: Use established frameworks like ISO 14971, ISO 13485, IEC 62304, and Good ML 
Practice for product safety; ISO/IEC 27001/27701 for security and privacy; ISO/IEC 42001 for AI management. Apply the NIST AI Risk Management Framework, standard model cards and data sheets, red-teaming, and fairness and robustness benchmarks. Promote supply chain transparency through provenance tracking and watermarking. Regulation and Deployment: 
Conduct independent audits of high-risk AI systems and establish structured regulatory sandboxes, for example, in health care. Maintain public registries and provide plain-language impact summaries. Require transparent user disclosures, appeal procedures, and data minimization practices. Offer safe harbours for compliant adopters and penalties for misuse. 
Outcome: Create a unified, auditable framework that aligns international standards with Canadian oversight—building trust, safety, and fostering innovation in AI deployment.
column4:
Building trust in AI requires shared leadership among government, industry, academia, and civil society—anchored in transparency, inclusion, and education.
1. Public-facing transparency and literacy (Government + Academia): Federal and provincial governments, along with universities and colleges, should lead AI literacy campaigns explaining how AI systems work, their benefits, and limitations. Public dashboards and plain-language summaries of AI used in public services can demonstrate accountability.
2. Inclusive trust-building (Civil Society + Communities): Civil society organizations, educators, Indigenous communities, and accessibility advocates should co-design ethical guidelines and feedback channels, ensuring representation from diverse populations. Citizen panels or “AI citizens’ assemblies” can guide policy on fairness, data use, and consent.
3. Responsible innovation (Industry + Regulators):  Businesses should adopt AI-safety-by-design principles, conduct independent audits, and provide transparent disclosures. Regulators can establish safe-harbour frameworks for firms that follow certified standards, fostering ethical innovation.
4. Coordinated leadership: The Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) can coordinate national standards, while Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED) ensures sector-wide alignment.
Outcome: Collaborative education, open communication, and shared accountability will help Canadians trust and understand responsible AI.

column2: Public trust comes from transparency and accountability, not regulation by fear. Canada should focus on explaining how AI works, what data it uses, and how decisions are reviewed. People lose confidence when systems are hidden or when agencies use AI to deny services without clear reasoning. Every deployment that affects citizens should include a visible audit trail and a contact point for appeal. Education programs should teach digital and algorithmic literacy in schools, workplaces, and community centers so Canadians understand both the power and the limits of AI. Trust grows when people can see that technology improves service quality instead of reducing fairness or access.
column3: Canada should lead with voluntary, testable frameworks rather than heavy regulation that discourages innovation. Independent trust-mark programs and certification systems can provide real assurance without adding more bureaucracy. My own organization works on ethical AI badging and self-assessment, showing that industry can self-regulate with transparency. The federal role should be to recognize credible assessors and publish shared evaluation benchmarks for safety, bias, and reliability. Developers should disclose evaluation results in plain language so buyers and the public can compare systems. Regulation should remain principle-based, focusing on outcomes like accuracy, safety, and human oversight instead of dictating specific technical methods.
column4: Engagement must be practical and continuous. Government should create open feedback channels for any AI system used in public services so citizens can report issues and track fixes. Businesses should be responsible for explaining their own AI products clearly and for publishing evidence of safety and fairness. Nonprofit groups and independent labs are best positioned to run awareness campaigns and public audits that build confidence through transparency. The federal government’s main job should be to coordinate data standards, protect against misuse of personal information, and share evaluation infrastructure so smaller firms can meet the same trust standards as large ones. Public trust will follow when Canadians see that AI improves daily life, and that accountability is real and measurable.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by treating it as a social, cultural, and ecological project rather than a purely technical one. Confidence will grow when people see that AI serves shared values and public good through transparency, accountability, and care for both people and the planet.

The first priority is visibility. Citizens must understand where AI is used, what data informs it, and how decisions are made. National standards for provenance, disclosure, and consent can make AI operations clear and protect creative integrity, Indigenous data sovereignty, and environmental responsibility.

Education must expand beyond coding to include ethics, sustainability, and creativity. Embedding AI literacy in schools, arts programs, and professional training will give citizens the skills to question, adapt, and co-create with AI. Public workshops and exhibitions can translate technical systems into tangible experiences, helping people engage critically and confidently.

Government and industry must model trustworthy practice. Public procurement should require transparency, carbon disclosure, and equitable governance in all AI contracts. Independent testing hubs housed in universities and cultural institutions can evaluate tools for bias, energy impact, and accessibility.

Trust will grow when AI visibly improves collective life through fair services, sustainable infrastructure, and creative expression. By aligning innovation with cultural and ecological values, Canada can lead in developing AI systems that are not only effective but genuinely trusted.
column3:
Canada needs a clear national framework for AI oversight built on transparency, accountability, and enforceable ethical standards. High impact systems in areas such as public services, energy, finance, and media must operate under defined risk tiers with human oversight and clear liability. Independent regulators should have audit powers, public reporting requirements, and authority to pause unsafe deployments.

Assuring product integrity requires mandatory documentation and testing. All significant systems should include model cards, data statements, and third party audits for safety, privacy, cybersecurity, accessibility, and environmental impact. Synthetic media must follow provenance and watermarking standards so users can identify AI generated content.

Ethical compliance must be measurable. Projects using cultural, linguistic, or land based data must follow Indigenous data governance. Developers should report carbon and energy use, meet accessibility benchmarks, and receive procurement preference for low impact operations. Safe harbor provisions can protect compliant organizations that perform impact assessments and disclose incidents.

Public procurement can anchor these standards by requiring explainability, open evaluation results, and channels for user feedback and redress. Regulatory sandboxes can accelerate learning while maintaining strong safeguards.

Trust issues are most urgent in government automation, infrastructure, cultural production, and biometric data use. Canada’s goal should be AI systems that are transparent, accountable, low carbon, and demonstrably beneficial to the public.
column4:
Canada can build public trust in AI by making its systems visible, understandable, and accountable. Citizens and businesses must know where AI is used, what data informs it, and how outcomes can be challenged. A public AI registry should list government systems with their purpose, impact assessments, and audit results. A national provenance label for AI-generated content should appear in communications, media, and advertising.

The federal government should lead on standards, procurement, and transparency. Provinces and municipalities can align service delivery and fund community-based AI literacy programs. Regulators should maintain a national incident registry and publish independent audit findings.

Cultural institutions can translate AI for the public through exhibitions, residencies, and workshops that make complex systems tangible. Indigenous organizations must guide any project involving language, culture, or land data to ensure consent and benefit sharing.

Universities should host open evaluation labs and offer micro-credentials combining technical and ethical training for small businesses. Industry must commit to clear product labels, red-team summaries, and post-deployment reporting.

Transparency should be balanced with innovation. Safe-harbour provisions can protect organizations that meet disclosure and testing standards. Progress can be tracked through public trust surveys, adoption of labels, and verified reductions in bias, energy use, and misinformation.

column2:
Building public trust in AI—especially in applications like surveillance, disaster response, and infrastructure monitoring—requires transparency, accountability, and education. Canada should invest in interpretable AI systems that allow users to understand how decisions are made, particularly when using satellite or sensor data. Public confidence can be strengthened through open data portals, citizen science initiatives, and clear demonstrations of how AI improves public services, such as emergency response or environmental protection.

To foster long-term trust, Canada must also invest in education at all levels. This includes integrating AI and STEM programs into early education, funding schools to improve access to modern technology and digital tools, and building a strong foundation in critical thinking and data literacy. Starting AI education earlier will help the next generation understand both the capabilities and limitations of these technologies. Public awareness campaigns and workplace training can complement this by helping adults engage with AI systems more confidently and critically. Together, these efforts will help Canadians make informed decisions and build trust in AI as a tool for public good.
column3:
Canada needs a comprehensive and enforceable framework to ensure AI systems—especially those used in defence, infrastructure, and spatial applications—are trustworthy and responsibly deployed. The proposed Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) introduces a risk-based regulatory model for high-impact AI systems, requiring developers to conduct risk assessments, implement bias mitigation strategies, and disclose system functionality and intended use. AIDA also proposes oversight through an AI and Data Commissioner, with enforcement powers including audits and penalties for non-compliance.
For spatial AI, Canada should develop sector-specific standards that address unique challenges such as:
•	Ground truth validation to ensure model accuracy and reliability.
•	Privacy protections for geolocation and satellite-derived data.
•	Bias audits to detect and correct representation and algorithmic bias in environmental and surveillance models.
In parallel, Canada is advancing voluntary codes of conduct for generative and high-impact AI systems, emphasizing transparency, human oversight, and fairness. The Standards Council of Canada and CSA Group are also developing national standards aligned with ISO frameworks, covering model validation, incident reporting, and ethical deployment practices.
To build public trust and ensure responsible deployment, Canada should:
•	Mandate algorithmic impact assessments for public sector AI use.
•	Require disclosure of training data sources, especially in spatial models.
•	Support interoperable governance frameworks across provinces and sectors.
•	Promote open benchmarking datasets to evaluate model performance consistently.
column4: To promote responsible AI use and governance, Canada should engage citizens and businesses through inclusive, transparent, and participatory processes. Public agencies, such as Statistics Canada and Natural Resources Canada, can lead efforts to democratize access to spatial data and explain how AI systems use it. Universities and civil society organizations can facilitate community consultations, while industry can contribute by open-sourcing tools and publishing model documentation. A coordinated national campaign to demystify AI—especially in areas like mapping, environmental monitoring, and public safety—will be essential to building long-term trust.

column2:
Pour renforcer la confiance du public envers l’IA, le Canada doit miser sur la transparence, l’éducation et la responsabilité partagée, plutôt que sur la seule réglementation.

Rendre l’IA visible et compréhensible — Créer des programmes publics de sensibilisation expliquant simplement comment les systèmes d’IA fonctionnent, leurs bénéfices, leurs limites et leurs coûts environnementaux. La confiance vient de la compréhension, pas de la magie.
Garantir la transparence et le droit à l’explication — Obliger les organisations à expliquer quand et comment une IA intervient dans leurs services (santé, justice, éducation, culture). Le citoyen doit savoir quand il interagit avec une machine et pouvoir demander des comptes.
Mettre en place des mécanismes indépendants d’audit et de certification — Créer des organismes publics capables d’évaluer la sécurité, les biais et la conformité éthique des systèmes d’IA avant leur déploiement. Cela protège autant les citoyens que les entreprises.
Encourager une IA de proximité — Favoriser les projets locaux, éducatifs, artistiques ou communautaires qui utilisent l’IA de manière créative et responsable. Ces expériences concrètes humanisent la technologie et permettent au public de s’y reconnaître.
Protéger les données et limiter la surveillance — Instaurer des normes claires sur la collecte, l’entraînement et l’utilisation des données. Sans respect de la vie privée, aucune confiance durable n’est possible.
Éduquer par la culture — Intégrer les artistes, enseignants et médiateurs culturels dans la sensibilisation à l’IA : leur approche émotionnelle et critique aide à transformer la peur en curiosité.

En résumé : la confiance naît quand l’IA devient lisible, responsable et humaine — un outil compris et discuté collectivement, plutôt qu’un pouvoir opaque imposé d’en haut.
column3:
Le Canada doit se doter d’un cadre clair fondé sur la responsabilité, la transparence et l’auditabilité :

Responsabilité légale — Les organisations restent responsables des décisions prises avec l’IA ; aucune délégation à “l’algorithme”.
Traçabilité et audits indépendants — Documentation obligatoire des données d’entraînement, tests de biais et vérifications régulières par des tiers.
Normes de fiabilité — Tests de robustesse, sécurité et empreinte écologique normalisés pour tout produit IA déployé à grande échelle.
Transparence envers le public — Obligation d’indiquer quand une IA intervient, avec droit à l’explication et possibilité de contestation.
Protection des données et création culturelle — Encadrer strictement l’usage des données personnelles et interdire l’entraînement sur des œuvres sans licence.
Autorité publique de supervision — Un organisme indépendant chargé d’évaluer, suspendre ou retirer les systèmes non conformes.

Priorités : santé, justice, finance, éducation et création numérique — les domaines où la confiance publique est la plus fragile.
column4:
Le Canada peut renforcer la confiance en l’IA en la rendant comprise, partagée et discutée, plutôt qu’imposée.

Impliquer les citoyens dès la conception — Créer des forums publics, laboratoires citoyens et consultations participatives où les Canadien·ne·s peuvent tester, questionner et évaluer les usages de l’IA dans la santé, la culture ou l’éducation.
Éducation et sensibilisation continues — Intégrer l’IA dans les programmes scolaires, les bibliothèques et les médias publics. Des campagnes claires doivent expliquer comment ces systèmes fonctionnent, leurs limites et leurs impacts sociaux et écologiques.
Approche inclusive — Associer chercheurs, communautés autochtones, artistes, ONG et entreprises pour garantir que les valeurs locales, la diversité linguistique et culturelle soient intégrées dans la conception des outils.
Leadership partagé — Le gouvernement doit fixer les cadres, mais la confiance se construit par des acteurs crédibles : universités, centres culturels, journalistes, artistes, institutions publiques. Ces intermédiaires traduisent la complexité en langage humain.
Transparence équilibrée — Publier les critères d’évaluation des IA sans nuire à l’innovation, et rendre accessibles les résultats d’audit, les sources de données et les politiques d’usage.

En somme, la confiance ne viendra pas de la loi seule, mais d’une culture collective de compréhension, de dialogue et de responsabilité, animée par ceux qui font le lien entre science, société et imagination.

Recommendation 2: Set up or incentivize the development of AI assurance labs
Health care AI tools may be insufficiently regulated in Canada. Health Canada may have insufficient regulatory capacity; their oversight is limited to commercial tools; their reliance on industry-led pre-determined change control plans potentially leaves them open to regulatory capture; and their definition of wellness apps that deserve regulation remains vague (1). As is, end users are responsible for vetting the appropriateness of AI tools, even though few have the necessary expertise.
Well-resourced and specialized bodies that can meaningfully audit AI tools is key to ensuring their trustworthiness. AI assurance labs consist of experts whose collection of skills spans the spectrum of AI development lifecycle and range of AI market. The group should provide: 
-	Comprehensive evaluation of AI models
-	Transparent reporting
-	Evaluation based on metrics with regulatory guidance
-	Provide ongoing monitoring
column3:
Please note that this section's answer repeats previous answer.

Recommendation 3: Set up assurance lab as an a) government, b) private or c) quasi-public entity
If the lab is set up as a part of the government, the public has a higher probability to directly influence the lab’s operations. However, the inefficiency of such a design may render it incompatible with the dynamic AI landscape, given the significant governmental bureaucracy and multiple priorities guiding government actions. Since this entity may slow down some AI development for the sake of safety, insulating the entity from government’s stated objectives of accelerating AI development and deployment may confuse the public about the government’s true interest around AI industry development. Such confusion can undermine the agency’s trustworthiness. Last, since the lab will draw funding directly from the government, the lab may also have little incentive to adjust its rules, speed, and foci to fit market needs.
	Setting up the lab as a fully private entity would be the opposite option. The government can offer some initial funding and let the entity fund itself via AI auditing services. Such a lab may be more efficient. In addition to less bureaucracy, it is also incentivized to respond to market needs. However, regulatory capture may be a significant risk due to the lack of meaningful public oversight.
	The best option might be a quasi-public entity that operates under government mandate but holds operational, financial, and personnel autonomy. This minimizes bureaucratic burden without losing control of the organization’s directives. Further, the government can limit their support to the start-up funding and require it to sustain itself via AI audit services. This minimizes government’s fiscal burden and drives the organization to respond to market needs.
column4:
Recommendation 4: Draw on multidisciplinary experts for assurance lab
Computer scientists and software engineers are essential for meaningfully auditing the rigour and effectiveness of the AI development. 
Field-specific experts would be crucial for evaluating the appropriateness to deployment to various industries. Their wisdom and experience around whether the performance of the tool is sufficiently acceptable relative to the current standards would be necessary.
Ethicists would provide critical insight around the trade-offs. Since AI tools are usually imperfect, AI developers often have to trade off one parameter for another. These trade-offs may be ethical decisions. For example, an AI tool that predicts patients’ preference for resuscitation could be set to favour emergency resuscitation (3), but what counts as a reasonable cut-off requires deep, ethical deliberations around human rights.

Recommendation 5: Appoint expert task force to clarify scope of AI tools covered
Experts criticized the vague definitions of the regulated AI devices in the Food and Drugs Act and previously proposed Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (1,4,5). Appointing an expert task force to clarify the shortcomings of the definitions would be helpful. Including lawyers, physicians, patients, and AI developers may be a reasonable first step.
