column2:
Public trust in AI will grow when people, whether they are patients in health care or customers in other industries, can clearly see benefits, understand how the technology is being used, and know that their information is protected. In health care, this means patients experience shorter wait times, improved access to their providers, and better communication, while remaining confident that physicians continue to guide their care. The same foundation applies in other sectors where transparency, accountability, and human oversight are essential to building confidence.
The biggest risks include privacy breaches, biased or inaccurate outputs, and uncertainty about who is responsible when something goes wrong. People will only trust AI when it is clear that it supports rather than replaces human judgment and leads to real improvements in their experience.
Canada can strengthen confidence by ensuring AI tools are independently evaluated for safety, accuracy, and bias before they are used in care. The results of these evaluations should be shared publicly in accessible language so that people can understand how decisions are made. Patients should always know when AI is being used, how it supports their care, and what protections are in place. Strong privacy safeguards, Canadian data storage, and clear consent processes are essential.
Building trust also means involving patients directly in design, testing, and evaluation. When people are part of shaping the technology, it is more likely to meet their needs and reflect community values. In rural and remote areas, AI can expand access to care through virtual support, language translation, and scheduling tools, but implementation must always be transparent and physician-led.
Public and professional education is key. Working with medical associations, governments can help patients and providers understand both the potential and the limits of AI. Trust will come when people see that AI improves care, reduces admin burdens.
column3:
Canada needs a clear, practical framework that embeds safety, transparency, and equity throughout the AI lifecycle in health care. Governance must assign clear roles for developers, deployers, and clinicians, supported by national oversight and a registry of approved tools with evaluation summaries and contact information. Provincial and territorial medical associations should participate in oversight and incident review.
AI tools should follow a defined assurance pipeline. Pre-market evaluation must include clinical relevance testing, bias and robustness checks, cybersecurity review, and usability testing with community providers. Post-market monitoring should track errors, drift, and equity impacts, with mandatory incident reporting and timely corrective action.
Privacy and data integrity must be ensured through purpose-specific data use, strong access controls, Canadian data residency for health applications, and clear communication to patients about how their information is used. Transparency should include plain-language model documentation, known limitations, and explanations of how AI supports clinical decisions.
Interoperability standards are essential so clinics are not locked into specific vendors. Tools must integrate seamlessly with existing systems using open formats and exportable models. Liability should be clearly defined, with safe-harbour protections for physicians and organizations that comply with evaluation, monitoring, and transparency requirements.
This layered, practical framework, which built on physician leadership and consistent national standards, that will ensure AI in Canada remains safe, ethical, and trusted while improving efficiency and access in community-based care.
column4:
Canada can build lasting trust in AI by making citizens and businesses active participants in how these technologies are developed, explained, and governed. In health care, this means ensuring patients and physicians understand how AI supports care, how data are protected, and how accountability is maintained. The same principle applies across industries: trust comes when people can see that AI serves human goals and reflects shared values.
Public-facing communication must move beyond technical details to focus on what AI means for people’s daily lives. Governments should coordinate a national education strategy that includes public campaigns, community dialogues, and school curricula to improve AI literacy. These efforts should use clear, accessible language that explains both the benefits and limitations of AI, with practical examples of how it improves outcomes safely.
Inclusive approaches to trust building must involve those most affected by AI. In health care, provincial and territorial medical associations, patient groups, and privacy advocates are well placed to lead engagement, co-design tools, and review policies. Industry partners can contribute transparency materials and user-friendly reporting on model performance and data use. Academic institutions can lead independent research and evaluation, while governments set the ethical and regulatory baseline.
Balancing transparency with innovation requires predictable rules that make responsible behaviour the easiest path. Clear standards for data use, disclosure, and oversight build confidence without slowing innovation. By grounding AI governance in collaboration, openness, and shared accountability, Canada can strengthen both public trust and its global reputation for ethical, human-centred innovation.

Diversity, diversity, diversity—this must be the foundation of any body tasked with making decisions, crafting legislation, and executing policy in the age of artificial intelligence. While the formation of Canada’s AI advisory committee is a welcome step, its composition falls short of representing the full spectrum of Canadian society.

Where are the voices of the Black community, Indigenous peoples, LGBTQ+ individuals, sex workers, immigrants, and other structurally marginalized groups? Their absence is not a minor oversight—it is a systemic failure. In 2025, the excuse of “limited mentorship” or “lack of available talent” is unacceptable. The inability to assemble a diverse committee is not a reflection of the communities—it is a reflection of institutional neglect.

If we cannot build inclusive representation at the very foundation of AI governance, we risk entrenching the very inequities this technology has the potential to dismantle. Diversity is not a box to check—it is a prerequisite for legitimacy, accountability, and justice.

Canada must do better. And it must shave started yesterday.

column2:
Pour rebâtir la confiance du public, qui est aujourd’hui très affectée, le Canada doit adopter une approche transparente, participative et centrée sur les droits humains, en s’appuyant sur des mécanismes concrets et vérifiables.
- Transparence radicale : Publier les données d’entraînement et les audits des systèmes à haut risque.
- Éducation et participation : Lancer un programme national d’alphabétisation en IA et inclure les communautés marginalisées.
- Sécurité technique : Exiger des IA "corrigibles" et créer un Bureau de la sécurité de l’IA.
- Ouverture et responsabilité : Rendre les IA publiques open source et taxer l’utilisation non éthique des données.
- Protection des droits : Instaurer un droit à l’explication et auditer les biais algorithmiques.
column3:
Pour garantir la fiabilité des systèmes d’IA, le Canada doit adopter des cadres juridiques stricts, inspirés des meilleures pratiques internationales, tout en évitant les lacunes des approches volontaires.
- Licences et plafonds énergétiques pour les IA à haut risque.
- Adoption légale de la Déclaration de Montréal et création d’un médiateur (ombudsman) de l’IA.
- Interdiction des agents autonomes non supervisés et responsabilité pénale des entreprises en cas de dommage causé par leurs systèmes d’IA ou incluant l’IA.
- Ouverture des algorithmes publics et droit à la réparation pour les victimes de décisions erronées impliquant l’IA.
- Lutte contre la discrimination par l’IA et soutien aux travailleurs (création de fonds public pour soutenir les travailleurs affectés par l’automatisation, financé par une taxe sur les bénéfices des entreprises d’IA.).
- implémenter le revenu minimum ou universel garanti (UBI) ou salaire à vie. Les projets pilotes ont déjà montré que ces programmes n’ont pas d’effet négatif sur la motivation à travailler, et au contraire permettent la reconversion de la force de travail.
column4:
Pour engager les citoyens et les entreprises, le Canada doit miser sur la transparence, la co-création et des mécanismes de reddition de comptes clairs, en impliquant tous les acteurs de la société.
- Gouvernement fédéral : Pour financer les programmes nationaux et adopter les lois (ex : médiateur de l’IA, jurys citoyens).
- Provinces et municipalités : Pour organiser les forums locaux et soutenir les laboratoires citoyens.
- Universités et instituts de recherche (Mila, CIFAR, Vector Institute) : Pour former le public et mener les simulations de crises.
- Syndicats et organisations de droits humains : Pour défendre les travailleurs et les groupes marginalisés.
Médias et bibliothèques : Pour éduquer et informer le public de manière accessible.
Ces propositions pourraient être intégrées dans la stratégie nationale sur l’IA ou dans le cadre de la loi C-27 (AIDA), en collaboration avec les provinces, les entreprises et la société civile.

column2:
Canada builds trust when people see AI making life better and leaders are open about how it works, why it was chosen, and what happens when things go wrong. We stop fear by telling real stories of impact, vetting tools, and showing that AI is here to lift people, not replace them.
Set a simple trust standard
Use a national vet and verify process with public scorecards. Test for safety, privacy, bias, and real-world benefit. Publish results in plain language before and after deployment.
Be transparent and accountable.
When the government funds or scales a tool, say why, what outcomes are expected, and how success will be measured If a mistake happens, explain it fast, fix it, and share the learning.
Teach AI to care
Center design on care for people and community. Ask in every project: does this protect dignity, widen access, and help someone do their job or learn faster. Make this the first gate for public funding.
Include more Canadians in the upside
Prioritize companies that deliver public value, not just returns to a few. Use Canadian firms with strong track records, such as InStage, to demonstrate how AI can support learning, skills, and work. Let communities see local benefits.
Literacy for everyone
Build AI literacy into schools, WIL, worker training, and public service. Give learners practice with reflection so they can name what they learned and when to question a result. 
Open sandboxes, not closed rooms
Run time-bound pilots in health, education, energy, and public service with clear rules and community oversight. Invite teachers, nurses, students, and small businesses to try, question, and co-design.
Independent oversight and quick decisions
Stand up an independent body to review risk claims and audit results. Move fast, but always with consultation and open reporting. Decisions can be quick and still be well explained.
Celebrate wins, not hype
Tell the stories of safer care, shorter waits, better learning, and faster hiring.
column3:
Canada already has a foundation for responsible AI—the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) and the Voluntary Code of Conduct—but to make AI truly trustworthy, we need a framework that makes integrity and care the norm, not an afterthought.
National Trust Framework. Create one clear standard for AI safety, privacy, and fairness. Use a public “vet and verify” model with plain-language scorecards that show how products were tested, who reviewed them, and what risks remain.

Independent oversight. Establish a national AI Standards and Assurance Office to certify tools against AIDA and ISO benchmarks, monitor incidents, and publish regular transparency reports.

Ethical design grounded in care. Require all publicly funded AI to demonstrate human benefit and ethical reflection: does it improve learning, safety, or equity? Teach AI to “care like a parent”—to protect people, communities, and dignity.

Sector-specific standards.
• Health: safety, explainability, bias testing.
• Education: data privacy, consent, learning integrity.
• Employment: transparency in assessment and hiring.
• Defence and public safety: human-in-the-loop and audit trails.

Liability and assurance. Define clear roles for builders, buyers, and users. Safe-harbour protection for firms that meet certified standards and mandatory disclosure for those that don’t.

Lifecycle accountability. Require ongoing monitoring, not one-time audits. Make re-certification every two years part of procurement.

Open sandboxes and evidence sharing. Let innovators test under oversight and share findings publicly to improve the ecosystem.

Public literacy and transparency. Pair every national deployment with accessible education so Canadians understand how decisions are made.

Trust is built when AI in Canada is seen as transparent, tested, and designed to care for people. By embedding ethics, oversight, and openness into every stage, Canada can be the world leader in human-centred, responsible AI.
column4:
Building trust in AI starts with understanding, not fear. Canada must teach AI early and often—through schools, workplaces, and communities—so every citizen knows what AI is, how it works, and how to question it responsibly. We are in a new age, and trust will grow when people feel capable, not powerless.

Start early. Introduce AI literacy from K-12 through post-secondary. Teach critical thinking, ethics, and reflection alongside technical skills so students learn how to use and question AI responsibly. Embed AI learning in Canada’s co-op and Work-Integrated Learning systems so it becomes real and relevant.

Use systems we already have. Leverage public libraries, chambers of commerce, colleges, unions, and community centres to host AI workshops and town halls. Build on these trusted networks instead of starting from scratch.

Lead with transparency. Government must model openness: publish why AI tools are used, what data informs them, and how outcomes are evaluated. Quick, clear explanations—not secrecy—build confidence.

Inclusive engagement. Invite educators, students, small businesses, Indigenous communities, and newcomers into the conversation early. Design listening sessions before policies are finalized so people feel heard, not managed.

Shared leadership.
• Government: set transparent policy and public reporting standards.
• Academia: provide neutral research and literacy materials.
• Industry: demonstrate responsible use and share good-practice data.
• Community organizations: translate complex ideas into everyday language.

Public storytelling. Tell real stories of Canadian AI improving lives—faster training, safer workplaces, more inclusive learning—so the narrative shifts from risk to value.

Trust will come when Canadians see AI as something built with them and for them. Teach it early, show it clearly, and lead with transparency and care.

column2: The environmental and economic costs of AI must be factored in to any rollout of AI infrastructure programs. There are steep environmental costs of using AI and with Canada's natural resources -- particularly our reserves of freshwater -- our country will bear a significant brunt of the global damage brought on by heavy AI usage. The economic costs will take the form of lost jobs in multiple sectors and a growing population of young people who will have ever increasingly limited job opportunities in the future as work disappears. To mitigate these costs, AI companies must be taxed in a meaningful way to offset the disruption to our economy and our infrastructure and the revenue from taxation reinvested into things like green energy projects and alternative water cooling technologies to replace the need for fresh water. These reinvestment areas can become secondary growth industries, allowing Canada to become growth leaders in finding ethical applied solutions to the problems generated by AI. A portion of taxation should also go towards social programs like universal basic income, shorter work weeks and education initiatives to offset the loss of circulating money in our economic system as AI continues to decimate stable Canadian jobs in everything from accounting to manufacturing to telecommunications to writing. As for education, I hope the government is prepared for multiple generations of Canadians with growing dysfunctional relationships with AI. Misinformation and disinformation, adverse mental health effects from AI-enabled psychosis, and reduced higher-thinking capacity due to over-reliance on AI pose real threats to the fabric of society. Counteracting these threats will require strong legislation, meaningful and effective guardrails, and vigilant monitoring and enforcement of regulations. As the world becomes increasingly destabilized, there is room for Canada to stand out as a leader in ethical application of AI by looking at the societal impacts as well.

column2:
Public trust cannot be built without accountability. Confidence comes from knowing that if an AI system causes unintended harm, there is a clear and accessible mechanism for redress. The most important step is to move beyond development-phase regulations and implement a robust post-deployment liability and governance framework.

This framework must: 1. Formally recognize all types of AI harm, including tangible harms like financial loss and intangible harms like discrimination, privacy violations, and loss of autonomy. 2. Establish clear liability rules so consumers are not left to navigate complex AI supply chains to find the responsible party. 3. Ensure transparency through mandatory reporting, which demonstrates that failures are being tracked and mitigated.

Public trust cannot be built on voluntary principles like the HAIP, which the G7 "Toolkit for SMEs" relies on. Trust is built on tangible accountability and accessible redress.

The most important things to do are: 
1. Establish an AI Ombudsperson: Create an independent, expert office (as detailed in my memo: "Establishing AI Liability and Post-Deployment Frameworks to Protect Consumers and Ensure Accountability" (https://drive.google.com/file/d/11AALYrG8uES5kbwb7o14LZ7zaFEggv4W/view?usp=drive_link) ) where any Canadian can go for help when they are harmed by an AI system. This is the #1 way to build public confidence. 

2. Create an AI Compensation Fund: For cases where a single liable party is impossible to identify, this fund (funded by levies on AI companies) ensures victims are not left without recourse.
column3:
We need an interlocking set of legal, governance, and technical frameworks. This includes:

1. Regulation (The "What"): A Tiered AI Supply Chain Liability Framework that amends AIDA, PIPEDA, and the Consumer Protection Act . 
2. Governance (The "Where"): An AI Ombudsperson Office as the central body for public redress and oversight. 
3. Standard (The "How"): A technical, forensic standard to make liability attribution fair and possible. The Latent Risk Attribution (LRA) Protocol (which includes the "Hacker-Holweg Comparative Test" ) is a state-of-the-art model for this. It provides a "Burden-Shifting Rule" to prove whether a fault was "Introduced" by an SME or "Revealed" (latent) in the base model . 
4. Norm (The "Feedback Loop"): A mandatory, public AI Incident Reporting System to ensure continuous, real-world learning from failures.
5. An Adaptive Post-Deployment Framework: Based on standards like ISO/IEC 42001:2023, this would mandate post-deployment monitoring, risk mitigation, and continuous learning from incidents.

"Establishing AI Liability and Post-Deployment Frameworks to Protect Consumers and Ensure Accountability" - https://drive.google.com/file/d/11AALYrG8uES5kbwb7o14LZ7zaFEggv4W/view?usp=drive_link" 

"The Latent Risk Attribution (LRA) Protocol" - https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RPlCEVVInnHl7RSD2JulXTd0anbKgP6SzQSKHg1yg4c/edit?usp=drive_link
column4:
The proposed AI Ombudsperson Office is best placed to lead direct public engagement. This independent office would fuel trust by:  
1. Creating a "user-friendly online platform for consumers to easily report AI-induced harm and file claims". 
2.  Providing objective assessments in liability cases and overseeing dispute resolution.

The independent oversight body managing the Public AI Incident Reporting System would also build trust through transparency. By making incident data publicly accessible, it allows researchers, civil society, and the public to understand the real-world risks and track how they are being mitigated.

It would be the single, trusted, independent source for: 
Citizens: Running public literacy campaigns and providing a "user-friendly online platform... to easily report AI-induced harm and file claims". 
Businesses (SMEs): Providing clear guidance, best practices, and a low-cost, predictable Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanism to resolve issues before they require costly litigation

column3:
AI technology advances very quickly, so the norms which make sense for today's AI models might not make sense tomorrow. Furthermore, the rate of improvement is exponential, so there is a real danger that an AI-related emergency situation might occur, e.g. a rogue model hacking into other systems and acquiring more compute, at a pace at which the government is not used to react.

In order to be able to act quickly in such a situation, the government needs a way to quickly impose extra constraints on all existing AI systems in case of emergency, similar to the way in which the government imposed a lockdown during COVID. For this, the government needs to acquire, ahead of time, the cooperation of the Canadian AI companies who will impose those constraints.

One way to do this could be to require AI companies to obtain a permit in order to operate in Canada. One of the requirements for obtaining this permit could be to provide up-to-date contact information of an employee who has the authority to shut down the company's AI system if needed, or some lighter measure as applicable.

It would be even better if the permit required the company to provide a standardized API to the Canadian government, via which the government could immediately impose constraints and automatically perform audits. Of course, given the cost of implementing such an API, it would probably make sense to only impose it to AI companies above a certain size.

Each such company would need to allow the government to use the API to perform one of the following emergency actions, depending on the nature of the emergency:
- Disable the publicly-accessible endpoints which allow unauthenticated members of the public to access the AI system.
- Disable the AI system's access to the internet.
- Shut down the compute on which the AI System runs.

column2:
Establish a responsible knowledge sharing ecosystem: Foster coordinated knowledge sharing efforts among federal, provincial, municipal governments, industry, and academia to scale responsible AI applications that deliver measurable public benefits in high-trust sectors such as health, environment, and public service delivery. Knowledge sharing can be championed by government, chambers of commerce, and industry associations through sector specific communities of practice, a national ad campaign, structured mentorship opportunities, and a centralized, publicly accessible online library of AI use cases. 

Lead by example: All levels of government can be the leading model of AI adoption by demonstrating successful service delivery, procurement, and program management that leverages AI. Demonstrating AI’s value across many areas of work like grant approvals, regulatory efficiency, and processing services can build public trust and push businesses working with government to adopt responsibly.

Emphasize education over regulation: Encourage proactive disclosure and public education initiatives that clarify how AI is adopted across organizations and how risks are managed under current laws. Modernize frameworks such as the Privacy Act to strengthen protection of personal data in the use of AI. Continue close engagement with chambers of commerce who act as trusted partners of businesses in navigating pressing challenges.

Equip SMEs to adopt responsible practices: Provide tools and supports for data governance, privacy protection, cybersecurity, and ethical risk management to ensure smaller enterprises can build public trust.

Harmonize policies with global frameworks: Advance interoperable AI standards consistent with OECD and G7 principles to ensure trust, accountability, and cyber resilience across Canadian businesses. Encourage the use of voluntary industry codes of conduct that align with these frameworks to demonstrate responsible innovation.

In most creative sectors, and Writing and Publishing in particular, the biggest concerns surround use of published works for AI training without full disclosure and appropriate compensation to content creators.  The current flurry of lawsuits suing AI companies for copyright infringement point to a need for clarity in Copyright legislation; the mandated five-year review of same is an ideal opportunity for this.  Simply announcing a plan for this review would be a first step for government to build confidence; to date virtually nothing has been announced.
column3: See previous comments regarding protection against copyright violations.  Per the lawsuits mentioned above, it is clear that large AI players have taken advantage of ambiguities in copyright law to unfairly profit from content creators' work, to the on-going detriment of creative sector participants.  The substantial sums government funnels to the creative sectors will have diminishing returns when launched into today's largely unregulated AI sector, with its virtually limitless capacity for content ingestion.
column4: Get on with the mandated Copyright review.

column2:
You earn the trust of the people when you act in the public interest: 

	•	Adopt a Public-Interest AI Charter for any Canadian-built LLM/conversational interface: commitments to safety, transparency, bilingual and Indigenous-language support, accessibility, and cultural representation—backed by enforceable service standards.
	•	Radical transparency by default: publish model cards, training-data provenance statements, evaluation results (including bias, safety, and robustness), and release a plain-language “How it works/limits” note for people.
	•	Independent oversight and audits: require third-party red-teaming, safety audits, and annual public reports; create an ombudsperson for complaints and redress with binding remedies.
	•	Human-in-the-loop for high-stakes uses: mandate human review and clear avenues for appeal in areas like health, finance, employment, education, and public benefits; prohibit certain uses (e.g., real-time mass biometric surveillance).
	•	Stronger privacy and data governance: modernise privacy law and public-sector data rules; minimise data collection; protect sensitive datasets; require secure MLOps and incident disclosure within set timelines.
	•	Trustworthy public procurement: set federal procurement baselines (risk classification, impact assessment, auditability, logging, fail-safes); publish a registry of government AI systems with lifecycle “sunset unless renewed” clauses.
	•	Participatory governance: stand up citizen panels (including youth, rural, and Indigenous representation) to review policies, datasets, and evaluations; publish their recommendations and responses.
	•	Clear labelling and education: require “AI-generated/assisted” disclosures, provide easy opt-outs where feasible, and fund national digital/AI literacy tied to schools, libraries, and community organisations.

Do these visibly—starting with a Canadian LLM and conversational interface operated under this charter—and trust will be earned.

column2:
To build public trust, Canada should make transparency the foundation of all AI-enabled health services. This begins with requiring plain-language disclosure in both official languages whenever AI is used in care, consistent with provincial consent laws. Patients and providers must clearly understand the role, limits, and expected outcomes of AI to build trust and ensure accountability across the health system.
AI systems must also embed privacy and security by design, grounded in Canada’s privacy frameworks under PIPEDA and provincial legislation like PHIPA. The use of identifiable patient data for training should be prohibited without explicit authorization and transparent consent. These safeguards protect patient privacy, reduce risks of data misuse or model overreach, and strengthen public confidence in responsible innovation.
Canada should avoid restricting domestic innovators to only Canadian-built foundational models. Such limits would isolate the ecosystem and slow progress. Instead, governments should promote safe, standards-based use of both Canadian and global models within secure Canadian infrastructure, ensuring that data, privacy, and economic value stay under Canadian control.To maintain ethical integrity, Canada should operationalize fairness and explainability by requiring continuous bias monitoring and ensuring that AI outputs are interpretable to clinicians and patients. Turning these ethical principles into practical safeguards will ensure that trust, safety, and equity remain central to AI use in health care.
Before scaling any technology, Canada should rely on sandbox environments to test new tools in controlled, real-world settings. Regular policy reviews every two to three years will keep oversight current with technological advances, ensuring that health AI evolves responsibly and that innovation and trust continue to grow together.
column3:
Canada should establish a federal framework for trustworthy AI in health care to promote consistency, safety and accountability across the system. This framework should define clear standards for transparency, explainability and oversight. It must also be reviewed and updated regularly to stay current with emerging AI technologies, ensuring rules evolve alongside new capabilities and risks.
To support collaboration and scalability, Canada must make interoperability a national standard. Publishing a Pan-Canadian Interoperability Standard for Health AI will enable safe data exchange and build confidence across jurisdictions. This approach reduces integration costs, improves compatibility, and allows trusted, validated tools to scale efficiently across the health network.
Canada should ensure its frameworks align with international norms. We should be able to use global technologies that meet high safety and ethical standards, without forcing compliance with uniquely Canadian rules that isolate the market. We have seen this risk before in privacy policy, when Canada considered introducing standards out of step with the rest of the world, which could have discouraged major technology players from operating here.
Strong privacy and security safeguards must anchor this framework. Compliance with PIPEDA and provincial privacy legislation, including strict limits on using identifiable patient data for training without explicit authorization and transparent consent, will uphold product integrity while reflecting Canada’s ethical and legal commitments to patient protection.
The framework should include adaptive evaluation and procurement mechanisms that move validated tools quickly and safely from pilot to deployment. Incorporating performance-based incentives and clear evaluation metrics will ensure that scalable AI solutions deliver real-world improvements in outcomes and efficiency, while maintaining the highest standards of safety, trust, and public accountability.
column4:
The Government of Canada must lead by establishing clear rules, open communication, and strong collaboration across the health and technology sectors. It should use plain-language, bilingual communication to explain when and how AI is being used in health care, publish transparent evaluation criteria and results, and maintain national frameworks that are updated regularly to reflect ongoing technological change.
A coordinated, multi-stakeholder approach is essential for balanced and informed oversight. Establishing a National Artificial Intelligence in Health Care Advisory Council, composed of clinicians, technologists, ethicists, and patients, would help align national priorities, identify risks early, and guide the responsible deployment of AI in health care.
Sandbox pilots should be central to this collaborative approach. Partnering with health providers and patient groups to test AI tools in real-world settings will generate evidence of safety, efficacy, and value. These pilots will also demonstrate that Canada’s governance model is both innovative and accountable, ensuring that new technologies enhance rather than disrupt trust in health care delivery.
Building AI literacy and public trust must be a shared responsibility across all sectors. Industry should lead by embedding transparency and fairness in product design, while health institutions and academic organizations integrate AI education into training programs so clinicians can use these tools safely and effectively. Governments must support this by conducting policy reviews every two to three years and publishing clear updates that maintain regulatory predictability. Together, these efforts will create a health AI ecosystem defined by openness, integrity, and lasting public confidence.

column2:
Building public trust in AI requires transparency, accountability, and education. Canadians must understand not only how AI works but also how it is governed and who benefits from it. Trust grows when people can see clear rules, real accountability, and responsible leadership behind every AI system.

The first step is transparency in design and deployment. Companies should clearly explain how data is used, how decisions are made, and how people can appeal or correct errors. Mandatory disclosure of AI-generated content and strong standards for consent will help reduce confusion and misuse. Programs like VoxID Canada already show how technology can verify authenticity and protect human identity through watermarking and traceability, giving creators and users confidence that their data is secure and respected.

Government must also create a visible framework for accountability and safety. Independent audits, certification systems, and clear penalties for misuse will signal that Canada treats AI ethics as seriously as product safety or privacy law. Public institutions should lead by example by adopting only verifiable and responsible AI tools.

Education is another pillar of trust. Schools, workplaces, and media organizations should teach practical AI literacy so that Canadians can understand its benefits and limitations. Public awareness campaigns can highlight real-world examples of ethical AI improving health care, environmental monitoring, and accessibility.

Finally, open dialogue with citizens, unions, and communities is essential. People are more likely to trust AI when they have a voice in how it is used. By combining transparency, oversight, and public education, Canada can build a relationship with AI that is grounded in understanding, confidence, and shared responsibility.
column3:
Ensuring AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed requires a clear framework of standards, regulations, and professional norms that unite technical innovation with public accountability. The foundation must include transparent governance, independent oversight, and consistent enforcement across all sectors that develop or deploy AI.

Canada should establish a national AI assurance framework that requires companies to demonstrate product integrity, explainability, and ethical compliance before large-scale deployment. This could include mandatory risk assessments, algorithmic audits, and certification for systems used in sensitive domains such as health care, finance, justice, and media.

Strong data governance rules are essential to guarantee privacy, consent, and security across the full AI lifecycle. Developers should maintain traceability of training data and disclose how it influences outcomes. Government regulators need the authority to review and penalize misuse, ensuring consistent accountability between public and private actors.

Independent testing labs and open evaluation standards would help verify claims of safety, fairness, and accuracy. Universities, research councils, and trusted organizations can play a central role in setting these technical benchmarks.

Public trust also depends on transparency in digital content. Initiatives like VoxID Canada provide a practical model, using watermarking and verification to ensure the authenticity of AI-generated or voice-based media. Embedding this kind of traceability across other sectors will make AI systems more accountable and easier to audit.

Finally, Canada should promote a shared code of conduct across industry, academia, and government to define responsible AI behavior. With clear rules, strong oversight, and verified transparency, AI in Canada can grow with public confidence and international credibility.
column4:
Building trust in AI depends on open communication, shared responsibility, and consistent leadership across sectors. Canadians will support AI when they see that it serves public interests, operates transparently, and is governed by credible institutions.

The first step is public engagement and education. Citizens need clear, accessible information about how AI systems make decisions and how they affect daily life. Schools, community organizations, and public broadcasters should help build AI literacy through real-world examples and interactive learning. Businesses can also host open demonstrations or transparency reports that show how AI improves services while protecting privacy.

Government plays a key role in setting the tone. It should establish an independent AI transparency office that communicates standards, publishes audits, and invites citizen feedback. Regulators must work with universities, civil society, and unions to ensure policies reflect a wide range of voices. Academic institutions can lead public research and dialogue on fairness, accountability, and social impact.

Industry leaders must commit to responsible AI charters and share best practices through open certification or labeling systems. Initiatives like VoxID Canada show how transparency and traceability can foster trust by proving the authenticity of AI-generated or voice-based content. This kind of collaboration between technology developers, creators, and policymakers strengthens both innovation and confidence.

Trust grows when people feel included and informed. By combining education, transparency, and shared governance across government, industry, and civil society, Canada can ensure AI becomes a trusted tool that reflects public values and supports long-term national progress.

Incentivize cross-sector collaboration to co-design assurance standards and responsible AI certifications. Embedding education and workforce training in these frameworks ensures every stage of AI—from design to deployment—advances Canadian values and social responsibility.

Canada builds public trust in AI when people can see that the technology is solving real problems, in real communities, under clear rules. We must be transparent about what the AI is doing, what data it uses, and how it was evaluated and make independent testing routine. We must bring communities into the design and deployment process so AI isn’t “done to” people but built with them. Our CIFAR Solution Network on AI for Diabetes Prediction and Prevention is doing exactly this: taking already-validated diabetes risk models and working with public health, health-system partners, and communities to deploy them responsibly and equitably, with an explicit focus on prevention and dashboard tools for decision-makers.   

https://cifar.ca/publications-reports/reach/novel-networks/
column3: There is already a substantial body of high-quality international AI frameworks, from the EU, OECD, NIST and others,  so I wouldn’t recommend creating an entirely new Canadian model. Instead, Canada should leverage and align with that existing work, and make it operational for our context: health and public health, federal–provincial data, Indigenous data governance, and public-sector procurement. The priority is not inventing new principles, but turning shared principles (safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, human oversight) into requirements for anyone building AI into Canadian systems.
column4: Our CIFAR AI for Diabetes Prediction and Prevention project models this approach. When Canadians can see AI improving health outcomes safely and fairly, confidence will grow.

column2: Public libraries support AI regulation that maximizes the benefits of the technology while actively mitigating its risks—regulation that reflects Canadian values and strengthens our democracy. Yet, a recent KPMG study reveals that Canada ranks 42nd out of 47 countries in public trust in AI systems and 44th in AI training, underscoring a critical need for trusted, accessible public education. As consistently ranked among the most trusted institutions in the country, public libraries are uniquely positioned to fill this gap. We offer safe, judgment-free spaces where Canadians can explore AI, ask questions, and build critical thinking skills with the guidance of skilled librarians. With a strong foundation in privacy protection, intellectual freedom, and digital literacy, libraries are ideal partners in delivering transparent, inclusive AI education that empowers all Canadians.
column4:
Canadians consistently recognize public libraries as trusted, democratic institutions that provide equitable access to information, resources, and training. Every month, over 16,000 people participate in technology programs at public libraries, reflecting our vital role in building digital skills and fostering lifelong learning. 
As AI continues to reshape our economy and society, it is essential that all Canadians—regardless of age, background, or location—have access to AI literacy and training. Public libraries offer multigenerational, community-based learning environments that remove cost barriers and support critical thinking, digital skills, and workforce adaptation.
Public libraries believe in AI regulation that harnesses the benefits of the technology while actively mitigating its risks. We advocate for policies that reflect Canadian values, defend data privacy rights, and strengthen our democracy. As community education hubs, libraries operate under high standards of data protection, and our users trust us to safeguard their information and guide them in protecting their own. Trust in AI begins with education from trusted sources—and libraries provide the transparent, judgment-free spaces where Canadians can safely explore, question, and understand AI with the support of skilled librarians.

column2:
•	As generative AI emerges and enters a period of widespread use, the field risks exacerbating an ongoing public crisis of confidence in AI technology. This is because without developing a more mature evaluation science for generative AI systems, the potential risks and limitations of these technologies may not be fully understood and addressed.

• Establish professional licensing for AI engineers to ensure qualified practitioners are designing and implementing AI systems.

• Require safety certification for AI systems, particularly those used in critical infrastructure, which should only be designed and built by licensed engineers.

• Regulate the use of AI in professional practice through governance frameworks including AI codes of ethics, competency requirements, and practice standards.

• Implement comprehensive standards at multiple levels: practice standards (technical and management), industry standards, international standards, and engineering assurance audits.

• Ensure professional self-governing organizations verify that their practitioners are competent in AI use and knowledgeable about AI codes of ethics.
column3:
•	There is a disconnect between the current AI evaluation culture, with its focus on benchmarking models, and real world, grounded approaches to the assessment of performance and safety.

• Develop comprehensive engineering practice standards and guidelines for AI development and deployment.

• Establish professional practice standards and guidelines for AI practitioners across all relevant fields.

• Implement regular engineering assurance audits for AI systems.

• Adopt relevant international standards such as FDA/MDR and ICH 8 & 9 guidelines where applicable.
column4:
• Prioritize AI safety promotion over general AI adoption promotion in public messaging.

• Develop and enforce comprehensive AI codes of ethics across all sectors.

• Ensure government agencies train their personnel on AI ethics and responsible use.

• Require clear disclosure when AI has been used to generate content or make decisions.

• Implement AI authentication systems to verify trusted AI applications.

• Provide citizens with the right to decline AI use in services affecting them.

• Give the public greater control over how AI is used in their personal interactions with services.

• Establish AI "disgorgement" remedies that allow for correction when AI systems cause harm.

1. AI Literacy for the Public: A significant barrier to trust is the lack of understanding of how AI works and its potential implications for daily life. Misinformation or lack of knowledge breeds fear. Potential actions:
 - National AI Literacy Campaign: Launch a national AI literacy initiative to educate the public on how AI works, its benefits, and the risks involved. This could include public service campaigns, online resources, and workshops at schools and community centers.
 - Incorporate AI into Education: Integrate AI fundamentals into educational curricula at all levels—primary, secondary, and tertiary. This should include topics like machine learning basics, data ethics, privacy, and AI policy to create an informed future workforce and electorate.

In this regard, CEIMIA is willing and able to contribute, both in terms of certification development with its SRAIS mentorship programme mentioned above and towards international recognition with its international advocacy and scientific diplomacy capacity. This certification could improve Canadian’s AI-related trade as it would signal certification-holding firms that their AI solutions have gone an extra step compared to their competitors; allow for greater procurement contract bidding outcomes (often in public sector procurement as they seek RAI - or similar language - solutions during procurement processes); and boost Canada’s overall AI market reputation at an international level.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by implementing strong, citizen-first protections, particularly for vulnerable groups like children, and ensuring that AI systems include features such as an “off switch” to prevent misuse. Governance should prioritize safety and societal well-being over commercial pressures, preventing a repeat of past tech sector dynamics where innovation was driven primarily by profit. Diverse ownership and community benefits in AI development can further ensure technologies serve broader societal interests.
Public confidence also depends on education and transparency. Citizens need accessible information about AI risks and safeguards, alongside active engagement in policymaking. By demonstrating that AI tools are developed and deployed responsibly, with government oversight that protects all Canadians, particularly marginalized populations, trust in AI technologies can grow alongside informed adoption.
column3:
Canada should adopt AI governance frameworks that prioritize people and the environment over business interests. Regulations must ensure data privacy, algorithmic transparency, and accountability, while addressing ethical concerns in AI development. Critical areas of oversight include AI systems used in decision-making affecting public welfare, where trust is most acutely needed.
Environmental sustainability must also be integrated into AI standards. Data centers should be carbon neutral, conserve water, and minimize land impacts, reflecting the growing environmental toll of AI infrastructure. By embedding these requirements into regulatory frameworks, Canada can promote responsible AI deployment that is ethical, trustworthy, and aligned with societal and environmental priorities.
column4:
Canada can engage citizens and businesses in responsible AI use by creating a Citizen Advisory Committee with access to the Minister and senior civil servants. This committee would bring diverse perspectives, including civil society and grassroots organizations, ensuring AI governance reflects public values. Annual public reporting on AI use should outline applications, safeguards, and lessons learned, while providing opportunities for civil society to highlight gaps or risks. Transparency can be maintained without exposing sensitive proprietary systems.
Reporting should also include environmental and resource impacts, such as electricity, carbon, and water use associated with AI infrastructure, using existing frameworks like the NPRI. Leadership should be shared: the government sets standards and maintains oversight, while civil society and community organizations monitor compliance and ensure trust-building efforts are inclusive, equitable, and focused on societal benefit.

column2:
Despite climbing 8% from 2022 to 40%, Canada has one of the lowest rates of optimism when it comes to seeing AI products and services as more beneficial than harmful, according to the AI Index Report 2025. Only 24% of Canadians said that they trust companies that use artificial intelligence will protect their personal data. 
To help combat these issues, the Government of Canada should establish clear frameworks, policies and standards to govern AI use and address public concerns around privacy, consent and algorithmic decision-making. To help address concerns around data sovereignty and ownership, the federal government should ensure that AI infrastructure is anchored in public facilities in Canada. With their existing academic expertise in AI, universities are ideal public venues to host compute infrastructure. Public venues will ensure that AI technologies benefit from greater transparency and public accountability. Moreover, placing AI infrastructure in Canadian universities will help to reduce the reliance on foreign compute technologies, building further trust among the public. 
Canadian universities, as the leaders in AI technology across the country, are uniquely positioned to assist the government in building awareness campaigns on how to use AI safely and ethically and when to best use AI technologies to avoid errors and bias. Already they offer public lectures, workshops and conferences to create spaces for public dialogue on difficult issues.
column3:
AI will be a key disruptor in Canada’s economy and society. The government should engage universities, as the leaders of Canada’s AI research success, in the development of ethical AI policy and regulation beyond this initial taskforce consultation.  
The Government of Canada should look to develop legislation, frameworks and regulations that cover issues such as:
•	Privacy
•	Data ownership
•	The obligation of AI developers and proprietors to disclose and explain algorithmic decision-making 
•	The obligation of companies using AI tools to disclose when and how they are using AI, their AI tools’ algorithmic decision-making, and error rates. 
•	Consent (particularly around the use of technologies that make it difficult for the public to determine what is real and what is AI-generated)
•	Addressing bias
•	Requirements for human oversight quality over high-stakes decision-making
•	Procurement standards for government and/or public sector use of AI
•	Other ethical and safety standards
Canada should ensure coherence and coordination with international partners and look to existing models, such as the European Union AI Act. 
As AI technology evolves, universities will be pivotal and ethical partners in providing advice to government to ensure that Canada remains a leader in AI research and reaches its full potential for industry application and commercialization.
column4:
As leaders in the development of Canada’s AI, universities should be consulted early in drafting AI regulations to balance the importance of protecting data and privacy with creating an enabling environment for research and innovation. As ecosystems with interdisciplinary teams of experts, universities across the country are working on these issues and have a wealth of knowledge and expertise that can be leveraged.
The Government should also look to Canada’s universities as a partner in public engagement to build trust in AI and AI governance. Across the country, universities are offering courses and degrees that equip students on the ethical uses and limitations of AI, provide SMEs with dedicated guidance on the effective, safe and ethical use of AI and offer micro-credentials that help upskill and reskill workers. In addition, they offer public lectures, workshops and conferences where they engage the public and other sectors in difficult discussions. These efforts could be further leveraged. 
Universities Canada is ready and willing to help support further engagement on these issues between government, industry and Canada's universities.

column2:
The most important thing is to do the “right thing” - even if the right thing is admitting to uncertainty or even mistakes. External communication from researchers, government and industry needs to be proactive. The media will need to somehow report on potential and known issues while avoiding the typical catastrophizing or exaggeration incentivized by their business model.  Falling behind in communication after incidents will quickly spoil trust.

Grounding public trust in AI on the public’s AI literacy would be extremely unwise.  It would also be unfair to the public. Politicians and policy makers have yet to show such AI literacy themselves. Confidence will be built over time by avoiding incidents - and minimizing the politicisation of use cases, especially for those in the public sector.
column3:
Most professional sectors have existing frameworks and oversight bodies to address safety and ethics (medicine, construction, transportation, etc).  We should not duplicate their efforts but instead let them take the lead and listen to their calls for additional legislation where needed.  Blanket legislation will have unpredictable and unintended consequences otherwise.

Some domains will remain without adequate oversight. I submit that these domains are already fraught with integrity and ethical concerns which have long needed to be addressed, well before our current AI moment. These should be a priority.  Oversight should be created that is independent of technology and which can manage the principal incentives giving rise to the problems.
column4: Incentives are key.  Proactive responsible AI use means recognizing and tackling misaligned incentives. Trust is earned, it is an outcome of other actions.  Any PR, advertisement or body with a primary purpose of “fuelling” trust immediately has the opposite effect as soon as there is reason to doubt that trust - especially in the age of social media. This question feels misguided by positioning trust as a primary goal. Just do the right thing.

column2:
• Set a clear risk ladder (low/medium/high/critical) so people know which AI needs guardrails and which doesn’t.
• Publish a baseline “safety bar” for any AI touching health, money, kids, jobs, critical services: pre-deployment testing, human-in-the-loop, audit logs, rollback plans.
• Create a national certification/label (like an “AI safety mark”) with tiers; make big buyers (gov, hospitals, utilities) prefer certified systems.
• Stand up a public registry for high-risk deployments (what it does, who’s accountable, how to appeal decisions).
• Require incident reporting and “model recalls” for serious harms or eval failures; publish post-mortems.
• Ship content authenticity at scale (C2PA-style provenance + watermark detection) to fight deepfakes, especially for elections and child safety.
• Bake in privacy & security by design (privacy-preserving training, data minimization, secure MLOps, red-teaming for bio/cyber misuse).
• Make government procurement the trust engine: no certification = no production; pilots only with monitoring and exit criteria.
• Give the public recourse that works: simple appeal channels, ombudsperson for algorithmic harms, and SLA on response times.
• Run a national AI literacy push: short, practical modules for students, SMEs, and frontline workers (how to use, where errors happen, how to spot fakes).
column3:
• Risk-based AI rulebook (light for low risk, strict for high/critical) aligned with global norms; one playbook, not 20 conflicting ones.
• Assurance pipeline: documented risk assessment → standardized evaluations (safety, bias, robustness, cybersecurity) → third-party audit for high-risk → continuous monitoring.
• Adopt proven standards: NIST AI RMF for risk, ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems), ISO 23894 (AI risk), and secure-by-design baselines (e.g., supply-chain, model provenance).
• Transparency that matters: model/data cards, intended-use limits, eval results, fail modes; no forced weight disclosures unless safety demands it.
• Priority hotspots needing extra rules: health diagnostics/triage, financial decisions/credit, employment/screening, critical infrastructure/OT, kids’ platforms and elections integrity.
• Post-market vigilance: drift detection, periodic re-cert, incident portals, and enforcement with fines + suspension rights.
• Accessibility & fairness: require usability testing across languages and abilities; measure disparate error rates and mitigate before scaling.
column4:
• Government (federal/provincial): set the rules, run the certification/registry, and act as first buyer; publish plain-English “nutrition labels” for systems in public services.
• Independent safety institute + Standards Council: design eval suites, accredit auditors, publish red-team templates, maintain incident taxonomy.
• Regulators (privacy, finance, health, elections, broadcasting): issue sector guidance, run audits, and coordinate on joint enforcement for cross-sector harms.
• Academia & civil society: citizen juries, public model audits, community clinics that help SMEs and residents understand/appeal decisions.
• Industry: disclose evals, support bug bounties/red-team programs, and fund workforce training; commit to incident transparency and sunset plans for unsafe systems.
• Public-facing tactics that actually build trust: quarterly open evaluations, community townhalls with live demos and Q&A, a self-serve “Is this AI?” authenticity checker, and grants for local groups to run media/AI literacy workshops.

column3:
Canada needs a clear, interoperable framework for AI governance that ensures trust without stifling innovation. The foundation should balance accountability, transparency, and flexibility, enabling responsible deployment while supporting companies that build and operationalize AI.
Key elements should include:
•	National AI Assurance Framework: Establish a standardized process for validating AI systems—covering data provenance, model explainability, bias mitigation, and performance monitoring. Certification programs could provide a “trust mark” for compliant AI products, helping businesses demonstrate responsible practices to customers and regulators.  This framework should align with other allies so companies need only to follow one standard.
•	Risk-Based Regulation: Adopt a tiered oversight model aligned with global best practices (e.g., the EU AI Act), focusing regulatory scrutiny on high-impact use cases such as healthcare, finance, and public safety, while maintaining lighter-touch requirements for low-risk applications. This framework should align with other allies so companies need only to follow one standard.
•	Ethical and Technical Standards: Develop Canadian-aligned standards for model transparency, human oversight, and security in collaboration with industry and international partners. These should integrate with ISO and IEEE frameworks to ensure interoperability across borders.
•	Governance Mechanisms: Create an independent national AI oversight body to coordinate policy, certification, and compliance, while ensuring ongoing consultation with industry to keep frameworks practical, international and innovation-friendly.
•	Data Integrity and Model Security: Introduce guidelines for securing training data, protecting model IP, and ensuring auditability throughout the AI lifecycle.
By embedding trust and assurance frameworks into both product development and deployment, Canada can build a reputation for reliable, ethical, and export-ready AI—strengthening dom
column4:
Recommendation (what & why)
Adopt a shared, role-based trust strategy: government sets standards and enables transparency; industry operationalizes governance and transparency; academia & civil society provide independent assessment and public education.
Operational levers (how)
•	Government: set risk-tiered standards, maintain a public certification registry, and run national literacy campaigns.
•	Industry: adopt standard model cards, user-facing explanations, and independent audits; participate in sector advisory panels.
•	Academia & NGOs: perform independent evaluations, lead citizen juries, and produce plain-language research summaries.
•	Joint actions: citizen juries for high-impact domains; sectoral advisory boards that include consumer advocates; public pilot programs with transparent reporting.
Measures of success
Public trust indices; number of certified systems; resolution rate of citizen complaints; diversity of participants in advisory bodies.

column2:
Public trust in AI depends not only on technical reliability but on transparency, accountability, and informed confidence. Research shows that trust arises when people can understand and evaluate the intent and effects of AI systems, rather than being asked to take them on faith (Afroogh et al., 2024).

Before trust can grow, Canadians must be able to make sense of the technology itself. KPMG’s 2025 global survey found that Canada ranks 44th of 47 countries in AI literacy and 42nd in public trust (KPMG Canada, 2025). Low literacy makes it easier for misinformation and distrust to spread—especially when public perception is shaped by the actions of a few dominant global players. The presence of large technology companies in the AI industry means that trust-eroding decisions by major firms, such as Facebook or Google, can undermine confidence in AI systems as a whole, regardless of their origin or purpose (Afroogh et al., 2024).

Internal documentation is not enough. Canada should establish and maintain public AI registers modelled on international examples such as the UK’s Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard (ATRS), where citizens can easily find which systems are in use, their purpose, the data they rely on, and available avenues for feedback or recourse. Evidence from the OECD and open-government research shows that consistent disclosure frameworks improve accountability, understanding, and public confidence. When citizens can clearly see and understand how a technology works—what it does, what data it uses, and who is responsible—they are more likely to trust it.

Building confidence also requires that people have real opportunities to shape how AI is used in their communities. Citizen panels, participatory audits, and transparent feedback loops should be normalized for high-impact systems. Studies of trust in AI show that genuine participation—where people can question, influence, and monitor decisions—builds far more durable legitimacy than explanations
column3:
To ensure AI systems in Canada are trustworthy, governance must be both globally interoperable and locally grounded. Frameworks such as the OECD AI Principles, the EU AI Act, and the AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) provide guidance on transparency and accountability, but Canada must adapt them to its own context.
Canada should pair risk-based AI governance with a public-facing standard for usable transparency—a framework that works externally to help residents understand AI systems and internally to help governments and vendors assess ethical and responsible-use expectations. Digital Trust for Places & Routines (DTPR)—developed in Canada and advanced globally by Helpful Places—fills that gap. Recognized by UN-Habitat and the American Planning Association, DTPR creates a shared taxonomy of transparency, accountability, and informed public engagement to ensure only trustworthy AI systems are deployed responsibly.
DTPR makes digital and AI technologies understandable through consistent visual language and structured information on purpose, data use, governance, risks, and rights. It connects physical cues to an online “nutrition label” and feedback loop, enabling residents to see how a system works and raise questions in accessible terms. Externally, it provides a clear public-facing layer through signage, registries, and feedback tools; internally, it functions as a procurement and governance filter prompting agencies to consider purpose, oversight, and recourse before adoption.
Recognized internationally and proven in municipal pilots—from Long Beach to Sydney Olympic Park—DTPR demonstrates how transparency and engagement can measurably build public trust. As a made-in-Canada open standard already being adopted globally, formally acknowledging and scaling DTPR would cement Canada’s leadership in transparent, ethical, and people-centred AI governance—showing the world how to operationalize trust in everyday public life.

column2:
A large number of Canadians are rightfully concerned about the water and energy demands of AI and data centers. Residents of Rocky View County, Alberta recently rejected a proposed data center complex due to environmental concerns. Parts of the US serve as important examples as to the need to ensure environmentally responsible data center development. In the states, $64 billion of data centre investment has been delayed or blocked, linked to local community pushback. Local concerns are primarily about higher utility bills, water consumption, noise and green space preservation. In other words, communities in the states are opposing data centers due to their environmental and resource impacts.This opposition is bipartisan


There are increasing numbers of news headlines detailing the environmental risks of large-scale AIl build-out in Canada. AI will not receive wide-spread trust and support until its environmental harms are mitigated. 


85% of Canadians polled said they believed that AI should be regulated. Regulation is the first step to build public trust in AI. To date, there are no regulations in place protecting Canada’s environment from the potential harms of large, data-center build and AI adoption. The government must regulate, and then advertise how it is making Canada a hub for CleanAI in order to build public trust. Trusted messengers of this include environmental NGOs and small CleanAI innovators, rather than international tech firms which have a poor reputation for environmental protection.
column3:
For Data Center Operators:
-Mandatory annual public reporting of Scope 1-3 greenhouse gas emissions, energy and water consumption, water sources, waste generation, and electricity supply sources
-Required cumulative impact assessments focusing on local ecology, electricity price impacts for consumers, and watershed health
-Binding efficiency standards including:
-Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) targets
-Carbon Usage Effectiveness (CUE) requirements
-Water Usage Effectiveness (WUE) limitations
-100% fossil-free electricity requirement (renewable plus storage) for new data centers, which would also accelerate development by addressing grid connection limitations

For AI Service Providers:
-Annual public reporting of Scope 1-3 emissions, energy sources, and data center partnerships
-Disclosure of energy consumption for both model training and inference
-Transparency requirements regarding the environmental impacts of AI products throughout their lifecycle


Environmental requirements may directly benefit innovation in Canadian AI technology. The more efficient a data center is, the cheaper it is to run. Setting binding efficiency mandates will ensure that only the most innovative companies build in Canada.
Additionally, the government should establish a centralized authority for investigating and addressing AI-related harms, including environmental impacts affecting local communities. This comprehensive approach would ensure that the most innovative and efficient companies build in Canada while maintaining public trust.
column4:
The current AI Governance Taskforce has faced criticism for its limited stakeholder inclusion. A notable gap is the absence of environmental and climate experts, despite the significant environmental implications of AI development. Expanding the Taskforce to include sustainability expertise would signal the government's commitment to environmentally responsible AI development.
A multi-stakeholder approach to AI governance should include:
-Environmental organizations with expertise in resource management and climate impacts
-Indigenous communities whose lands and resources may be affected by AI infrastructure
- Indigenous-led data center/ AI/ data sovereignty initiatives
-Academic researchers studying the environmental dimensions of digital technologies
-CleanAI innovators developing sustainable solutions

column2:
Risks posed by AI tools and services.
To build trustworthy systems and public confidence, we must transparently address the inherent risks. This includes potential bias, privacy violations, security breaches, and the possibility of high-stake failures. Effective bias mitigation strategies and clear lines of authority are essential. A mandatory, public incident reporting database for high-risk AI systems remains crucial, creating a traceable record of failures and demonstrating a commitment to learning from mistakes, rather than pretending risks don't exist.

Drivers of public and business mistrust.
The primary driver of mistrust is a lack of accountability, often due to the "black box" nature of AI. Establishing clear legislative liability for operators is vital. Furthermore, we should be leveraging AI initiatives themselves to build traceability and observability platforms. These platforms can provide non-repudiable auditable logs and insights into AI decision-making, and inference processes, offering the technical foundation needed to assign responsibility and build confidence that systems are operating as intended.

Educational and literacy strategies to foster informed confidence.
Education strategies must focus on critical thinking and go beyond specific technical skills or task applications. A national curriculum should teach citizens how to question AI outputs, understand the limitation of current AI, grasp the realistic scope of AI (not just experimental task-specific uses), and recognize the risk of contradictions or nonsensical outputs. Informed confidence is built by demystifying AI, empowering people to assess it critically, and equipping them to hold it accountable for its actions and limitations.
column3:
High-risk sectors like aerospace, banking, finance, and healthcare should treat AI as a governed system with cryptographically verifiable lifecycle controls, not just as software. ISO/IEC 42001 adds an AI management system on top of existing baselines such as ISO/IEC 27001 and SOC 2, clarifying responsibilities for risk assessment, model governance, supplier oversight, and continuous monitoring. In practice, this means end-to-end traceability and immutable, cryptographically signed logging across data provenance, training runs, model versions, evaluation results, deployment history, runtime metrics, drift and bias tests, incident and near-miss records, and rollback decisions. Each critical action, such as model retraining or parameter updates, can generate a cryptographic proof of inference or proof of model integrity, ensuring that decisions and computations can be independently verified without exposing proprietary data. Controls should also extend to third-party models and data providers, with contracts that require cryptographic attestation of model versioning and verifiable vendor logs integrated into the organization’s audit trail.

SOC 2 programs can be updated to include AI-specific controls under Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy, augmented by zero-knowledge attestations and digital signatures to certify compliance. Examples include least-privilege access to models and datasets with cryptographic key management, tamper-resistant storage for inference and training logs, and proof-based thresholds that automatically trigger retraining or rollback when model behavior deviates from policy. Documented explainability artifacts can be linked to cryptographic commitments, ensuring traceability of every decision. Together, ISO 42001 alignment and AI-augmented SOC 2 frameworks enhanced with cryptographic proofs create a verifiable accountability layer that regulators and enterprise customers can trust while giving internal tea
column4: Canada can strengthen trust in AI by engaging entrepreneurs, students, academia, and non-profit organisations such as Canada’s AI Sovereignty & Innovation Cluster (CAISIC) in transparent and inclusive outreach. Entrepreneurs can lead community engagement by hosting meetups, demonstrating how safeguards, bias checks, and cryptographic proofs keep systems accountable. Students can serve as AI ambassadors, organising workshops and open red-teaming events that help citizens and small businesses understand both the promise and risks of AI. Academia can provide independent validation through assurance labs, develop explainability research, and offer micro-credentials in responsible AI governance. Non-profits like CAISIC can act as neutral conveners, promoting Canadian AI sovereignty, fostering ethical innovation, and translating technical standards into accessible public education and practical guidelines.
