column2:
Création de systèmes d’IA sûrs et renforcement de la confiance du public envers l’IA
Que la stratégie nationale conçoive des lignes directrices et des normes en matière de langues officielles pour la conception, la mise en œuvre et l’optimisation des modèles d’IA.
Les modèles d’IA s’appuient sur des bases de données, des corpus, des algorithmes et des cycles de mise en œuvre. Pour pallier les biais linguistiques et les effets négatifs à la mise en œuvre de modèles, la conception de normes linguistiques et de lignes directrices est cruciale. Il existe un risque réel que les iniquités actuelles soient exacerbées, que les services soient automatisés et qu’on ne consulte pas adéquatement les communautés francophones en contexte minoritaires (CFSM). Ces lignes directrices et normes assureront la conformité avec les droits linguistiques, la représentativité des modèles et la participation équitable des CFSM. 

Recommandation 8 : Que la stratégie nationale prévoie l’élaboration de formation et d’ateliers de sensibilisation aux biais linguistiques en relation avec la conception et la mise en œuvre des modèles d’IA.

Grâce à la matérialisation de l’information, les algorithmes peuvent développer des compétences qui étaient jusqu’à présent abstraites et limitées à la main-d’œuvre humaine. Ces compétences nouvellement automatisées entrainent une fragmentation des tâches dans les professions aux compétences complexes, de réflexion critique et de créativité. Cette réallocation des tâches vers l’automatisation produit une perte d’agentivité. Là où cela devient un enjeu préoccupant est lorsqu’on considère la langue comme une simple compétence linguistique, et non dans toutes ces dimensions individuelles et collectives.

Veuillez consulter notre mémoire de l'Association des collèges et des universités de la francophonie canadienne.

Recommandation 7 : Que la stratégie nationale conçoive des lignes directrices et des normes en matière de langues officielles pour la conception, la mise en œuvre et l’optimisation des modèles d’IA.

Les modèles d’IA s’appuient sur des bases de données, des corpus, des algorithmes et des cycles de mise en œuvre. Pour pallier les biais linguistiques et les effets négatifs à la mise en œuvre de modèles, la conception de normes linguistiques et de lignes directrices est cruciale. Il existe un risque réel que les iniquités actuelles soient exacerbées, que les services soient automatisés et qu’on ne consulte pas adéquatement les CFSM. Ces lignes directrices et normes assureront la conformité avec les droits linguistiques, la représentativité des modèles et la participation équitable des CFSM. 

Que la stratégie nationale positionne le Canada comme leadeur mondial en matière d’IA dans la francophonie internationale et favorise la diplomatie scientifique francophone.
La recherche en matière d’IA requerra une collaboration mondiale pour établir des stratégies, des normes linguistiques, et développer des modèles d’IA inclusifs. À l’international, les marchés francophones sont les lieux propices à la coopération et à la commercialisation de modèles canadiens. Les enjeux et biais linguistiques sont multiples dans la conception et la mise en œuvre de modèles. La stratégie nationale permettra d’assurer la normalisation de modèles bilingues, accessibles et abordables. Les chercheuses et chercheurs francophones pourront favoriser la recherche conjointe en français, la diplomatie scientifique, et assurer le rôle crucial du Canada en matière d’IA.

Recommandation 3 : Que la stratégie nationale assure la dualité linguistique et intègre les droits linguistiques à la conception, la mise en œuvre et l’optimisation des modèles d’IA à tous les secteurs de l’économie.

Recommandation 4 : Que la stratégie nationale favorise la recherche appliquée dans les collèges en contexte francophone minoritaire et la collaboration avec le secteur privé.

Recommandation 8 : Que la stratégie nationale prévoie l’élaboration de formation et d’ateliers de sensibilisation aux biais linguistiques en relation avec la conception et la mise en œuvre des modèles d’IA.

Recommandation 12 : Que la stratégie nationale développe des programmes de littératie numérique en français tenant compte des enjeux des CFSM.

Recommandation 13 : Que la stratégie nationale garantisse des possibilités de stage, et facilite la création d’emplois et de partenariats avec le secteur privé en contexte francophone minoritaire.

column2:
To build public trust in AI technologies, Canada should pair strong regulation with tangible, visible practices that demonstrate accountability of developers, providers and users, as well as concrete ways that engage to address major challenges posed by AI systems and raised by the public. A modernized comprehensive AI Act (could be expanded on the version of Bill C-27) should be also include federal agencies and impose specific requirements on developers and providers of AI systems, proportionate to the risk they pose.

Canada should also establish an independent AI Commissioner (e.g., modeled on already existing ones such as the AI Security Institute in UK, or the EU AI Office) to provide templates and useful documentation (e.g., for algorithmic impact assessments), to audit and monitor systems, to investigate harms and serve as a support platform to all stakeholders, including patients or individuals potentially affected by the use of AI.
column3:
About AI Oversight, we suggest: ensuring the right to contest any decision made with the help of an AI system, whether automated or not; a right to know how the system makes its decisions; and a right to request that any personal information used as input in generative AI applications (such as conversational agents) be handled appropriately. It could also include activating specific mechanisms to alert a treating physician or parents in cases where critical questions or responses might indicate imminent danger.

About Regulation According to Risk, we suggest: regulatory obligations should be proportionate to the level of risk posed, but developers and providers should be better supported in conducting risk assessments through the use of templates, case studies, and a set of mandatory risk factors to consider. There could be an obligation to implement a risk management system, and legislation should also define measurable thresholds (such as probability, nature, or intensity of potential harm) that must not be exceeded. Additionally, applications that are not classified as medical devices but that still affect individuals’ physical or mental health (including those processing health-related data) should fall within the scope of this federal law.

About Ethical Integrity and the Adoption of Ethical Guidance, we suggest: It is important to promote the use of developed ethical tools, and possibly to mandate or refer to research centers or organizations that already have relevant expertise. These experts could develop and disseminate practical tools to help stakeholders comply with and go beyond standards for responsible, inclusive, and context-adapted AI that reflects the needs of target populations and environments.
column4: Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses through multi-level, transparent, and inclusive strategies that both explain AI systems and demonstrate accountability. One way we could suggest focus on public education and engagement, using a variety of tools (such as podcasts, science or knowledge communication tools, short videos on official media channels, etc. – that could be created by mandated experts) to provide clear, verified and accurate information on a various range of topics related to AI (incl. AI ethics, AI in healthcare, data privacy, etc.). This type of content could be federally sponsored and distributed through a public channel or account (e.g., ISED, the existing Office of Privacy Commissioner, or a new AI agency as mentioned above) and could also be used to encourage active participation, such as citizen panels or deliberative forums.

column2: Canada would benefit from increased transparency over the process that is involved in training and implementing AI, and from making interaction with AI-based services optional rather than mandatory. Public mistrust may be alleviated if the public has the option to use a service that does not necessarily AI to be integrated, and products or services that use AI should be clearly labelled so the public understands what is being generated by a machine, and what is not.
column3: Comprehensive transparency about the methods for training AI, regular audits regarding the way money is used when it comes to AI research and development, strengthened privacy legislation for Canadian citizens, and regulations that demand clear labelling and declaration of AI when it is used in a service or a product. AI should be at most an option for the public; not the only choice.
column4: Public education measures would be helpful, such as advertising campaigns regarding what AI can and cannot do. It would also be useful to dedicate some resources to encourage students and members of the public to employ critical thinking skills when reading unverified information online.

column2:
Pour instaurer une confiance durable du public envers les technologies d'IA, le Canada doit mettre en place un cadre de développement fondé sur la transparence, l’inclusion et la responsabilité. 
La confiance ne peut émerger que si les citoyennes et citoyens se sentent partie prenante des décisions qui façonnent ces technologies. Cela implique de les inclure activement dans les consultations publiques, de rendre les processus de développement accessibles et compréhensibles, et de garantir que les systèmes d’IA respectent les droits fondamentaux.
Un volet essentiel consiste à soutenir des formations critiques sur les biais algorithmiques, afin que les travailleuses et travailleurs puissent non seulement utiliser les outils d’IA, mais aussi en comprendre les limites et les risques. Ces formations doivent être conçues en collaboration avec les groupes communautaires et les organismes spécialisés en employabilité, qui sont bien placés pour accompagner les personnes les plus vulnérables aux transformations du marché du travail.
Le financement public accordé aux entreprises et aux projets en IA doit être conditionné à des engagements clairs en matière d’éthique, de diversité et de sécurité. Cela permettrait de garantir que les innovations technologiques ne se développent pas au détriment de l’équité sociale.
Enfin, le gouvernement doit assurer une gouvernance ouverte et transparente, en créant des mécanismes de reddition de comptes et en soutenant la recherche indépendante sur les impacts sociaux de l’IA. En accompagnant les travailleuses et travailleurs face aux changements, en protégeant les droits des citoyennes et citoyens, et en favorisant une culture de responsabilité, le Canada peut bâtir une adoption de l’IA qui soit à la fois efficace, équitable et socialement acceptée.
column3:
Pour assurer que les produits d’IA développés au Canada soient fiables et déployés de manière responsable, il est essentiel de mettre en place un cadre réglementaire robuste, éthique et inclusif. 
Le CIAFT souligne que l’IA ne peut être considérée uniquement comme une opportunité économique : elle doit être encadrée par des principes qui protègent les droits des personnes et préviennent les dérives technologiques.
1 ) Cadre éthique obligatoire
Le Canada doit adopter des normes nationales claires en matière d’éthique de l’IA, incluant la non-discrimination, la transparence des algorithmes, la protection des données personnelles et la reddition de comptes. Ces normes doivent être contraignantes pour tout produit d’IA développé ou commercialisé au pays.
2) Inclusion dans les processus de conception
Les équipes de développement doivent refléter la diversité de la population canadienne. Cela implique des politiques d’embauche équitables et des mécanismes de participation citoyenne dans la conception et l’évaluation des systèmes d’IA, afin de limiter les biais sexistes, racistes ou socioéconomiques.
3) Certification et audit des systèmes d’IA
Un système de certification indépendant doit être mis en place pour évaluer la fiabilité, la sécurité et l’impact social des produits d’IA. Des audits réguliers, menés par des instances publiques ou communautaires, permettront de garantir que les systèmes respectent les normes établies.
4) Règlementation sur la propriété intellectuelle et la souveraineté technologique
Le Canada doit protéger les innovations locales en IA par des lois claires sur la propriété intellectuelle, tout en favorisant l’hébergement des données et des infrastructures sur le territoire national. Cela renforcera la souveraineté économique et technologique du pays.
5) Responsabilité partagée
Les entreprises, les gouvernements & les institutions de recherche doivent collaborer pour établir des règles de responsabilité en cas de préjudice causé par un système d’IA.
column4: Le Canada peut mobiliser citoyens et entreprises en misant sur la transparence, l’éducation et la participation active. Cela implique d’organiser des consultations inclusives, de soutenir des formations accessibles sur les enjeux éthiques de l’IA, et de promouvoir des pratiques exemplaires dans les milieux de travail. Les organismes communautaires, comme le CIAFT, sont particulièrement bien placés pour diriger ces efforts, grâce à leur expertise en mobilisation, en formation et en défense des droits. En les plaçant au cœur de la gouvernance de l’IA, le Canada peut bâtir une confiance durable et une adoption responsable.

column2:
Building public trust in AI technologies requires a proactive, transparent, and inclusive approach. Canada should establish clear, enforceable guidelines for the safe and ethical use of AI, including robust standards for data security, privacy, and algorithmic accountability. These guidelines must be co-developed with input from industry, academia, civil society, and affected communities to ensure they reflect diverse perspectives and values.

An independent academic advisory committee should be established to monitor emerging risks and provide evidence-based recommendations to government. This committee would serve as a conduit for ongoing dialogue and policy adaptation, helping Canada stay ahead of technological developments and associated risks.

Canada should also develop mechanisms to address specific concerns such as age-appropriate use of AI, intellectual property protection, and energy prioritization for high-impact applications. 

Guidance on navigating international regulations will support Canadian companies in exporting AI technologies responsibly.
Investing in sovereign digital infrastructure is essential to reduce reliance on foreign-controlled systems and mitigate geopolitical vulnerabilities. 

Mission-driven initiatives focused on quantum-resilient cryptography and cybersecurity preparedness will further strengthen Canada’s resilience.

Together, these actions will foster public confidence, promote responsible innovation, and position Canada as a global leader in trustworthy AI.
column3:
Canada must implement comprehensive frameworks that ensure AI products are secure, ethical, and inclusive. These should include:
•	Standards for the protection of personal information and cybersecurity.
•	Requirements for environmental, health, and community impact assessments.
•	Equity and accessibility guidelines to prevent systemic bias and exclusion.
•	Backup protocols and reliability standards to ensure continuity of service.
•	Mandatory use of Algorithmic Impact Assessment tools for high-risk applications.
These frameworks must be adaptable to evolving technologies and grounded in human rights principles. Their implementation will promote transparency, accountability, and public trust in AI systems deployed across sectors.
column4:
Canada can promote responsible AI use and trust in governance by enabling inclusive engagement and clear accountability. 
Government should lead the development of policy, legislation, and oversight mechanisms that ensure ethical data use and AI deployment.

Academia should lead research on emerging risks, societal impacts, and mitigation strategies, providing evidence-based insights to inform policy. 

Industry must adopt best practices and collaborate with regulators to ensure compliance and innovation.

Citizens must be empowered through education and transparency. Public awareness campaigns should explain how AI systems work, how data is used, and what rights individuals have. 

Community organizations and public institutions can serve as trusted intermediaries.

Government should also lead emergency preparedness efforts for critical infrastructure and establish safeguards for high-risk sectors. 
These coordinated efforts will build a culture of trust, responsibility, and shared stewardship in Canada’s AI ecosystem.

column2:
Public trust isn’t earned by explanations alone; it’s earned when people can see and use their rights - consent, contestation, and redress - and when violators face consequences. Canada should center enforceable, risk-tiered rules and transparent oversight so confidence flows from accountability, not optimism.

Make enforceable rules the foundation of trust: Adopt proportionate, risk-tiered obligations; center lawful basis/consent for sensitive data; require clear notice, access, explanation, and effective redress routes.

Stand up public AI registries for high-impact uses: Require fit-for-purpose documentation (system purpose, data provenance, evaluation results, known limitations, contact for complaints) with exemptions narrowly defined and reviewable.

Require independent assurance: For higher-risk systems, mandate third-party audits/evaluations, post-deployment monitoring, incident reporting, and periodic public summaries.

Procure and fund only what’s auditable: Condition public money and vendor eligibility on documented data provenance, algorithmic impact assessments, monitoring logs, portability and deletion pathways, and timely disclosure of material incidents.

Align with credible global safety frameworks—plus Canadian rights: Map obligations to recognized assurance frameworks while embedding Canadian privacy, human-rights, and Indigenous-led data governance where relevant.

Cut anxiety, then teach: Pair rights and remedies with clear public guidance on how to exercise them; reserve “AI literacy” programs to show people how to use access, appeal, and deletion—not to substitute for them.
column3:
Standards and norms matter only when they translate into duties people can enforce. A Canadian framework should put Charter and human-rights protection first, pair risk-tiered obligations with real penalties and redress, and require public registries and independent audits for high-impact systems.

Anchor the framework in rights, not commerce: Bill C-27 was critiqued for treating privacy/AI chiefly as market issues. Any AI law should center Charter and human-rights protection and the public interest, with commensurate oversight and penalties.

Draw bright-line bans where risk is unacceptable. Prohibit AI uses that pose irremediable or disproportionate risks to fundamental rights (e.g., certain biometric mass surveillance, social scoring, manipulative targeting of protected groups), with narrowly tailored exceptions subject to independent approval.

Enforce existing law—and add meaningful redress. Resource regulators to enforce privacy, copyright, and consent obligations (including data provenance for training). Create effective remedies: access, explanation, rectification, deletion/portability, compensation, and—where appropriate—a private right of action.

Make high-impact AI visible and auditable. Require public registries for high-risk deployments; mandate fit-for-purpose transparency (purpose, data sources/provenance, evaluations/limits, contacts). For higher-risk systems, require independent audits/evaluations, post-deployment monitoring, and incident reporting with public summaries.

Use standards as tools, not substitutes. Reference credible international standards for risk management, assurance, and security, but bind them into statutory obligations (risk-tiered duties, audit rights, documentation, and lifecycle controls), including Indigenous-led data governance where relevant.
column4:
Public trust and market certainty are preconditions to durable AI growth. Set hard legal boundaries, empower an independent regulator, and treat data governance as shared power—not just notice—so businesses can scale responsibly inside clearly defined guardrails.

Government’s role: legislate and enforce. Pass specific, rights-based AI and privacy rules and resource enforcement of existing copyright, privacy, and consent laws—so obligations are clear and violations have consequences.

Independent regulator’s role: oversight with teeth. Create a new, fully independent, well-resourced Officer of Parliament to supervise high-impact AI, issue guidance, conduct audits, and impose remedies and penalties.

Civil society & public’s role: shared governance, not FYI. Use inclusive engagement and treat data governance as shared authority. Co-design Indigenous data sovereignty programs aligned with OCAP®/CARE and FPIC, with community control and benefit-sharing.

Business & industry’s role: innovate within guardrails. Build and deploy AI inside the rights-based legal boundaries set by Parliament and enforced by the regulator; adopt auditable practices (provenance, impact assessments, monitoring, incident disclosure) as standard operating procedure.

column2:
Canada can build public trust in AI by actively adopting and implementing ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 42 international standards for artificial intelligence. SC 42 develops globally harmonized standards addressing AI safety, transparency, bias mitigation, risk management, and governance—providing proven frameworks that demonstrate responsible AI practices.
National adoption of SC 42 standards ensures Canadian AI systems meet internationally recognized benchmarks, enabling independent verification and fostering confidence among citizens and businesses. This harmonization facilitates cross-border interoperability while reducing regulatory uncertainty.
Canada should prioritize leveraging SC 42 standards, through the Standards Council of Canada, into certification programs and conformity assessment schemes, making compliance verifiable and meaningful. Equally important is developing accessible educational resources that explain how these international standards protect Canadians—demystifying AI safeguards in plain language.
By championing SC 42 adoption nationally and contributing to their ongoing development internationally, Canada positions itself as a trustworthy AI leader while empowering citizens with standards-backed assurance.

Canada can build trust in AI by ensuring transparency, accountability, and a structured management system approach in AI deployment. Canadian adoptions of international standards from ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 42 provide concrete guidance in these areas. For example:

CSA ISO/IEC 42001 – AI Management Systems helps organizations implement structured governance, risk management, and oversight practices.
CSA ISO/IEC 23894 – AI Risk Management provides frameworks to identify, assess, and mitigate AI-related risks.
CSA ISO/IEC 5259 series – Data Quality supports reliable and reproducible AI outcomes, which is critical for public confidence.
CSA ISO/IEC 42006 - Requirements for bodies providing audit and certification of artificial intelligence management systems
column3:
To ensure AI products are responsible and trustworthy, Canada should promote the adoption of standards-based governance and oversight mechanisms based on the aforementioned ISO/IEC JTC1/SC42 Artificial Intelligence standards which have been adopted as National Standards of Canada.  

Management system standards, in particular, give organizations a structured framework to embed responsible practices throughout a product lifecycle. They guide companies in establishing policies, monitoring outcomes, and continually improving processes, helping to reduce risks while supporting innovation and adoption at scale.

CSA ISO/IEC 42001 specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an Artificial Intelligence Management System (AIMS) within organizations.  As the world’s first AI management system standard, CSA ISO/IEC 42001 provides guidance for navigating the rapidly evolving AI landscape. It addresses unique challenges such as ethical considerations, transparency, and continuous learning, giving organizations a structured way to manage risks and opportunities while balancing innovation with effective governance.
column4:
Canada can build AI trust by establishing a national network of locally-embedded AI advisory councils that directly feed into the Standards Council of Canada's SC42 mirror committee. These regional councils would bring together citizens, SMEs, large corporations, academics, and community leaders to discuss AI impacts in their specific contexts—from agricultural automation in Saskatchewan to healthcare AI in Nova Scotia.
The Standards Council of Canada is uniquely positioned to lead this infrastructure, given its mandate to coordinate Canadian participation in international standardization. By funding focus groups within each council to examine the needs and expectations of Canadian society and business ecosystems, Canada ensures diverse voices shape international AI governance frameworks. These focus groups would enable Canadian experts at the international level to gather feedback on practical implications and strengthen the development of robust, relevant standards.
The Government of Canada should fund this initiative, providing resources for facilitator training, translation services, and digital participation platforms. The Standards Council would coordinate methodology and synthesize inputs for SC42 mirror committee representatives, ensuring Canadian positions are strengthened by broad stakeholder engagement. This creates accountability loops where standards aren't imposed top-down but built bottom-up, transforming international AI governance into a genuinely inclusive Canadian endeavor that balances technical innovation with stakeholder engagement.

column2:
Well, it sure is time for regulation even though the AI industry implies this will stifle innovation. The public already mistrusts the government and if the government continues to refuse to bring in some guardrails, the public will continue to go further down the negative rabbit hole. If you want to establish confidence you have to clean up the dramatic field day tech is having over privacy and the algorithmic control of information offered by social media. This doesn’t mean censorship per se but surely can insist chat bots romancing children or default data grabs set to “on” and not “off” are not unreasonable. As well, establishing a social contract protocol that requires an answer to “why are you developing “X” project?” “What is the benefit to Canadians?” “How will you know it is successful?” “What will you do if it turns out rogue to your intentions?”

Literacy strategies are not so complex but have to be implemented in the workplace and academia. So far money has gone to science tech training but is virtually non existent in basic literacy. Not everyone is going to make AI but everyone is impacted by it. We need to achieve a basic level of literacy where Canadians can discuss expectations and reservations with enough intelligence to let the Elon Musks of this world realize they have agency in where all this is going. Right now tech bafflegab and hope tends to prevent citizens from articulating fears and hopes altogether. 

Media literacy in the curriculum took well over a decade to be entrenched. AI literacy can’t take that long. This has to be taken out of science and put into all aspects of schooling eg Social studies, English, History and more.
column3:
Well right now Canada is somewhat barren of the impact products like we get from Meta et al. If, however, we become focused on life changing problem solving tech, then the trust will be in abundance. We can’t compete with the trite AI but could really rock the universe if we are all in on human improvement. We won’t need likes, shares and subscribers if we can crack illness. The “thanks” will come in volumes.

Ethics and what not simply have to mirror our values: no harm, transparency, empirical evidence of “it works” and so on. Citizens have been played a lot by tech promises so, yeah, we’d have to get to the moon on whatever projects may be funded/supported by government. Anything less puts the deal in peril akin to the Metrolinx ongoing build of an LRT in Toronto. How many years now? Just damages the outcome of it if it never comes.

Oversight has to be legitimate and call out problems as they develop and not be left to fester. This may be a challenge if oversight is too closely linked to the developing process. Really an independent and robust entity has to be established that isn’t beholden to the other side for any of it to have credibility.
column4:
The government needs to separate itself from delivery of AI literacy efforts altogether and enable libraries, schools and the workplace to deliver the efforts. Rationale here is the issue of declining trust toward government. As well, tech companies have too much invested in their industry to provide an objective presentation. There are hundreds of “projects” and individuals who provide education and focus group like work that are well placed to be the facilitators of improving the relationship between citizens and government. There must be a separation between “church and state” to have any chance of success. Reporting needs to be transparent and unedited. Those involved in supporting a “trust endeavour” to bring AI potential, safeguarding and all of this into a level of accountability and understanding that citizens can accept have to be seen as independent from industry and government.

That's a very insightful perspective, positioning accelerated learning and skill augmentation as the primary mechanism for building public trust and confidence in AI.

Here is the formulated text addressing how Canada can build public trust in AI by focusing on augmentation and learning:

🤝 Building Public Trust and Confidence in AI Technologies
The most important strategy for building public trust and confidence in AI technologies and addressing their associated risks is to empower the public through augmented skill development and the acceleration of learning.

1. Focus on Augmentation and Accelerated Learning
Public trust will be built not through abstract ethical statements, but through a personal, positive, and demonstrable experience of AI's utility.

Skill Augmentation: The primary focus must be on promoting AI as a tool for the augmentation of existing human skills. This demystifies the technology and immediately connects it to professional and personal growth.

Acceleration of Learning: Highlight and support the fact that AI enables individuals to learn at a rate that has never been previously present in human history. This transforms AI from a threat into an indispensable educational partner.

Empowering the User: The confidence is built by ensuring users understand how to prompt and command the AI effectively to support their specific interests and lead them down a path of optimal development.

2. Connecting Confidence to Capability
By emphasizing capability, the public naturally begins to trust the technology.

Active Engagement: Government and industry programs must move beyond passive awareness campaigns to actively promoting hands-on engagement with AI tools for learning and skill development.

Risk Mitigation through Literacy: The best way to address the risks AI presents is to increase AI literacy across the population. A well-informed public that understands how AI works, its capabilities, and its limitations is inherently better equipped.
column3:
That is a very principles-driven response, focusing on the concepts of truthfulness, moral goodness, and material cooperation as the foundation for responsible AI deployment.

Here is the formulated text detailing the framework, standards, regulations, and norms needed to ensure AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed:

✨ Frameworks for Trustworthy and Responsibly Deployed AI
To ensure that AI products in Canada are trustworthy and responsibly deployed, the required frameworks, standards, regulations, and norms must be fundamentally aligned with the principles of truth, objectivity, moral goodness, and the fostering of human cooperation.

1. Core Norms: Truthfulness and Moral Goodness
The most important norm is ensuring that AI products work reliably and contribute to the common good.

Truthfulness and Objectivity: The AI framework must mandate systems that are demonstrably truthful and objective. While being values-based, the standards must be designed to avoid being overly biased toward any single set of particular beliefs or values, ensuring broad societal acceptance and fairness.

Moral Goodness and Non-Harm: Regulations must explicitly prohibit AI use cases that enable harm. For example, the knowledge transfer of how to build a bomb is an example of a non-beneficial use case that must be restricted, as it does not contribute to the augmentation of human cooperation.

2. Frameworks for Fostering Cooperation
AI should be viewed and regulated as a means of fostering material cooperation across society.

Cooperation Mandate: Standards should be developed to encourage and measure AI's ability to facilitate collaboration. The existing material cooperation among major AI partnership companies (e.g., in the United States) to further growth serves as a powerful model for the future of domestic and international AI development.

3. Regulatory Requirements for Responsible Deployment
The resulting regulations must translate these norms for good.

Here is the formulated text detailing how Canada can proactively engage citizens and businesses to promote responsible AI use and the best entities to lead these efforts:

🎨 Proactive Engagement and Trust Building in Responsible AI
To proactively engage citizens and businesses and promote responsible AI use and trust in its governance, Canada must employ public-facing strategies centered on accessible, culturally relevant, and compelling digital media.

1. Public-Facing Strategy: Digital Content for Engagement
The primary engagement strategy should focus on creative digital content that breaks down complex AI concepts and aligns them with Canadian values.

Canadian Content Creation: Invest significantly in the creation of direct, sophisticated Canadian content for digital platforms. This addresses the current absence of domestically produced educational material.

Tailored Digital Media: Utilize various forms of digital creativity, including:

Cartoons and Gamification for younger children and primary education.

Digital Art and Interactive Media for general public awareness.

Sophisticated Documentary and Explainer Videos for older citizens and businesses.

Communication Goals: The content must serve as a means of communicating vision, transferring knowledge, transferring skill, and enabling flourishing by demonstrating AI's benefits in a trusted, recognizable context.

2. Leadership and Collaborative Efforts to Fuel Trust
Trust is best fueled by entities that possess both technical expertise and a connection to the public. The approach must be inclusive, balancing transparency with the need to protect innovation.

Best Placed to Lead:

Academia and Research Institutes: Best placed to lead efforts that fuel transparency and explainability by translating complex AI systems into easily digestible public-facing explanations.

Utterly serious:
Establish law with regard to the operating systems on phones (and computers, etc).

If, at the outset, Canadian law required all devices that can access, use, collect, store, and sell our data to have a universal, simple opt-out as the first step of set-up, it would accomplish several things:

column2: Pour renforcer la confiance du public envers les technologies d’IA, le Canada doit adopter un cadre législatif équilibré et transversal qui aborde clairement les enjeux et risques majeurs, notamment la protection de la vie privée, la sécurité nationale et la prévention des usages malveillants ou préjudiciables, en particulier envers les personnes vulnérables comme les enfants. Ce leadership fédéral est essentiel pour éviter la fragmentation des règles entre provinces ou secteurs, qui compliquerait la conformité des entreprises et risquerait de créer des inégalités. En mettant en place une réglementation cohérente et adaptée, le Canada pourra instaurer un climat de confiance, favoriser l’adoption responsable de l’IA et soutenir l’innovation et la compétitivité à l’échelle nationale.
column3: Pour garantir que les produits d’IA canadiens soient fiables et déployés de manière responsable, le Canada doit encourager l’adoption de cadres de gestion reconnus internationalement, tels que l’ISO/IEC 42001 et le NIST AI RMF, qui exigent la mise en place de pratiques de gouvernance rigoureuses au sein des organisations. Il est conseillé que le gouvernement fédéral préconise l’intégration de ces standards dans la conception et le déploiement des solutions d’IA, en complément d’un encadrement législatif national. Ce cadre devrait notamment couvrir la protection de la vie privée, la sécurité nationale, la prévention des usages malveillants comme l’hypertrucage, ainsi que les risques pouvant porter atteinte à l’intégrité des individus, en particulier celle des enfants.

Le gouvernement fédéral, en tant que régulateur, est le mieux placé pour diriger ces efforts, établir un cadre national cohérent et éviter la fragmentation provinciale. Les entreprises, en tant qu’acteurs de terrain, doivent intégrer la gouvernance de l’IA dans leurs pratiques et former leurs employés à une utilisation responsable, contribuant ainsi à bâtir un climat de confiance durable.

The launch of ChatGPT fundamentally democratized access to AI because for the first time non-experts could use AI tools using natural language. As a result, AI now has the potential to empower not just those who are technically savvy, but all Canadians. 
Through widespread literacy and education, AI can become a comprehensive and inclusive engine of growth. Unfortunately, AI literacy remains Canada’s most neglected foundational element. 

Canada must engage in a mass AI literacy campaign that builds awareness, trust, and excitement. Public awareness campaigns emphasizing practical AI literacy and an openness to technological change can shift societal perspectives from caution to optimism, building a culture that is open to change. A comprehensive national AI skills and training initiative, spanning all educational levels and corporate sectors, can foster an AI-ready culture and workforce. Everyone, from high school students to executives, must better understand AI’s challenges and opportunities. 

Moreover, investing in our people will not only increase our chances of increasing business productivity and creating world-beating firms, it will also ensure that the benefits are more broadly shared in society.

column2:
The Government of Canada needs to be honest.  And the Government of Canada needs to be prepared.

The government also needs to be ready, willing and able to gather critics together to discuss their concerns.

Plus, there need to be appropriate whistleblower protections.  And the government may ultimately need to give itself, or at least a regulator -- presumably one operating at arm's length -- the ability to fine, suspend, amend or even shut down companies engaging in projects that are deemed to be too dangerous.

Tolerable risk thresholds need to be identified.  The onus needs to be any research institutions and/or companies that are working in AGI, for them to display beyond a reasonable doubt that what they are doing is not going to result in dangerous outcomes.

Furthermore, 'building confidence' is simply not an objective that should be pursued if it is one based on non-disclosure of material information and/or mis/disinformation.

With nuclear power, there was the head of the nuclear regulator, Linda Keen.  She argued that a Chalk River facility needed retooling to be prepared for an unlikely, but possible, earthquake in the Greater Ottawa area.  She wanted a second back-up power system installed.  And she wanted it installed by a given date.  The operator of the plant couldn't seem to figure out how to successfully install a second back-up system.

The risks of something going really bad were, in Ms. Keen's view, unacceptably high.  So she ordered the facility shut down.  That angered the politicians of the time.  Ms. Keen felt that the risks of something really bad occurring would be 1 in 1000.  She wanted a risk lowered to 1 in a million.

The government overruled her.  And then the PM fired her.

Hence, there is the question:  Even if Canada gets top-notch protection on paper now, once AI systems are in place, what is to stop a future government from trashing the regulations and pursuing a reckless agenda?  What guarantees would the public have?
column3:
Comprehensive regulations are needed.  Plus, regulations on companies cannot be bound by the 'One-for-One' Rule.

Plus, there may be a case for serious liability, including upon individuals, particularly if non-disclosure to regulators of risks was involved.

Mechanisms for whistleblowers to reach out need to be established and whistleblowers need to be protected.  Canada seems to have a pretty bad record on this front.

Jan 29, 2022
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p7svjeB7c4o

AI might presently be described as an area of technology that might be described as a sort of 'Wild West.'  A lack of whistleblower protections may mean that an international company wishing to pursue risky projects might opt to conduct some risky projects in Canada -- with potentially disturbing results.

Consulting with other countries, particularly Western European countries, would be key.

That being noted, an AI regulator needs to be established.  It is hard for Canadian regulatory staff to meet with foreign regulatory staff to compare ideas for frameworks when a Canadian regulatory body for AI doesn't seem to exist as of yet.

I would recommend that an AI regulator not be associated with the Ministry of Industry.  With around 39 members of the current cabinet (including secretaries of state), but with the potential risks of AI, there may be a case to be made for there to be a Minister of AI Regulation.  This might be akin to how there is a Minister of Natural Resources along with a Minister of the Environment and Climate Change.
column4:
I would feel better about AI if a parliamentary committee were able to invite in Audrey Tang to testify, in a public committee meeting, and if she were able to present her thoughts, alongside a Canadian expert or two.

If she were able to answer questions about some of the more alarming potential risks associated with AI, I think that that would be helpful.  As she would be someone outside of the Government of Canada, and as she has been one to embrace radical transparency, she might feel more free to answer questions freely than some top-level Canadian civil servants who might worry about somehow inadvertently spilling some classified information.

I would also be interested in having a parliamentary committee speak to Tristan Harris.  Given that he is an American and a former Google employee, I think that a meeting involving him might also get noticed by some people south of the border, and there is some rationale to be concerned about an unchecked and unregulated pursuit of AI by our southern neighbour.

For me, there would still be the onus on those pursuing AI in Canada to go beyond the claim that at least we, in Canada, are going to regulate more than the U.S. does.  If there were a mad cow epidemic in the U.S. that was killing thousands of people each year through prion-infected beef, I don't think that it would cut it if we were to suggest that we would limit our mad cow situation to several dozen people each year.  Similarly, I think that there is a case to be made for pursuing prudent regulation of AI, despite whatever competitive challenges that may mean for Canadian companies in comparison to their American counterparts.

If things go seriously awry with one AI company in the U.S., that could raise doubts about American companies, generally.  I would hope that such a perception would not fall upon Canada and unchecked / unregulated Canadian AI companies.

column2:
Public trust grows when Canadians can see that AI serves people first and is governed transparently. Canada should lead with clear, enforceable standards for safety, privacy, and accountability: rules written in plain language and applied consistently across sectors. Establish a National AI Safety and Transparency Office to certify systems and publish audit results so citizens and businesses can see how AI is evaluated. Pair regulation with education and literacy programs that help workers, students, and policymakers understand what AI can and cannot do, and how it is governed.

AI is an opportunity to strengthen public trust in institutions, provided it is deployed responsibly—research shows that trust in AI and trust in democratic institutions rise together. By demonstrating that responsible innovation can coexist with strong governance, Canada can lead globally in building a trustworthy digital society.
column3: AI technology has advanced to the point that it can and must be governed through legislation and existing democratic processes. Arguments that AI is “too complex” to regulate are misplaced: the technology is now auditable, testable, and measurable enough for lawmakers to set enforceable standards. Canada should legislate AI directly, using its established legal system and obligations under international law, privacy, human rights, and intellectual property frameworks as anchors. Governance should be a collaboration between government, universities, and private firms to apply state-of-the-art oversight tools. In short, AI is ready for the rule of law and Canada is ready to lead.
column4:
Real trust in AI comes from a system where every group with power or exposure has a role and a stake. Canada should lead a national trust compact linking government, industry, Indigenous nations, workers, universities, and marginalized communities.
* Government legislates and enforces clear, democratic rules for safety, privacy, and accountability consistent with international law and human rights frameworks.
* Businesses earn trust through transparent practices, independent audits, and fair labour transitions for workers affected by automation. Responsible commercialization should create prosperity while upholding equity: trust as competitive advantage.
* Indigenous governments exercise data sovereignty and co-develop governance aligned with OCAP® principles, ensuring Indigenous data, AI models, and benefits remain under Indigenous control.
* Marginalized and equity-seeking communities including persons with disabilities, racialized Canadians, and low-income workers must be funded and empowered to participate directly in AI design, oversight, and literacy programs.
* Universities and public institutions host open testing and education hubs where citizens can see how AI works and challenge it.
* Civil society and community groups convene citizen panels and local trust councils in multiple languages and accessible formats.

Canada’s competitive edge will come from prosperity built on legitimacy: an ecosystem where capital, inclusion, and law reinforce one another so that every Canadian, especially those most affected, can see themselves in the governance of AI.
AI Impact Alliance: www.allianceimpact.org

column2: Supporting established global certification such as ISO 42001 - management system for AI standards instead of coming up with more AI guidelines and principles.
column3: ISO 42001 is a great foundation for AI governance, risk and compliance management. There is a misconception that ISO 42001 is only helping to improve the organizational posture. In fact, it provides a framework to identify, measure, mitigate and monitor AI systems at the societal, organization, system, model, and data levels.

column2: Start with plain rules people can see and understand: clear standards for safe use, simple consent and data practices, and specific bans or guardrails for high-risk uses. Make independent testing and public reporting the norm—common evaluations, red-team results, incident reports, and easy ways to file complaints and get fixes. Use the public sector as a model user: run small pilots in schools, healthcare, and services, publish what works and what fails, and only scale tools that meet safety and equity benchmarks (including Indigenous data governance). Require transparency labels so people know when AI is in use and what it’s doing. Invest in AI literacy for the public and frontline workers so trust grows with understanding; that is where I can contribute directly through training and pilot design. Finally, tie incentives to responsible behavior—procurement preference, tax credits, or certifications for products that meet strong safety, privacy, accessibility, and bias-testing standards.
column3: We need a clear, usable rule set that builders and buyers can follow: privacy-by-design and security-by-design as defaults; data governance that respects consent, purpose limits, and Indigenous data sovereignty; transparency labels so people know when AI is used and what it does; model cards + system cards that document training data, limits, and risks; independent safety testing (bias, robustness, accessibility) with public incident reporting; and Algorithmic Impact Assessments for higher-risk uses in the public and regulated sectors. Tie this to procurement: governments and schools should only buy tools that meet these standards and pass a third-party audit. Add regulatory sandboxes with exportable compliance certificates so Canadian products can scale globally. In short: clear standards, real testing, plain-language transparency, strong data rights—and procurement that rewards doing it right.
column4: Canada can build trust in AI by including citizens, educators, and businesses early and often in how AI is used, taught, and regulated. Public trust grows when people understand AI and feel their voices matter. Governments should lead national AI literacy and awareness campaigns through schools, community programs, and public media—something I already do in my own work with educators and professionals. Businesses should commit to transparent communication about how their AI systems work and how they protect privacy. Universities and non-profits can host open dialogues and training, while government ensures independent oversight and public reporting so people see accountability in action. In short, trust comes from education, openness, and shared responsibility—where government sets the rules, researchers provide evidence, businesses follow clear standards, and educators help every Canadian understand what AI means for them.

column2:
Trust comes from visible safeguards, clear benefits, and meaningful control.

1) Transparency by default: disclose when AI is used; provide plain‑language explanations; publish model and data summaries for high‑impact uses.

2) Independent assurance: third‑party audits and certifications; public registers of government AI systems; incident reporting with timelines and remedies.

3) Human choice & redress: human‑in‑the‑loop for consequential decisions; appeal/override channels; accessible complaint portals.

4) Literacy at scale: national "AI Basics" program (English/French/Indigenous languages), workplace micro‑credentials, community workshops via libraries and colleges, and deepfake awareness.

5) Privacy & security: privacy‑preserving techniques, strict access controls, and published DPIAs for public‑sector deployments.

6) Demonstrate value: showcase safe, beneficial deployments with independent evaluations and equity audits.
column3:
Build a layered regime: risk‑based law, technical standards, certification, and sector guidance.

1) Legislation: proportionate, risk‑tiered duties for data governance, testing, transparency, post‑market monitoring, and penalties for egregious harms.

2) Standards: align with international best practice for robustness, security, and transparency; publish Canadian profiles for health, finance, transport, and public safety.

3) Certification & audit: authorize accredited conformity assessment bodies; require third‑party audits for high‑impact systems; encourage voluntary certification for medium‑risk systems with incentives.

4) Sector rules: align with sector regulators and provincial privacy commissioners; harmonize interpretation to minimize friction.

5) Accountability: algorithm registers, impact assessments, redress mechanisms, and periodic public reporting of incidents and mitigations.
column4:
Engagement must be ongoing, inclusive, and actionable.

1) Channels: standing public forums, citizen juries on hard questions, and an AI engagement portal for consultations, explainers, and incident reports.

2) Inclusive delivery: fund civil‑society partners (libraries, Indigenous organizations, newcomers’ groups) to run local workshops; provide multilingual, accessible materials.

3) Shared leadership: government sets rules and models transparency; industry implements responsible AI programs and participates in audits; academia provides independent testing and literacy content; civil society bridges to communities; media supports fact‑checking on AI misinformation.

4) Feedback → policy: publish "you said, we did" summaries linking input to regulatory updates and standards.

column3:
I am skeptical that any such framework is possible, in the context of all the other questions on this survey. The absolute _glut_ of funding and incentives being poured on any AI anything necessarily means that people will try to incorporate AI technology into _everything_ to access those funds and benefit from those incentives, regardless of whether the technology is a net positive for a given application or, worse, whether it poses significant danger.

It must not be _profitable_ to release AI products that cause harm to people, or even products that merely don't provide meaningful value.
